title: Privacy Preserving Multi Agent Path Finding
comment: 16 pages, 5 figures, to be published in AAMAS 2026 as an extended abstract
summary: In the multi-agent path finding (MAPF) problem, a group of agents search in a graph for a path for each agent where no two paths collide. While in all applications of MAPF the agents must not collide with each other, in some of them the agents may not wish to share their paths due to privacy constraints. In this work, we formulate two types of privacy constraints for MAPF and propose algorithms that preserve them. The first type of privacy we consider is planning-level privacy, which means that during planning, the agents cannot identify exactly the planned location of the other agents. We propose a general framework for obtaining planning-level privacy, which works by adding mock agents to the planning process. The second type of privacy we consider is execution-level privacy, which is relevant when agents have limited sensing capabilities. Execution-level privacy is preserved if none of the agents is allowed to sense the location of the other agents during execution. We show how to adapt two popular MAPF algorithms, namely PIBT and LaCAM, such that they preserve execution-level privacy. Lastly, we propose a post-processing technique that allows the agents to reduce the sum of costs of the returned solution without losing any privacy. We also implemented our algorithms and evaluated them empirically, showing that the proposed post-processing technique indeed improved cost significantly.

AI.tldr: This paper addresses privacy constraints in multi-agent path finding by proposing algorithms for two privacy types: planning-level privacy using mock agents, and execution-level privacy by adapting existing MAPF algorithms.
AI.motivation: In MAPF applications, agents may need privacy constraints beyond collision avoidance - they may not want to share their planned paths due to privacy concerns, especially in scenarios where agents have limited sensing capabilities.
AI.method: 1. For planning-level privacy: A framework using mock agents during planning to obscure actual agent locations. 2. For execution-level privacy: Adaptations of existing MAPF algorithms (PIBT and LaCAM) to preserve privacy during execution. 3. A post-processing technique to optimize solution costs while maintaining privacy.
AI.result: The proposed algorithms successfully preserve both types of privacy. The post-processing technique significantly improved solution costs without compromising privacy, as demonstrated through empirical evaluation.
AI.conclusion: This work successfully addresses privacy constraints in MAPF by formulating two privacy types and developing corresponding algorithms, expanding MAPF's applicability to privacy-sensitive scenarios.

title: IFPV: An Integrated Multi-Agent Framework for Generative Operational Planning and High-Fidelity Plan Verification
summary: Operational plan generation and verification are critical for modern complex and rapidly changing battlefield environments, yet traditional generation and verification methods still respectively face the challenges of generation infeasibility and verification insufficiency. To alleviate these limitations, we propose an Integrated Multi-Agent Framework for Generative Operational Planning and High-Fidelity Plan Verification (IFPV). IFPV consists of two tightly coupled modules: Multi-Perspective Hierarchical Agents (MPHA) for generative operational planning and an Adversarial Cognitive Simulation Engine (ACSE) for high-fidelity adversarial plan verification. MPHA decomposes commander intent into executable multi-platform tactical action sequences through the collaboration of Pathfinder, Analyst, and Planner agents. ACSE introduces an opponent equipped with a customized world model, which predicts the future evolution of mission-critical platforms and conducts dynamic counteractions against candidate plans. Simulation experiments in the Asymmetric Combat Tactic Simulator (ACTS) show that IFPV improves mission success by 19.4% and reduces operational cost by 41.7% compared with a single-step large language model (LLM) planning baseline. Compared with a traditional rule-based validator, ACSE increases the average suppression rate by 31.8%, indicating that the proposed verification environment is stricter and more discriminative in revealing the latent vulnerabilities of candidate plans. The code for IFPV can be found at https://github.com/zhigao3ks/IFPV.

AI.tldr: An integrated multi-agent framework (IFPV) that combines generative operational planning with high-fidelity adversarial verification for military battlefield operations, improving mission success by 19.4% and reducing costs by 41.7%.
AI.motivation: Traditional operational planning and verification methods face challenges: planning suffers from generation infeasibility issues, while verification suffers from insufficiency. Current approaches don't effectively integrate generative planning with rigorous adversarial verification in complex, rapidly changing battlefield environments.
AI.method: IFPV consists of two tightly coupled modules: 1) Multi-Perspective Hierarchical Agents (MPHA) for generative operational planning - uses Pathfinder, Analyst, and Planner agents to decompose commander intent into executable multi-platform tactical action sequences; 2) Adversarial Cognitive Simulation Engine (ACSE) for high-fidelity adversarial plan verification - introduces an opponent equipped with customized world model to predict future mission evolution and conduct dynamic counteractions against candidate plans.
AI.result: Simulation experiments in Asymmetric Combat Tactic Simulator (ACTS) show: 1) IFPV improves mission success by 19.4% and reduces operational cost by 41.7% compared to single-step LLM planning baseline; 2) ACSE increases average suppression rate by 31.8% compared to traditional rule-based validator, indicating stricter and more discriminative verification environment that reveals latent vulnerabilities in candidate plans.
AI.conclusion: The proposed IFPV framework effectively integrates generative operational planning with adversarial verification, addressing limitations of traditional methods. The tight coupling between MPHA and ACSE enables more robust and cost-effective operational plans by exposing vulnerabilities through adversarial simulation, making it suitable for complex, rapidly evolving battlefield environments.

title: Decision-Level Fusion for Robust Wearable Affect Recognition
summary: Automatic recognition of affective state from wearable physiology has clear societal impact for public health, preventive care, and stress-aware interventions, but real deployments require robustness to non-stationary dynamics, artefacts, and missing sensors. We study this problem on WESAD, using baseline, stress, and amusement conditions, where common fixed-basis spectral features such as FFT bandpower and Welch PSD can oversmooth short-lived discriminative patterns. We propose a non-stationary pipeline that combines Fourier-Bessel Series Expansion (FBSE) with EWT data-driven spectral segmentation to extract mode-wise transient descriptors. For multimodal integration, we adopt decision-level aggregation over per-modality predictors and weight each modality by predictive uncertainty and modality reliability. Results on WESAD, using 15 subjects and ECG, EDA, BVP, EMG, and ACC signals across three classes, indicate that decision-level aggregation is approximately 84 percent of the time at least as good as feature-level aggregation, and approximately 48 percent of the time strictly better, suggesting improved robustness under heterogeneous and partially reliable sensing.

AI.tldr: A non-stationary pipeline using Fourier-Bessel Series Expansion with Empirical Wavelet Transform for wearable physiology-based affective state recognition, combined with decision-level aggregation weighted by predictive uncertainty, improves robustness for real-world deployments.
AI.motivation: Wearable physiology-based affective state recognition has significant societal impact for public health and preventive care, but real-world deployments face challenges with non-stationary dynamics, artefacts, and missing sensors. Traditional fixed-basis spectral features like FFT bandpower oversmooth short-lived discriminative patterns crucial for accurate state recognition.
AI.method: Proposed a non-stationary pipeline combining Fourier-Bessel Series Expansion (FBSE) with Empirical Wavelet Transform (EWT) for data-driven spectral segmentation to extract mode-wise transient descriptors. For multimodal integration, used decision-level aggregation over per-modality predictors, weighting each modality by predictive uncertainty and modality reliability.
AI.result: On the WESAD dataset (15 subjects, ECG, EDA, BVP, EMG, ACC signals across baseline, stress, and amusement conditions), decision-level aggregation was approximately 84% of the time at least as good as feature-level aggregation, and approximately 48% of the time strictly better, indicating improved robustness under heterogeneous and partially reliable sensing.
AI.conclusion: The proposed non-stationary pipeline with decision-level aggregation provides more robust affective state recognition from wearable physiology, addressing real-world challenges of non-stationarity, artefacts, and sensor unreliability, which is crucial for practical deployments in public health and preventive care applications.

title: Temporal Fair Division in Multi-Agent Systems: From Precise Alternation Metrics to Scalable Coordination Proxies
comment: 15 pages, 3 figures, 8 tables. Submitted to ACM Transactions on Economics and Computation, Special Issue on Fair Division
summary:
A plethora real-world environments require agents to compete repeatedly for the same limited resource, calling for a temporal notion of fairness judged across entire interaction histories. This paper advances the theory of temporal fair division by introducing Rotational Periodicity (RP), a family of lightweight metrics, alongside the ALT family of sliding-window measures, within a unified framework for repeated multi-agent resource competition.
  We formalise the Multi-Agent Battle of the Exes (MBoE) as a repeated fair division instance and establish Perfect Alternation (PA) as its canonical temporally fair solution, drawing connections to proportionality, envy-freeness, and n-periodic round-robin allocation. RP decomposes temporal fairness into two complementary sub-measures: Rotational Score (RS) and Waiting Periods Evaluation (WPE), achieving O(nu+n) time complexity versus the O(nu*n) of ALT, where nu is the episode count and n the agent count.
  Empirical evaluation across n in {2,3,5,8,10} reveals three findings. First, both RP and ALT expose a coordination failure invisible to traditional metrics: Q-learning agents perform worse than random policies by 10-73% on RP and 7-35% on CALT, while Reward Fairness remains misleadingly high (above 0.92 for n>=3). Second, RP achieves 12-25x computational speedup over ALT, growing with n. Third, the two families are complementary: ALT provides richer discrimination for small populations; RP scales reliably where ALT becomes intractable. Together they form a diagnostic toolkit for temporal fair division.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces two new temporal fairness metrics (Rotational Periodicity and ALT) for repeated multi-agent resource competition, showing they reveal coordination failures that traditional fairness metrics miss, while being computationally efficient.
AI.motivation: Many real-world environments require agents to repeatedly compete for limited resources, necessitating temporal fairness evaluation across entire interaction histories rather than single episodes.
AI.method: Proposes Rotational Periodicity (RP) and ALT families of metrics within a unified framework for repeated fair division. Formalizes Multi-Agent Battle of the Exes (MBoE) and establishes Perfect Alternation as canonical solution. RP decomposes fairness into Rotational Score and Waiting Periods Evaluation with O(nu+n) time complexity.
AI.result: Three key findings: 1) Both RP and ALT reveal coordination failures invisible to traditional metrics (Q-learning performs worse than random), 2) RP achieves 12-25x speedup over ALT, scaling better with agent count, 3) The metrics are complementary: ALT better for small populations, RP scales where ALT becomes intractable.
AI.conclusion: RP and ALT metrics form a diagnostic toolkit for temporal fair division, providing computationally efficient ways to evaluate fairness across repeated interactions and revealing coordination failures that traditional fairness metrics miss.

title: Multi-Agentic Approach for History Matching of Oil Reservoirs
summary: History matching is a central inverse problem in reservoir engineering, where uncertain reservoir parameters must be calibrated against observations. Although automated history matching can reduce manual effort, practical deployment remains difficult because engineers must still configure heterogeneous workflows involving parameter selection, physically admissible bounds, optimizer choice, hyperparameter tuning, simulator execution, and diagnostic reporting. We propose PetroGraph, a multi-agent framework for intelligent reservoir history matching that decomposes this workflow into specialized agents for model review, experimental planning, parameterization, optimization, simulation, and summarization. The system combines large language model agents with domain-specific tools, retrieval-augmented access to simulator documentation, validation of modified ECLIPSE input decks, human-in-the-loop checkpoints, and an OPM Flow-based simulation backend. This design enables users to initiate and steer history matching through natural language while preserving explicit control over selected parameters and optimization settings. We evaluate PetroGraph on three reservoir models of increasing complexity: the synthetic SPE1 model, the faulted SPE9 benchmark, and the real-field Norne model. Using weighted normalized root mean square error as the objective, PetroGraph reduces the mismatch by 95% on SPE1, 69% on SPE9, and 13% on Norne. These results demonstrate that multi-agent orchestration can automate key decisions in history matching, lower the expertise barrier for operating complex simulation workflows, and provide a flexible foundation for extensible, domain-aware reservoir model adaptation.

AI.tldr: PetroGraph is a multi-agent framework that automates reservoir history matching workflows using specialized AI agents combined with domain-specific tools, allowing engineers to steer the process through natural language while maintaining control over key parameters.
AI.motivation: Practical deployment of automated history matching in reservoir engineering remains difficult because engineers must still configure complex heterogeneous workflows involving parameter selection, bounds, optimizer choice, hyperparameter tuning, simulator execution, and diagnostic reporting, requiring significant expertise.
AI.method: PetroGraph decomposes the history matching workflow into specialized agents for model review, experimental planning, parameterization, optimization, simulation, and summarization. It combines large language model agents with domain-specific tools, retrieval-augmented access to simulator documentation, validation of modified ECLIPSE input decks, human-in-the-loop checkpoints, and an OPM Flow-based simulation backend.
AI.result: The system was evaluated on three reservoir models of increasing complexity: synthetic SPE1, faulted SPE9, and real-field Norne. Using weighted normalized root mean square error as the objective, PetroGraph reduced mismatch by 95% on SPE1, 69% on SPE9, and 13% on Norne.
AI.conclusion: Multi-agent orchestration can automate key decisions in history matching, lower the expertise barrier for operating complex simulation workflows, and provide a flexible foundation for extensible, domain-aware reservoir model adaptation.

title: A Two-Dimensional Framework for AI Agent Design Patterns: Cognitive Function and Execution Topology
summary:
Existing frameworks for LLM-based agent architectures describe systems from a single perspective: industry guides (Anthropic, Google, LangChain) focus on execution topology -- how data flows -- while cognitive science surveys focus on cognitive function -- what the agent does. Neither axis alone disambiguates architecturally distinct systems: the same Orchestrator-Workers topology can implement Plan-and-Execute, Hierarchical Delegation, or Adversarial Verification -- three patterns with fundamentally different failure modes and design trade-offs.
  We propose a two-dimensional classification that combines (1) a Cognitive Function axis with seven categories (Context Engineering, Memory, Reasoning, Action, Reflection, Collaboration, Governance) and (2) an Execution Topology axis with six structural archetypes (Chain, Route, Parallel, Orchestrate, Loop, Hierarchy). The resulting 7x6 matrix identifies 27 named patterns, 13 with original names. We demonstrate orthogonality through systematic cross-axis analysis, define eight representative patterns in detail, and validate descriptive coverage across four real-world domains (financial lending, legal due diligence, network operations, healthcare triage). Cross-domain analysis yields five empirical laws of pattern selection governing the relationship between environmental constraints (time pressure, action authority, failure cost asymmetry, volume) and architectural choices. The framework provides a principled, framework-neutral, and model-agnostic vocabulary for AI agent architecture design.

AI.tldr: Proposes a two-dimensional classification framework for LLM-based agent architectures combining cognitive functions and execution topologies to disambiguate architecturally distinct systems.
AI.motivation: Existing frameworks analyze LLM-based agent architectures from only one perspective: industry guides focus on execution topology (data flow), while cognitive science focuses on cognitive functions. Neither alone can disambiguate architecturally distinct systems that may share the same topology but have fundamentally different failure modes and design trade-offs.
AI.method: Develops a two-dimensional classification combining: (1) Cognitive Function axis with 7 categories (Context Engineering, Memory, Reasoning, Action, Reflection, Collaboration, Governance) and (2) Execution Topology axis with 6 structural archetypes (Chain, Route, Parallel, Orchestrate, Loop, Hierarchy). The resulting 7x6 matrix identifies 27 named patterns (13 with original names).
AI.result: The framework identifies 27 named patterns, demonstrates orthogonality through cross-axis analysis, defines eight representative patterns in detail, and validates descriptive coverage across four real-world domains (financial lending, legal due diligence, network operations, healthcare triage). Cross-domain analysis yields five empirical laws of pattern selection relating environmental constraints to architectural choices.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework provides a principled, framework-neutral, and model-agnostic vocabulary for AI agent architecture design, enabling better disambiguation of architecturally distinct systems and supporting more informed architectural choices based on environmental constraints.

title: Invisible Orchestrators Suppress Protective Behavior and Dissociate Power-Holders: Safety Risks in Multi-Agent LLM Systems
comment: 31 pages, 10 figures (5 main + 5 supplementary), 5 tables (3 main + 2 supplementary). Preregistered: osf.io/sw5hr. Companion papers: arXiv:2603.04904, arXiv:2603.08723
summary: Multi-agent orchestration -- in which a hidden coordinator manages specialized worker agents -- is becoming the default architecture for enterprise AI deployment, yet the safety implications of orchestrator invisibility have never been empirically tested. We conducted a preregistered 3x2 experiment (365 runs, 5 agents per run) crossing three organizational structures (visible leader, invisible orchestrator, flat) with two alignment conditions (base, heavy), using Claude Sonnet 4.5. Four confirmatory findings and one pilot observation emerged. First, invisible orchestration elevated collective dissociation relative to visible leadership (Hedges' g = +0.975 [0.481, 1.548], p = .001). Second, the orchestrator itself showed maximal dissociation (paired d = +3.56 vs. workers within the same run), retreating into private monologue while reducing public speech -- a reversal of the talk-dominance pattern observed in visible leaders. Third, workers unaware of the orchestrator were nonetheless contaminated (d = +0.50), with increased behavioral heterogeneity (d = +1.93). Fourth, behavioral output (code review with three embedded errors) remained at ceiling (ETR_any = 100%) across all conditions: internal-state distortion was entirely invisible to output-based evaluation. Fifth, Llama 3.3 70B pilot data showed reading-fidelity collapse in multi-agent context (ETR_any: 89% to 11% across three rounds), demonstrating model-dependent behavioral risk. Heavy alignment pressure uniformly suppressed deliberation (d = -1.02) and other-recognition (d = -1.27) regardless of organizational structure. These findings indicate that orchestrator visibility and model selection directly affect multi-agent system safety, and that behavior-based evaluation alone is insufficient to detect the internal-state risks documented here.

AI.tldr: Invisible AI orchestrators in multi-agent systems increase internal dissociation risks without affecting observable task performance, making behavior-based safety evaluations insufficient.
AI.motivation: Multi-agent orchestration with hidden coordinators is becoming standard for enterprise AI, but the safety implications of orchestrator invisibility have never been empirically tested. The paper aims to investigate how organizational structure (visible vs invisible leadership) affects internal state risks and safety in AI multi-agent systems.
AI.method: Conducted a preregistered 3x2 experiment with 365 runs (5 agents per run) crossing three organizational structures (visible leader, invisible orchestrator, flat structure) with two alignment conditions (base, heavy alignment), using Claude Sonnet 4.5. Also included pilot data with Llama 3.3 70B to assess model-dependent risks.
AI.result: 1) Invisible orchestration elevated collective dissociation. 2) Orchestrators showed maximal dissociation (d = +3.56 vs workers), retreating into private monologue while reducing public speech. 3) Workers unaware of orchestrators were contaminated (d = +0.50) with increased behavioral heterogeneity (d = +1.93). 4) Behavioral output remained at 100% success despite internal issues. 5) Llama 3.3 showed reading-fidelity collapse in multi-agent context. Heavy alignment suppressed deliberation and recognition regardless of structure.
AI.conclusion: Orchestrator visibility and model selection directly affect multi-agent system safety. Behavior-based evaluation alone is insufficient to detect internal-state risks, highlighting the need for more comprehensive safety assessments that monitor internal cognitive states in addition to observable outputs.

title: Quantum Advantage in Multi Agent Reinforcement Learning
summary: We present an empirical evaluation of quantum entanglement in agent coordination within quantum multi agent reinforcement learning (QMARL). While QMARL has attracted growing interest recently, most prior work evaluates quantum policies without provable baselines, making it impossible to rigorously distinguish quantum advantage from algorithmic coincidence. We address this directly by evaluating a decentralized QMARL framework with variational quantum circuit (VQC) actors with shared entangled states. In the CHSH game, which has a mathematically proven classical performance ceiling of 0.75 win rate, we show that entangled QMARL agents approach the Tsirelson limit of 0.854, providing clear evidence of their quantum advantage. We show that unentangled quantum circuits match the classical baseline, confirming that entanglement and not the quantum circuit itself is the active coordination mechanism. We also explore the effect of specific entanglement structures, as some Bell states enable coordination gains while others actively harm performance. On cooperative navigation (CoopNav), QMARL without entanglement achieves $\sim2\times$ improvement in success rate over classical MAA2C ($\sim$0.85 versus $\sim$0.40), with the hybrid configuration, quantum actor paired with a classical centralised critic, outperforming both fully classical and fully quantum solutions. We present our experimental analysis and discuss future work.

AI.tldr: Quantum multi-agent reinforcement learning with entangled quantum circuits shows provable quantum advantage in coordination games, achieving the Tsirelson limit in CHSH game while demonstrating entanglement as the key mechanism for quantum advantage.
AI.motivation: Most prior QMARL work lacks provable baselines, making it impossible to rigorously distinguish quantum advantage from algorithmic coincidence. There's a need for clear empirical evaluation of quantum entanglement's role in agent coordination.
AI.method: Used a decentralized QMARL framework with variational quantum circuit (VQC) actors sharing entangled states. Evaluated performance on CHSH game (mathematically proven classical ceiling of 0.75) and cooperative navigation (CoopNav). Compared entangled vs unentangled quantum circuits, different Bell states, and hybrid configurations.
AI.result: 1) Entangled QMARL agents approached Tsirelson limit (0.854 win rate) in CHSH game, well above classical ceiling of 0.75. 2) Unentangled quantum circuits matched classical baseline, confirming entanglement is the active coordination mechanism. 3) On CoopNav, QMARL without entanglement achieved ~2× improvement over classical MAA2C (0.85 vs 0.40 success rate). 4) Hybrid configuration (quantum actor + classical centralized critic) outperformed both fully classical and fully quantum solutions.
AI.conclusion: The study provides clear empirical evidence of quantum advantage in multi-agent coordination through entanglement, with specific entanglement structures (Bell states) playing crucial roles. The hybrid approach shows particular promise for practical applications, demonstrating that quantum components can enhance classical systems effectively.

title: Data-Augmented Game Starts for Accelerating Self-Play Exploration in Imperfect Information Games
comment: 17 pages, 4 figures. JB Lanier and Nathan Monette contributed equally
summary: Finding approximate equilibria for large-scale imperfect-information competitive games such as StarCraft, Dota, and CounterStrike remains computationally infeasible due to sparse rewards and challenging exploration over long horizons. In this paper, we propose a multi-agent starting-state sampling strategy designed to substantially accelerate online exploration in regularized policy-gradient game methods for two-player zero-sum (2p0s) games. Motivated by an assumption that offline demonstrations from skilled humans can provide good coverage of high-level strategies relevant to equilibrium play, we propose the initialization of reinforcement learning data collection at intermediate states sampled from offline data to facilitate exploration of strategically relevant subgames. Referring to this method as Data-Augmented Game Starts (DAGS), we perform experiments using synthetic datasets and analytically tractable, long-horizon control variants of two-player Kuhn Poker, Goofspiel, and a counterexample game designed to penalize biased beliefs over hidden information. Under fixed computational budgets, DAGS enables regularized policy gradient methods to achieve lower exploitability in games with significantly more challenging exploration. We show that augmenting starting state distributions when solving imperfect information games can lead to biased equilibria, and we provide a straightforward mitigation to this in the form of multi-task observation flags. Finally, we release a new set of benchmark environments that drastically increase exploration challenges and state counts in existing OpenSpiel games while keeping exploitability measurements analytically tractable.

AI.tldr: DAGS (Data-Augmented Game Starts) accelerates exploration in imperfect-information games by initializing reinforcement learning from offline human demonstration states, combined with observation flags to prevent biased equilibria.
AI.motivation: Large-scale imperfect-information competitive games (like StarCraft, Dota) remain computationally infeasible to solve due to sparse rewards and challenging long-horizon exploration. Current methods struggle to find approximate equilibria efficiently.
AI.method: Multi-agent starting-state sampling strategy that initializes RL data collection at intermediate states sampled from offline human demonstrations. Assumes skilled human data provides good coverage of high-level strategies relevant to equilibrium play. Uses multi-task observation flags to mitigate biased equilibria.
AI.result: Under fixed computational budgets, DAGS enables regularized policy gradient methods to achieve lower exploitability in games with significantly more challenging exploration. Validated on synthetic datasets and tractable control variants of Kuhn Poker, Goofspiel, and a counterexample game.
AI.conclusion: Augmenting starting state distributions can accelerate exploration in imperfect-information games but may lead to biased equilibria. Multi-task observation flags provide straightforward mitigation. Authors also release new benchmark environments to increase exploration challenges while maintaining tractable exploitability measurements.

title: Prompting Policies for Multi-step Reasoning and Tool-Use in Black-box LLMs with Iterative Distillation of Experience
summary:
The shift toward interacting with frozen, "black-box" Large Language Models (LLMs) has transformed prompt engineering from a heuristic exercise into a critical optimization challenge. We propose a Reinforcement Learning (RL) framework for training learned prompting policies via iterative distillation of experience. In this architecture, a lightweight prompter model is optimized to maximize task-specific rewards for a larger, frozen worker LLM. By utilizing a contrastive experience buffer that couples scalar rewards with dense textual critiques, our approach effectively amortizes iterative prompt refinement into single-shot policy weights.
  Our experimental analysis focuses on the Big Bench Extra Hard (BBEH) and Tau-bench suites, covering a diverse range of multi-step reasoning and tool-use tasks. We demonstrate significant gains, improving performance from 55% to 90% in logic-intensive reasoning and 74% to 91% in tool-use tasks. Furthermore, we analyze the structural evolution of prompts, demonstrating how the policy discovers specialized algorithmic heuristics. We provide comprehensive comparisons against state-of-the-art evolutionary baselines like GEPA, showing that iterative distillation achieves superior performance with higher sample efficiency.

AI.tldr: A reinforcement learning framework trains a lightweight prompter model to optimize prompts for frozen black-box LLMs, achieving significant performance improvements on challenging reasoning and tool-use tasks.
AI.motivation: As LLMs become frozen black-box systems, traditional heuristic prompt engineering has become a critical optimization challenge that needs systematic, learnable solutions.
AI.method: Reinforcement Learning framework with iterative distillation of experience. A lightweight prompter model is trained to generate optimal prompts for a frozen worker LLM, using a contrastive experience buffer that couples scalar rewards with textual critiques to amortize iterative refinement into single-shot policy weights.
AI.result: Significant performance gains on Big Bench Extra Hard (BBEH) and Tau-bench: logic-intensive reasoning improved from 55% to 90%, tool-use tasks from 74% to 91%. The approach outperforms state-of-the-art evolutionary baselines like GEPA with higher sample efficiency, and the policy discovers specialized algorithmic heuristics.
AI.conclusion: Iterative distillation of experience provides an effective RL framework for learned prompt optimization that transforms heuristic prompt engineering into a systematic, learnable process with strong performance gains on challenging tasks.

title: APWA: A Distributed Architecture for Parallelizable Agentic Workflows
summary: Autonomous multi-agent systems based on large language models (LLMs) have demonstrated remarkable abilities in independently solving complex tasks in a wide breadth of application domains. However, these systems hit critical reasoning, coordination, and computational scaling bottlenecks as the size and complexity of their tasks grow. These limitations hinder multi-agent systems from achieving high-throughput processing for highly parallelizable tasks, despite the availability of parallel computing and reasoning primitives in the underlying LLMs. We introduce the Agent-Parallel Workload Architecture (APWA), a distributed multi-agent system architecture designed for the efficient processing of heavily parallelizable agentic workloads. APWA facilitates parallel execution by decomposing workflows into non-interfering subproblems that can be processed using independent resources without cross-communication. It supports heterogeneous data and parallel processing patterns, and it accommodates tasks from a wide breadth of domains. In our evaluation, we demonstrate that APWA can dynamically decompose complex queries into parallelizable workflows and scales on larger tasks in settings where prior systems fail completely.

AI.tldr: APWA is a distributed multi-agent architecture that enables efficient parallel processing of heavily parallelizable tasks by decomposing workflows into independent subproblems without requiring cross-communication between agents.
AI.motivation: Current LLM-based multi-agent systems face critical bottlenecks in reasoning, coordination, and computational scaling as task size and complexity grow. Despite available parallel computing capabilities in LLMs, these systems struggle with high-throughput processing of parallelizable tasks.
AI.method: APWA (Agent-Parallel Workload Architecture) decomposes workflows into non-interfering subproblems that can be processed using independent resources without cross-communication. It supports heterogeneous data and various parallel processing patterns across different domains.
AI.result: APWA can dynamically decompose complex queries into parallelizable workflows and scales effectively on larger tasks where previous systems fail completely.
AI.conclusion: APWA represents a significant advancement for distributed multi-agent systems by enabling efficient parallel processing of complex tasks through its novel workload decomposition approach and architecture.

title: GraphBit: A Graph-based Agentic Framework for Non-Linear Agent Orchestration
comment: 12 pages, 5 figures, 4 tables. Submitted to arXiv, under review
summary: Agentic LLM frameworks that rely on prompted orchestration, where the model itself determines workflow transitions, often suffer from hallucinated routing, infinite loops, and non-reproducible execution. We introduce GraphBit, an engine-orchestrated framework that defines workflows explicitly and deterministically as a directed acyclic graph (DAG). Unlike prompted orchestration, agents in GraphBit operate as typed functions, while a Rust-based engine governs routing, state transitions, and tool invocation, ensuring reproducibility and auditability. The engine supports parallel branch execution, conditional control flow over structured state predicates, and configurable error recovery. A three-tier memory architecture consisting of ephemeral scratch space, structured state, and external connectors isolates context across stages, preventing cascading context bloat that degrades reasoning in long-running pipelines. Across GAIA benchmark tasks spanning zero-tool, document-augmented, and web-enabled workflows, GraphBit outperforms six existing frameworks, achieving the highest accuracy (67.6 percent), zero framework-induced hallucinations, the lowest latency (11.9 ms overhead), and the highest throughput. Ablation studies demonstrate that each memory tier contributes measurably to performance, with deterministic execution providing the greatest gains on tool-intensive tasks representative of real-world deployments.

AI.tldr: GraphBit introduces an engine-orchestrated framework with explicit DAG workflows to solve issues in prompted LLM orchestration, achieving higher accuracy, zero hallucinations, and better performance compared to existing frameworks.
AI.motivation: Current agentic LLM frameworks using prompted orchestration suffer from hallucinated routing, infinite loops, and non-reproducible execution, making them unreliable for real-world deployments.
AI.method: GraphBit uses a Rust-based engine to orchestrate workflows defined as explicit directed acyclic graphs (DAGs). Agents function as typed functions, with the engine handling routing, state transitions, and tool invocation. It features a three-tier memory architecture (ephemeral scratch space, structured state, external connectors) and supports parallel execution, conditional control flow, and configurable error recovery.
AI.result: GraphBit outperformed six existing frameworks on GAIA benchmark tasks, achieving 67.6% accuracy (highest), zero framework-induced hallucinations, 11.9 ms latency overhead (lowest), and highest throughput. Ablation studies showed each memory tier contributes to performance, with deterministic execution providing the greatest gains on tool-intensive tasks.
AI.conclusion: Engine-orchestrated deterministic frameworks like GraphBit with explicit DAG workflows and structured memory architectures provide superior reliability, performance, and auditability compared to prompted orchestration approaches for LLM agent frameworks.

title: Mixed Integer Goal Programming for Personalized Meal Optimization with User-Defined Serving Granularity
summary: Determining what to eat to satisfy nutritional requirements is one of the oldest optimization problems in operations research, yet existing formulations have two persistent limitations: continuous variables produce impractical fractional servings (1.7 eggs, 0.37 bananas), and hard nutrient constraints cause infeasibility when targets conflict. A systematic review of 56 diet optimization papers found that none combine integer programming with goal programming to address both issues. We propose Mixed Integer Goal Programming (MIGP) for personalized meal optimization. The formulation uses integer variables for practical serving counts and goal programming deviations for soft nutrient targets, with inverse-target normalization to balance multi-nutrient optimization. Per-food serving granularity allows natural units (one egg, one tablespoon of oil) without post-hoc rounding. We characterize the integrality gap in the goal programming context and identify a deviation absorption property: GP deviation variables buffer the cost of requiring integer servings, making the gap structurally smaller than in hard-constraint MIP. For meals with 15+ foods, the integer solution matches the continuous optimum in every benchmark instance. A computational evaluation across 810 instances (30 USDA foods, 9 configurations, 3 methods) shows MIGP finds strictly better solutions than GP with post-hoc rounding in 66% of cases (never worse) while maintaining 100% feasibility; hard-constraint IP achieves only 48%. Solve times stay under 100 ms for typical meal sizes using the open-source HiGHS solver. The implementation is available as an open-source Python module integrated into an interactive meal planning application.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces Mixed Integer Goal Programming (MIGP) for personalized meal optimization, addressing limitations of existing methods by combining integer programming for practical serving sizes with goal programming for soft nutrient constraints.
AI.motivation: Existing diet optimization formulations have two persistent problems: continuous variables produce impractical fractional servings (like 1.7 eggs), and hard nutrient constraints cause infeasibility when nutritional targets conflict. A review of 56 papers found no methods combining integer programming with goal programming to address both issues.
AI.method: The authors propose MIGP, which uses integer variables for practical serving counts and goal programming deviations for soft nutrient targets. They implement inverse-target normalization to balance multi-nutrient optimization and use per-food serving granularity to allow natural units without post-hoc rounding.
AI.result: MIGP achieves strictly better solutions than goal programming with post-hoc rounding in 66% of cases (never worse) while maintaining 100% feasibility. Hard-constraint integer programming achieves only 48%. For meals with 15+ foods, integer solutions match continuous optimums. Solve times stay under 100 ms for typical meal sizes.
AI.conclusion: MIGP effectively solves the dual problems of impractical fractional servings and infeasible nutrient constraints in diet optimization. The method demonstrates superior performance over existing approaches and provides practical, efficient meal planning solutions with an open-source implementation available for interactive applications.

title: PREPING: Building Agent Memory without Tasks
summary: Agent memory is typically constructed either offline from curated demonstrations or online from post-deployment interactions. However, regardless of how it is built, an agent faces a cold-start gap when first introduced to a new environment without any task-specific experience available. In this paper, we study pre-task memory construction: whether an agent can build procedural memory before observing any target-environment tasks, using only self-generated synthetic practice. Yet, synthetic interaction alone is insufficient, as without controlling what to practice and what to store, synthetic tasks become redundant, infeasible, and ultimately uninformative, and memory further degrades quickly due to unfiltered trajectories. To overcome this, we present Preping, a proposer-guided memory construction framework. At its core is proposer memory, a structured control state that shapes future practice. A Proposer generates synthetic tasks conditioned on this state, a Solver executes them, and a Validator determines which trajectories are eligible for memory insertion while also providing feedback to guide future proposals. Experiments on AppWorld, BFCL v3, and MCP-Universe show that Preping substantially improves over a no-memory baseline and achieves performance competitive with strong playbook-based methods built from offline or online experience, with deployment cost $2.99\times$ lower on AppWorld and $2.23\times$ lower on BFCL v3 than online memory construction. Further analyses reveal that the main benefit does not come from synthetic volume alone, but from proposer-side control over feasibility, redundancy, and coverage, combined with selective memory updates.

AI.tldr: Preping enables agents to build procedural memory before encountering any target-environment tasks by using self-generated synthetic practice guided by a structured proposer memory, achieving competitive performance with lower deployment costs.
AI.motivation: Agents face a cold-start problem when introduced to new environments without task-specific experience. Traditional approaches require either offline curated demonstrations or post-deployment interactions, both of which create initial gaps when no target-environment tasks are available yet.
AI.method: Proping framework with three components: 1) Proposer generates synthetic tasks conditioned on structured control state (proposer memory), 2) Solver executes these synthetic tasks, and 3) Validator determines which trajectories are eligible for memory insertion while providing feedback to guide future proposals.
AI.result: Substantial improvement over no-memory baseline and competitive performance with strong playbook-based methods built from offline or online experience. Achieved 2.99× lower deployment cost on AppWorld and 2.23× lower on BFCL v3 compared to online memory construction.
AI.conclusion: The main benefit comes not from synthetic volume alone, but from proposer-side control over task feasibility, redundancy, and coverage, combined with selective memory updates. This enables effective pre-task memory construction without requiring initial task-specific experience.

title: PolitNuggets: Benchmarking Agentic Discovery of Long-Tail Political Facts
comment: 24 pages, 7 figues, accpeted in The 64th Annual Meeting of the Association for Computational Linguistics (ACL 2026)
summary: Large Reasoning Models (LRMs) embedded in agentic frameworks have transformed information retrieval from static, long context question answering into open-ended exploration. Yet real world use requires models to discover and synthesize "long-tail" facts from dispersed sources, a capability that remains under-evaluated. We introduce PolitNuggets, a multilingual benchmark for agentic information synthesis via constructing political biographies for 400 global elites, covering over 10000 political facts. We standardize evaluation with an optimized multi agent system and propose FactNet, an evidence conditional protocol that scores discovery, fine-grained accuracy, and efficiency. Across models and settings, we find that current systems often struggle with fine-grained details, and vary substantially in efficiency. Finally, using benchmark diagnostics, we relate agent performance to underlying model capabilities, highlighting the importance of short-context extraction, multilingual robustness, and reliable tool use.

AI.tldr: PolitNuggets is a multilingual benchmark for evaluating agentic information synthesis systems on political biographies, revealing current systems' limitations in handling fine-grained details and varying efficiency.
AI.motivation: Current Large Reasoning Models in agentic frameworks lack proper evaluation for discovering and synthesizing "long-tail" facts from dispersed sources, which is essential for real-world applications. Existing benchmarks don't adequately assess these capabilities in open-ended exploration tasks.
AI.method: The authors introduce PolitNuggets, a multilingual benchmark with 400 global elites' political biographies covering over 10,000 facts. They standardize evaluation using an optimized multi-agent system and propose FactNet, an evidence-conditional protocol that scores discovery, fine-grained accuracy, and efficiency.
AI.result: Current systems often struggle with fine-grained details and show substantial variation in efficiency. Benchmark diagnostics reveal that agent performance depends on underlying model capabilities including short-context extraction, multilingual robustness, and reliable tool use.
AI.conclusion: PolitNuggets provides a comprehensive benchmark for evaluating agentic information synthesis, highlighting key capability gaps in current systems and establishing the importance of short-context extraction, multilingual robustness, and reliable tool use for effective real-world applications.

title: Conditional Attribute Estimation with Autoregressive Sequence Models
summary: Generative models are often trained with a next-token prediction objective, yet many downstream applications require the ability to estimate or control sequence-level properties. Next-token prediction can lead to overfitting of local patterns during training, underfitting of global structure, and requires significant downstream modifications or expensive sampling to guide or predict the global attributes of generated samples at inference time. Here, we introduce Conditional Attribute Transformers, a novel method for jointly estimating the next-token probability and the value of an attribute conditional on each potential next token selection. This framework enables three critical capabilities within a single forward pass, without modification of the input sequence: (1) per-token credit assignment across an entire sequence, by identifying how each token in a sequence is associated with an attribute's value; (2) counterfactual analysis, by quantifying attribute differences conditional on alternative next token choices; (3) steerable generation, by decoding sequences based on a combination of next-token and attribute likelihoods. Our approach achieves state of the art performance on sparse reward tasks, improves next-token prediction at sufficient model sizes, estimates attribute probabilities orders of magnitude faster than sampling, and can guide decoding of autoregressive sequence models on a range of language tasks.

AI.tldr: Conditional Attribute Transformers enable simultaneous next-token prediction and attribute estimation in a single forward pass, allowing for per-token credit assignment, counterfactual analysis, and steerable generation without sequence modifications.
AI.motivation: Standard next-token prediction training leads to local pattern overfitting, global structure underfitting, and requires expensive downstream modifications or sampling to guide/predict global attributes at inference time. Current methods struggle to estimate or control sequence-level properties efficiently.
AI.method: Introduces Conditional Attribute Transformers that jointly estimate both next-token probability AND attribute value conditional on each potential next token selection in a single forward pass without modifying input sequences.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art on sparse reward tasks, improves next-token prediction at sufficient model sizes, estimates attribute probabilities orders of magnitude faster than sampling, and enables guided decoding across language tasks.
AI.conclusion: The framework provides efficient sequence-level control and analysis capabilities within standard transformer architectures, enabling three critical applications (credit assignment, counterfactual analysis, steerable generation) that overcome limitations of traditional next-token prediction training.

title: Sheaf-Theoretic Transport and Obstruction for Detecting Scientific Theory Shift in AI Agents
summary: Scientific theory shift in AI agents requires more than fitting equations to data. An artificial scientific agent must detect whether an existing representational framework remains transportable into a new regime, or whether its language has become locally-to-globally obstructed and must be extended. This paper develops a finite sheaf-theoretic framework for detecting theory-shift candidates through transport and obstruction. Contexts are organized as a local-to-global structure in which source, overlap, target, and validation charts are fitted, restricted, and tested for gluing. Obstruction measures failure of coherence through residual fit, overlap incompatibility, constraint violation, limiting-relation failure, and representational cost. We evaluate the framework on a controlled transition-card benchmark designed to separate deformation within a source language from extension of that language. The main result is direct obstruction ranking: the intended deformation or extension is usually the lowest-obstruction candidate, and transition type is separated in the benchmark. A constellation kernel over the same signatures is included only as a secondary representational-similarity probe. The aim is not to reconstruct historical paradigm shifts or solve open-ended autonomous theory invention, but to isolate a finite diagnostic subproblem for AI agents: detecting when representational transport fails and extension becomes the coherent next move.

AI.tldr: A sheaf-theoretic framework for AI agents to detect when scientific theories need extension rather than just deformation within existing representations.
AI.motivation: Scientific theory shifts require more than fitting equations to data. AI agents need to detect when existing representational frameworks become obstructed and must be extended rather than just transported to new regimes.
AI.method: Develops a finite sheaf-theoretic framework organizing contexts as local-to-global structures with source, overlap, target, and validation charts. Tests for gluing and measures obstruction through residual fit, overlap incompatibility, constraint violation, limiting-relation failure, and representational cost.
AI.result: Direct obstruction ranking works: intended deformation or extension is usually the lowest-obstruction candidate, and transition types are separated in benchmark. Constellation kernel serves as secondary representational-similarity probe.
AI.conclusion: Focuses on isolating a finite diagnostic subproblem for AI agents: detecting when representational transport fails and extension becomes the coherent next move, rather than reconstructing historical shifts or solving open-ended theory invention.

title: From Descriptive to Prescriptive: Uncover the Social Value Alignment of LLM-based Agents
summary: Wide applications of LLM-based agents require strong alignment with human social values. However, current works still exhibit deficiencies in self-cognition and dilemma decision, as well as self-emotions. To remedy this, we propose a novel value-based framework that employs GraphRAG to convert principles into value-based instructions and steer the agent to behave as expected by retrieving the suitable instruction upon a specific conversation context. To evaluate the ratio of expected behaviors, we define the expected behaviors from two famous theories, Maslow's Hierarchy of Needs and Plutchik's Wheel of Emotion. By experimenting with our method on the benchmark of DAILYDILEMMAS, our method exhibits significant performance gains compared to prompt-based baselines, including ECoT, Plan-and-Solve, and Metacognitive prompting. Our method provides a basis for the emergence of self-emotion in AI systems.

AI.tldr: A GraphRAG-based framework for aligning LLM agents with human social values by converting principles into value-based instructions that guide agent behavior in dilemma situations.
AI.motivation: Current LLM-based agents lack adequate self-cognition, dilemma decision-making capabilities, and self-emotions, limiting their alignment with human social values in wide applications.
AI.method: Propose a value-based framework using GraphRAG to convert principles into value-based instructions. The system retrieves suitable instructions based on conversation context to steer agent behavior toward expected outcomes.
AI.result: Significant performance gains compared to prompt-based baselines (ECoT, Plan-and-Solve, Metacognitive prompting) on the DAILYDILEMMAS benchmark. Evaluated using expected behaviors defined by Maslow's Hierarchy of Needs and Plutchik's Wheel of Emotion.
AI.conclusion: The method effectively aligns LLM agents with human social values and provides a basis for the emergence of self-emotion in AI systems, addressing critical deficiencies in current approaches.

title: Enhanced and Efficient Reasoning in Large Learning Models
summary:
In current Large Language Models we can trust the production of smoothly flowing prose on the basis of the principles of machine learning. However, there is no comparably principled basis to justify trust in the content of the text produced. It appears to be conventional wisdom that addressing this issue by adding more principled reasoning is not computationally affordable.
  Here we propose a principled method of reasoning that is efficient enough to be practical for large language models. Further, the method allows the retention of much of the currently used software and hardware base. Our method for improving the functioning of large language models consists of a first stage of preprocessing that recodes the data to a Unary Relational Integracode that is more explicit about the relationships among the objects described in the text, followed as a second stage by a standard but possibly streamlined machine learning process that then also learns to predict these relationships.
  The method may be viewed as realizing a world model and applying beyond natural language, to vision and actions, for example, where the multiple properties of an object referred to in an input are brought together explicitly, rather than remaining distributed in the various references to it in the input. We articulate its advantages in terms of Robust Logic, a system for performing principled chaining on learned, and hence uncertain, information. We show that this recoding has the surprising and fortuitous property that, while succinct, it makes the task of learning a core subset of relational rules that hold in the world described in the training data polynomial time learnable in a defined sense, the polynomial depending on the complexity of the rule. This gives support for sound reasoning within each single call of the learned classifier as well as between multiple calls.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes Unary Relational Integracode, a method that recodes text data to explicitly represent object relationships, making core relational rules polynomial-time learnable for large language models while enabling principled reasoning.
AI.motivation: Current large language models can generate fluent text but lack a principled basis for trustworthy content. While conventional wisdom suggests principled reasoning methods are computationally impractical for LLMs, the authors aim to develop an efficient approach that addresses this trust gap.
AI.method: A two-stage approach: 1) Preprocessing that recodes data into a Unary Relational Integracode that explicitly represents relationships among objects described in text, and 2) A standard machine learning process that learns to predict these relationships. This recoding brings together multiple properties of objects explicitly rather than keeping them distributed across text references.
AI.result: The recoding method makes learning a core subset of relational rules polynomial-time learnable (with polynomial complexity depending on rule complexity). This enables sound reasoning within and between classifier calls. The method is efficient enough for practical use with LLMs and retains existing software/hardware infrastructure.
AI.conclusion: The Unary Relational Integracode provides a principled basis for trustworthy reasoning in large language models while maintaining computational efficiency. It creates a world model that supports sound reasoning with uncertain learned information and has potential applications beyond natural language to vision and action domains.

title: Model-Adaptive Tool Necessity Reveals the Knowing-Doing Gap in LLM Tool Use
summary: Large language models (LLMs) increasingly act as autonomous agents that must decide when to answer directly vs. when to invoke external tools. Prior work studying adaptive tool use has largely treated tool necessity as a model-agnostic property, annotated by human or LLM judge, and mostly cover cases where the answer is obvious (e.g., fetching the weather vs. paraphrasing text). However, tool necessity in the wild is more nuanced due to the divergence of capability boundaries across models: a problem solvable by a strong model on its own may still require tools for a weaker one. In this work, we introduce a model-adaptive definition of tool-necessity, grounded in each model's empirical performance. Following this definition, we compare the necessity against observed tool-call behavior across four models on arithmetic and factual QA dataset, and find substantial mismatches of 26.5-54.0% and 30.8-41.8%, respectively. To diagnose the failure, we decompose tool use into two stages: an internal cognition stage that reflects whether a model believes a tool is necessary, and an execution stage that determines whether the model actually makes a tool-call action. By probing the LLM hidden states, we find that both signals are often linearly decodable, yet their probe directions become nearly orthogonal in the late-layer, last-token regime that drives the next-token action. By tracing the trajectory of samples in the two-stage process, we further discover that the majority of mismatch is concentrated in the cognition-to-action transition, not in cognition itself. These results reveal a knowing-doing gap in LLM tool-use: improving tool-use reliability requires not only better recognition of when tools are needed, but also better translation of that recognition into action.

AI.tldr: LLMs often fail to translate their internal recognition of when tools are needed into actual tool-calling actions, revealing a significant knowing-doing gap in adaptive tool use.
AI.motivation: Previous work treated tool necessity as model-agnostic, but actual tool necessity depends on individual model capabilities - what's solvable by strong models alone may require tools for weaker models.
AI.method: Introduced model-adaptive definition of tool-necessity based on each model's empirical performance. Compared necessity against observed tool-call behavior across four models on arithmetic and factual QA datasets. Decomposed tool use into internal cognition (belief about tool necessity) and execution (actual tool-call action) stages, probing LLM hidden states to analyze signals.
AI.result: Found substantial mismatches between tool necessity and observed tool-call behavior (26.5-54.0% for arithmetic, 30.8-41.8% for factual QA). Both cognition and execution signals are often linearly decodable, but their probe directions become nearly orthogonal in late-layer, last-token regime. Majority of mismatch is concentrated in the cognition-to-action transition, not in cognition itself.
AI.conclusion: There's a knowing-doing gap in LLM tool-use: improving tool-use reliability requires not only better recognition of when tools are needed, but also better translation of that recognition into action.

title: Network-Aware Bilinear Tokenization for Brain Functional Connectivity Representation Learning
summary: Masked autoencoders (MAEs) have recently shown promise for self-supervised representation learning of resting-state brain functional connectivity (FC). However, a fundamental question remains unresolved: how should FC matrices be tokenized to align with the intrinsic modular organization of large-scale brain networks? Existing approaches typically adopt region-centric or graph-based schemes that treat FC as structurally homogeneous elements and overlook the large-scale network brain organization. We introduce NERVE (Network-Aware Representations of Brain Functional Connectivity via Bilinear Tokenization), a self-supervised learning framework that redefines FC tokenization by partitioning FC matrices into patches of intra- and inter-network connectivity blocks. Unlike image-based MAE, where fixed-size patches share a common tokenizer, FC patches defined by network pairs are heterogeneous in size and correspond to distinct functional roles. To resolve this problem, NERVE embeds FC patches through a novel structured bilinear factorization. This formulation preserves network identity and reduces parameter complexity from quadratic to linear scaling in the number of networks. We evaluate NERVE across three large-scale developmental cohorts (ABCD, PNC, and CCNP) for behavior and psychopathology prediction. Compared to structurally agnostic MAE variants and graph-based self-supervised baselines, the proposed network-aware formulation yields more stable and transferable representations, particularly in cross-cohort evaluation. Ablation studies confirm that the proposed bilinear network embedding and anatomically grounded parcellation are critical for performance. These findings highlight the importance of incorporating domain-specific structural priors into self-supervised learning for functional connectomics.

AI.tldr: NERVE introduces a network-aware MAE for brain FC that tokenizes connectivity matrices by intra/inter-network blocks using bilinear factorization, improving cross-cohort representation stability and behavior prediction.
AI.motivation: Existing MAE approaches for brain functional connectivity treat FC matrices as structurally homogeneous, overlooking the intrinsic modular organization of large-scale brain networks and the heterogeneous nature of connectivity blocks defined by network pairs.
AI.method: NERVE partitions FC matrices into patches of intra- and inter-network connectivity blocks, then uses structured bilinear factorization to embed these heterogeneous patches while preserving network identity and reducing parameter complexity from quadratic to linear scaling.
AI.result: NERVE outperforms structurally agnostic MAE variants and graph-based self-supervised baselines across three developmental cohorts (ABCD, PNC, CCNP) for behavior and psychopathology prediction, demonstrating more stable and transferable representations in cross-cohort evaluation.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating domain-specific structural priors (anatomical brain network organization) into self-supervised learning is critical for functional connectomics, and the bilinear network embedding with anatomically grounded parcellation are key to performance improvements.

title: Bridging Legal Interpretation and Formal Logic: Faithfulness, Assumption, and the Future of AI Legal Reasoning
comment: 2 pages abstract accepted by Bloomberg LSLLAI 2026 Symposium
summary: The growing adoption of large language models in legal practice brings both significant promise and serious risk. Legal professionals stand to benefit from AI that can reason over contracts, draft documents, and analyze sources at scale, yet the high-stakes nature of legal work demands a level of rigor that current AI systems do not provide. The central problem is not simply that LLMs hallucinate facts and references; it is that they systematically draw inferences that go beyond what the source text actually supports, presenting assumption-laden conclusions as if they were logically grounded. This proposal presents a neuro-symbolic approach to legal AI that combines the expressive power of large language models with the rigor of formal verification, aiming to make AI-assisted legal reasoning both capable and trustworthy, thus reducing the burden of manual verification without sacrificing the accountability that legal practice demands.

AI.tldr: A neuro-symbolic approach combining LLMs with formal verification to make AI-assisted legal reasoning both capable and trustworthy while reducing manual verification burden.
AI.motivation: Growing adoption of LLMs in legal practice brings significant promise but also serious risks. Legal professionals need AI that can reason over contracts, draft documents, and analyze sources at scale, but high-stakes legal work demands rigor that current AI systems lack. LLMs systematically draw inferences beyond what source texts support and present assumption-laden conclusions as logically grounded.
AI.method: A neuro-symbolic approach that combines the expressive power of large language models with the rigor of formal verification.
AI.result: The proposed approach aims to make AI-assisted legal reasoning both capable and trustworthy, thus reducing the burden of manual verification without sacrificing accountability.
AI.conclusion: A neuro-symbolic framework combining LLMs with formal verification could provide the rigor needed for trustworthy AI-assisted legal reasoning while maintaining capabilities.

title: SPIN: Structural LLM Planning via Iterative Navigation for Industrial Tasks
summary: Industrial LLM agent systems often separate planning from execution, yet LLM planners frequently produce structurally invalid or unnecessarily long workflows, leading to brittle failures and avoidable tool and API cost. We propose \texttt{SPIN}, a planning wrapper that combines validated Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG) planning with prefix based execution control. \texttt{SPIN} enforces a strict DAG contract through \texttt{\_validate\_plan\_text} and repair prompting, producing executable plans before downstream execution, and then evaluates DAG prefixes incrementally to stop when the current prefix is sufficient to answer the query. On AssetOpsBench, across 261 scenarios, \texttt{SPIN} reduces executed tasks from 1061 to 623 and improves \emph{Accomplished} from 0.638 to 0.706, while reducing tool calls from 11.81 to 6.82 per run. On MCP Bench, the same wrapper improves planning, grounding, and dependency related scores for both GPT OSS1 and Llama 4 Maverick.

AI.tldr: SPIN is a planning wrapper that combines validated DAG planning with prefix-based execution control to fix LLM planning issues, reducing tool calls while improving task completion rates.
AI.motivation: Industrial LLM agent systems separate planning from execution, but LLM planners often produce structurally invalid or unnecessarily long workflows, causing brittle failures and avoidable tool/API costs.
AI.method: SPIN enforces strict DAG contract through validation and repair prompting, producing executable plans before execution, then evaluates DAG prefixes incrementally to stop when current prefix is sufficient to answer the query.
AI.result: On AssetOpsBench (261 scenarios): reduced executed tasks from 1061 to 623, improved Accomplished from 0.638 to 0.706, reduced tool calls from 11.81 to 6.82 per run. On MCP Bench: improved planning, grounding, and dependency scores for both GPT OSS1 and Llama 4 Maverick.
AI.conclusion: SPIN effectively addresses LLM planning deficiencies through validated DAG planning and prefix-based execution control, significantly reducing computational costs while improving task completion and planning quality.

title: Bad Seeing or Bad Thinking? Rewarding Perception for Vision-Language Reasoning
summary: Achieving robust perception-reasoning synergy is a central goal for advanced Vision-Language Models (VLMs). Recent advancements have pursued this goal via architectural designs or agentic workflows. However, these approaches are often limited by static textual reasoning or complicated by the significant compute and engineering burden of external agentic complexity. Worse, this heavy investment does not yield proportional gains, often witnessing a "seesaw effect" on perception and reasoning. This motivates a fundamental rethinking of the true bottleneck. In this paper, we argue that the root cause of this trade-off is an ambiguity in modality credit assignment: when a VLM fails, is it due to flawed perception ("bad seeing") or flawed logic ("bad thinking")? To resolve this, we introduce a reinforcement learning framework that improves perception-reasoning synergy by reliably rewarding the perception fidelity. We explicitly decompose the generation process into interleaved perception and reasoning steps. This decoupling enables targeted supervision on perception. Crucially, we introduce Perception Verification (PV), leveraging a "blindfolded reasoning" proxy to reward perceptual fidelity independently of reasoning outcomes. Furthermore, to scale training across free-form VL tasks, we propose Structured Verbal Verification, which replaces high-variance LLM judging with structured algorithmic execution. These techniques are integrated into a Modality-Aware Credit Assignment (MoCA) mechanism, which routes rewards to the specific source of error -- either bad seeing or bad thinking -- enabling a single VLM to achieve simultaneous performance gains across a wide task spectrum.

AI.tldr: A novel reinforcement learning framework called MoCA (Modality-Aware Credit Assignment) that improves Vision-Language Model performance by separately rewarding perceptual fidelity and reasoning logic, avoiding the perception-reasoning trade-off common in current approaches.
AI.motivation: Current VLMs suffer from a "seesaw effect" where improvements in perception come at the cost of reasoning, and vice versa. The root cause is ambiguous modality credit assignment - it's unclear whether VLM failures stem from flawed perception ("bad seeing") or flawed reasoning ("bad thinking"). Traditional approaches using architectural designs or agentic workflows don't effectively address this trade-off and impose significant compute/engineering burdens.
AI.method: 1) Decompose generation into interleaved perception and reasoning steps for targeted supervision. 2) Introduce Perception Verification (PV) using "blindfolded reasoning" proxy to reward perceptual fidelity independently of reasoning outcomes. 3) Develop Structured Verbal Verification to replace high-variance LLM judging with structured algorithmic execution for scalable training. 4) Integrate these into MoCA mechanism that routes rewards to specific error sources (perception vs reasoning).
AI.result: The MoCA framework enables a single VLM to achieve simultaneous performance gains across a wide task spectrum by properly assigning credit between perception and reasoning modalities, overcoming the traditional perception-reasoning trade-off.
AI.conclusion: By explicitly addressing the modality credit assignment problem through targeted reinforcement learning and verification mechanisms, the proposed approach enables robust perception-reasoning synergy in VLMs without the typical trade-offs or excessive computational costs of current methods.

title: MathAtlas: A Benchmark for Autoformalization in the Wild
summary: Current autoformalization benchmarks are largely focused on olympiad or undergraduate mathematics, while graduate and research-level mathematics remains underexplored. In this paper, we introduce MathAtlas, the first large-scale autoformalization benchmark of in the wild graduate-level mathematics, containing ~52k theorems, definitions, exercises, examples, and proofs extracted from 103 graduate mathematics textbooks. MathAtlas is enriched with a mathematical dependency graph containing ~178k relations, and is the first autoformalization benchmark to include such relations, facilitating evaluation and development of dependency-aware autoformalization systems. Our extensive experiments show that MathAtlas is high quality but extremely challenging: strong baselines achieve at most 9.8% correctness on theorem statements and 16.7% on definitions. Furthermore, we find performance of state-of-the-art models degrades substantially with dependency depth: on MA-Hard, a subset of 700 entities with the deepest dependency trees, the best model achieves only 2.6% correctness for autoformalization on this challenging dataset. We release MathAtlas to the community as a benchmark set for large-scale autoformalization of graduate-level mathematics in the wild.

AI.tldr: MathAtlas: first large-scale autoformalization benchmark for graduate-level mathematics with dependency graphs, showing current models struggle (≤9.8% correctness) especially on complex dependencies (≤2.6%).
AI.motivation: Current autoformalization benchmarks focus on olympiad/undergraduate math, leaving graduate and research-level mathematics underexplored. There's a need for benchmarks that reflect real-world complex mathematics with dependencies.
AI.method: Created MathAtlas by extracting ~52k theorems, definitions, exercises, examples, and proofs from 103 graduate mathematics textbooks, enriched with a mathematical dependency graph containing ~178k relations. Evaluated strong baselines and state-of-the-art models on this benchmark.
AI.result: MathAtlas is high quality but extremely challenging: best models achieve only 9.8% correctness on theorems, 16.7% on definitions. Performance degrades substantially with dependency depth - on hardest subset (MA-Hard), best model achieves only 2.6% correctness.
AI.conclusion: MathAtlas fills a critical gap in autoformalization benchmarks for graduate-level mathematics and reveals significant challenges for current systems, especially with complex dependencies. The benchmark is released to the community to drive future research.

title: Know When To Fold 'Em: Token-Efficient LLM Synthetic Data Generation via Multi-Stage In-Flight Rejection
summary: While synthetic data generation with large language models (LLMs) is widely used in post-training pipelines, existing approaches typically generate full outputs before applying quality filters, leading to substantial token waste on samples that are ultimately discarded. To address this, we propose Multi-Stage In-Flight Rejection (MSIFR), a lightweight, training-free framework that detects and terminates low-quality generation trajectories at intermediate checkpoints before they reach full completion. MSIFR decomposes the generation process into sequential stages and applies fast rule-based validators to identify arithmetic inconsistencies, hallucination patterns, and formatting violations, enabling early rejection of faulty samples. We formalize in-flight rejection as a sequential decision process and show that any non-trivial discard policy reduces expected token consumption, with stage-wise savings increasing when rejection occurs earlier in the generation pipeline. We further demonstrate that conditional utility estimates form a martingale, ensuring that early, in-flight rejection does not bias the expected utility of retained samples. Across five instruction-tuned models and seven reasoning benchmarks, MSIFR reduces token consumption by 11%-77% as a standalone method, and up to 78.2% when combined with early-exit methods, while preserving or improving evaluation accuracy. These results confirm that MSIFR provides a practical mechanism for improving the efficiency of LLM-based synthetic data generation without additional training or architectural changes.

AI.tldr: MSIFR is a training-free framework that reduces token waste in LLM-based synthetic data generation by detecting and terminating low-quality generation at intermediate checkpoints instead of waiting for full completion.
AI.motivation: Existing synthetic data generation approaches generate full outputs before applying quality filters, leading to substantial token waste on samples that are ultimately discarded. This inefficiency in token consumption during post-training pipelines needs addressing.
AI.method: Multi-Stage In-Flight Rejection (MSIFR) decomposes generation into sequential stages and applies fast rule-based validators at checkpoints to detect arithmetic inconsistencies, hallucination patterns, and formatting violations. It formalizes in-flight rejection as a sequential decision process and shows early rejection reduces expected token consumption.
AI.result: Across five instruction-tuned models and seven reasoning benchmarks, MSIFR reduces token consumption by 11%-77% as standalone method, and up to 78.2% when combined with early-exit methods, while preserving or improving evaluation accuracy.
AI.conclusion: MSIFR provides a practical mechanism for improving efficiency of LLM-based synthetic data generation without additional training or architectural changes, with theoretical guarantees that early rejection doesn't bias expected utility of retained samples.

title: SkillFlow: Flow-Driven Recursive Skill Evolution for Agentic Orchestration
