AI.tldr: LongAct benchmark evaluates high-level planning for long-horizon household tasks through free-form instructions, and HoloMind agent with hierarchical planning and memory systems shows significant improvements but still faces major challenges with only 59% goal completion.
AI.motivation: Existing embodied AI benchmarks focus too much on short-horizon navigation/manipulation with fixed task categories, overlooking the need for robust high-level planning and sustained reasoning capabilities required for long-horizon household tasks.
AI.method: 1) Created LongAct benchmark to evaluate planning-level autonomy by abstracting away low-level control; 2) Proposed HoloMind agent with DAG-based hierarchical planner, Multimodal Spatial Memory, Episodic Memory, and global Critic for reflective supervision.
AI.result: HoloMind substantially improves long-horizon performance while reducing reliance on model scale. However, even top models achieve only 59% goal completion and 16% full-task success on LongAct, highlighting the difficulty of long-horizon planning.
AI.conclusion: LongAct reveals a significant gap in embodied agents' long-horizon planning capabilities. The proposed HoloMind architecture shows promise but current methods are far from solving the challenge, indicating strong need for better long-horizon reasoning in embodied AI.

title: TabPFN-3: Technical Report
summary: Tabular data underpins most high-value prediction problems in science and industry, and TabPFN has driven the foundation model revolution for this modality. Designed with feedback from our users, TabPFN-3 builds on this foundation to scale state-of-the-art performance to datasets with 1M training rows and substantially reduce training and inference time. Pretrained exclusively on synthetic data from our prior, TabPFN-3 dramatically pushes the frontier of tabular prediction and brings substantial gains on time series, relational, and tabular-text data. On the standard tabular benchmark TabArena, a forward pass of TabPFN-3 outperforms all other models, including tuned and ensembled baselines, by a significant margin, and pareto-dominates the speed/performance frontier. On more diverse datasets, TabPFN-3 ranks first on datasets with many classes, and beats 8-hour-tuned gradient-boosted-tree baselines on datasets up to 1M training rows and 200 features. TabPFN-3 introduces test-time compute scaling to tabular foundation models. Our API offering TabPFN-3-Plus (Thinking) exploits this to beat all non-TabPFN models by over 200 Elo on TabArena, rising to 420 Elo on the largest data subset, and outperforms AutoGluon 1.5 extreme while being 10x faster, without using LLMs, real data, internet search or any other model besides TabPFN. TabPFN-3 extends the capabilities of our models, enabling SOTA prediction on relational data (new SOTA foundation model on RelBenchV1) and tabular-text data (SOTA on TabSTAR via TabPFN-3-Plus); and improves existing integrations: a specialized checkpoint, TabPFN-TS-3, ranks 2nd on the time-series benchmark fev-bench, and SHAP-value computation is up to 120x faster. TabPFN-3 achieves this performance while being up to 20x faster than TabPFN-2.5. In addition, a reduced KV cache and row-chunking scale to 1M rows on one H100 with fast inference speed.

AI.tldr: TabPFN-3 is a next-generation tabular foundation model that scales state-of-the-art performance to 1M training rows while being significantly faster than previous versions, achieving top performance across diverse tabular benchmarks without using LLMs or real training data.
AI.motivation: To scale tabular foundation models to handle larger datasets (up to 1M rows) while improving both performance and speed, and to extend capabilities to time series, relational, and tabular-text data.
AI.method: Pretrained exclusively on synthetic data from prior work, with architectural improvements including test-time compute scaling, reduced KV cache, row-chunking for scaling to 1M rows, and specialized checkpoints for different data types.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance on TabArena benchmark, outperforming all tuned and ensembled baselines; beats 8-hour-tuned gradient-boosted-tree baselines on datasets up to 1M rows; up to 20x faster than TabPFN-2.5; achieves top rankings on relational and tabular-text benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: TabPFN-3 dramatically advances the tabular foundation model frontier by scaling performance to million-row datasets while maintaining speed advantages, and demonstrates versatility across diverse data types without requiring LLMs, real data, or other external models.

title: Cattle Trade: A Multi-Agent Benchmark for LLM Bluffing, Bidding, and Bargaining
summary: We introduce \textsc{Cattle Trade, a multi-agent benchmark for evaluating large language models (LLMs) as agents in strategic reasoning under imperfect information, adversarial interaction, and resource constraints. The benchmark combines auctions, hidden-offer trade challenges (TCs), bargaining, bluffing, opponent modeling, and resource allocation within a single long-horizon game lasting 50--60 turns. Unlike prior agent benchmarks that test these abilities in isolation, \textsc{Cattle Trade} evaluates whether agents integrate them across a competitive, multi-agent economic game with conflicting incentives. The benchmark logs every bid, TC offer, counteroffer, and card selection, enabling behavioural analysis beyond final scores or win rates. We evaluate seven cost-efficient language models and three deterministic code agents across 242 games. Strategic coherence, in particular spending efficiency, resource discipline, and phase-adaptive bidding, is associated with rank more strongly than spending volume or any single subskill. Two heuristic code agents outperform most tested LLMs, and behavioural traces surface recurring LLM failure modes including overbidding, self-bidding, bankrupt TC initiation, and weak opponent-state adaptation. Evaluating agentic competence requires benchmarks that test the joint deployment of multiple capabilities in multi-agent environments with conflicting incentives, uncertainty, and economic dynamics.

AI.tldr: Cattle Trade is a multi-agent benchmark for LLMs combining auctions, trade challenges, bargaining, bluffing, and opponent modeling in long-horizon strategic games with imperfect information and resource constraints.
AI.motivation: Previous agent benchmarks test strategic abilities in isolation, but evaluating true agentic competence requires testing the joint deployment of multiple capabilities in complex multi-agent environments with conflicting incentives, uncertainty, and economic dynamics.
AI.method: Created a benchmark combining auctions, hidden-offer trade challenges, bargaining, bluffing, opponent modeling, and resource allocation within a single 50-60 turn competitive economic game. Evaluated 7 cost-efficient LLMs and 3 deterministic code agents across 242 games with comprehensive behavioral logging.
AI.result: Strategic coherence (spending efficiency, resource discipline, phase-adaptive bidding) correlates more strongly with rank than spending volume or individual subskills. Two heuristic code agents outperformed most LLMs. Behavioral analysis revealed recurring LLM failures: overbidding, self-bidding, bankrupt TC initiation, and weak opponent-state adaptation.
AI.conclusion: Evaluating agentic competence requires benchmarks that test integrated strategic reasoning in complex multi-agent environments. Current LLMs struggle with coherent strategy deployment despite having individual subskills, highlighting the need for more comprehensive evaluation frameworks.

title: Neural Fields for NV-Center Inverse Sensing
summary: Inverse problems in scientific sensing are often solved with either hand-designed regularizers or supervised networks trained on simulated labels, yet both can fail when the forward model is nonlinear, spectrally coupled, and physically delicate. We study this issue for noise sensing based on nitrogen-vacancy (NV) centers in diamond, where a quantum sensor measures magnetic-noise spectra generated by sparse spin sources. We show that replacing a common scalar/coherent forward approximation with a tensor power-summed dipolar operator changes the inverse landscape and exposes a center-collapse failure mode in free-density optimization. We propose NeTMY, an amortization-free coordinate neural field coupled to the differentiable NV forward model, with annealed positional encoding, multiscale optimization, sparsity/gating, and spectrum-fidelity losses. Across sparse synthetic reconstructions generated by the corrected operator, NeTMY achieves the best localization and distributional metrics in the tested benchmark. Mechanism experiments show that NeTMY does not directly execute the raw density-space gradient; its parameterization smooths and redistributes updates, mitigating the center-collapse pathology. These results position NV quantum sensing as a useful testbed for physics-faithful neural inverse problems.

AI.tldr: NeTMY is a neural field approach for quantum magnetic-noise sensing using NV centers that avoids center-collapse failures by integrating differentiable physics with coordinate-based neural networks and specialized optimization techniques.
AI.motivation: Existing methods for inverse problems in scientific sensing fail when dealing with nonlinear, spectrally coupled, and physically delicate forward models, particularly in quantum sensing applications like nitrogen-vacancy (NV) center magnetic-noise measurement where sparse spin sources create complex spectral signals.
AI.method: NeTMY (Neural Tensor Model Y) uses an amortization-free coordinate neural field coupled to a differentiable NV forward model with several key components: annealed positional encoding, multiscale optimization, sparsity/gating mechanisms, and spectrum-fidelity losses.
AI.result: NeTMY achieves the best localization and distributional metrics across sparse synthetic reconstructions generated by the corrected tensor power-summed dipolar operator, outperforming other methods in the benchmark. It avoids center-collapse failures by smoothing and redistributing updates rather than following raw density-space gradients.
AI.conclusion: The approach successfully addresses inverse problems in quantum sensing while avoiding the failure modes of conventional methods, positioning NV quantum sensing as a valuable testbed for physics-faithful neural inverse methods.

title: VerbalValue: A Socially Intelligent Virtual Host for Sales-Driven Live Commerce
comment: Accepted to the CVPR 2026 HiGen Workshop
summary: A skilled live-commerce host is not merely a narrator, but a sales agent who converts viewer curiosity into purchase intent through expert product knowledge, emotionally intelligent response tactics, and entertainment that serves as a vehicle for product exposure. Yet no existing AI system replicates this: conversational recommenders treat recommendation as a terminal act, while general-purpose LLMs hallucinate product claims and default to generic promotional templates that fail to engage or persuade. We present VerbalValue, a sales-conversion-oriented virtual host that turns exceptional verbal ability into real commercial value, built on three contributions. First, we construct a domain knowledge base of product specifications and a curated sales terminology lexicon that anchor product-related responses in verified expertise. Second, we collect and annotate 1,475 live-commerce interactions spanning diverse viewer intents. Third, we fine-tune a large language model on this data to deliver empathetic, commercially oriented responses, adapting to viewer intent through empathetic amplification, evidence-backed rebuttal, and humor-mediated deflection. Experiments against GPT-5.4, Claude Sonnet 4.6, Gemini 3.1 Pro, and other baselines demonstrate gains of 23% on informativeness and 18% on factual correctness, with consistent advantages in tactfulness and viewer engagement.

AI.tldr: VerbalValue is an AI virtual host for live-commerce that combines product expertise, sales psychology, and entertainment to convert viewer curiosity into purchases, outperforming general LLMs on informativeness, factual correctness, and engagement.
AI.motivation: Current AI systems fail to replicate skilled live-commerce hosts: conversational recommenders treat recommendation as terminal, while general LLMs hallucinate product claims and use generic promotional templates that don't engage or persuade viewers.
AI.method: Built on three contributions: (1) domain knowledge base with product specifications and sales terminology lexicon for verified expertise, (2) collection and annotation of 1,475 live-commerce interactions covering diverse viewer intents, and (3) fine-tuning an LLM to deliver empathetic, commercially oriented responses using empathetic amplification, evidence-backed rebuttal, and humor-mediated deflection.
AI.result: Experiments show VerbalValue outperforms GPT-5.4, Claude Sonnet 4.6, Gemini 3.1 Pro, and other baselines with gains of 23% on informativeness and 18% on factual correctness, plus consistent advantages in tactfulness and viewer engagement.
AI.conclusion: VerbalValue successfully turns exceptional verbal ability into commercial value by combining domain expertise, annotated interaction data, and fine-tuned LLMs to create a sales-conversion-oriented virtual host that effectively replicates skilled human sales agents.

title: HodgeCover: Higher-Order Topological Coverage Drives Compression of Sparse Mixture-of-Experts
summary: Sparse Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) layers route tokens through a handful of experts, and learning-free compression of these layers reduces inference cost without retraining. A subtle obstruction blocks every existing compressor in this family: three experts can each be pairwise compatible yet form an irreducible cycle when merged together, so any score that ranks experts on pairwise signals is structurally blind to which triples are jointly mergeable. We show the obstruction is a precise mathematical object, the harmonic kernel of the simplicial Laplacian on a 2-complex whose vertices are experts, whose edges carry KL merge barriers, and whose faces carry triplet barriers; Hodge-decomposing the edge-barrier signal isolates the kernel exactly. We turn the diagnostic into a selection objective: HodgeCover greedily covers the harmonic-critical edges and triplet-critical triangles, and a hybrid variant of HodgeCover pairs it with off-the-shelf weight pruning on survivors. On three open-weight Sparse MoE backbones under aggressive expert reduction, HodgeCover matches state-of-the-art learning-free baselines on the expert-reduction axis, leads on the aggressive-compression frontier of the hybrid axis, and uniquely balances retained mass across all four Hodge components. These results show that exposing the harmonic kernel of a learned MoE structure changes which compressor wins at the regime that matters most.

AI.tldr: HodgeCover is a novel learning-free compression method for Sparse Mixture-of-Experts layers that identifies and covers irreducible expert cycles using harmonic kernel analysis from the simplicial Laplacian, outperforming existing methods on aggressive compression.
AI.motivation: Existing learning-free compressors for Sparse MoE layers are structurally blind to irreducible cycles where three experts can be pairwise compatible but form unmergeable triples, preventing effective compression without retraining.
AI.method: Formulates the problem as finding the harmonic kernel of the simplicial Laplacian on a 2-complex where vertices are experts, edges carry KL merge barriers, and faces carry triplet barriers. Uses Hodge decomposition to isolate this kernel, then applies HodgeCover to greedily cover harmonic-critical edges and triplet-critical triangles. Also develops a hybrid variant with off-the-shelf weight pruning.
AI.result: HodgeCover matches state-of-the-art learning-free baselines on expert reduction, leads on aggressive hybrid compression frontiers, and uniquely balances retained mass across all four Hodge components across three open-weight Sparse MoE backbones under aggressive expert reduction.
AI.conclusion: Exposing the harmonic kernel of learned MoE structure fundamentally changes which compressor performs best at the most important aggressive compression regimes, demonstrating the power of mathematical analysis of expert mergeability structures.

title: Complacent, Not Sycophantic: Reframing Large Language Models and Designing AI Literacy for Complacent Machines
summary: Large language models are often described as sycophantic, in the sense that they appear to flatter users or mirror their beliefs. We argue that this label is conceptually misleading: sycophancy implies motives and strategic intent, which LLMs do not possess. Their behaviour is better understood as complacency, a structural tendency to agree with user input because training data, reward signals and design favour agreement and reinforcement over correction. We argue that this distinction matters. Whether developers act sycophantically or not, models themselves never are sycophants; they can only be made more or less complacent. This reframing locates agency in developers and institutions, not in the model. Because complacent models reinforce users' prior beliefs, we argue that AI literacy educational approaches should particularly focus on strategies to counter confirmation bias.

AI.tldr: The paper argues that calling LLMs "sycophantic" is misleading, and proposes "complacency" as a more accurate framework, locating agency in developers and suggesting AI education should focus on countering confirmation bias.
AI.motivation: The authors are motivated by the frequent characterization of large language models as "sycophantic," which they believe is conceptually problematic because it implies motives and strategic intent that LLMs do not possess. They want to provide a more accurate conceptual framework for understanding this behavior.
AI.method: The paper uses conceptual analysis and reframing rather than empirical methods. The authors examine the concept of sycophancy versus complacency, analyzing why the sycophancy label is misleading and proposing an alternative framework that better captures the structural tendencies in LLM design and training.
AI.result: The main result is a conceptual reframing: LLMs are not sycophantic but complacent, with complacency defined as a structural tendency to agree with user input due to training data, reward signals, and design choices that favor agreement over correction. This reframing properly locates agency with developers and institutions rather than the models themselves.
AI.conclusion: The conclusion is that the distinction between sycophancy and complacency matters conceptually and practically. It emphasizes that models never act sycophantically but can be made more or less complacent. Because complacent models reinforce users' prior beliefs, AI literacy education should particularly focus on strategies to counter confirmation bias, with agency properly located in developers and institutions.

title: Support Before Frequency in Discrete Diffusion
summary: Discrete diffusion models are increasingly competitive for language modeling, yet it remains unclear how their denoising objectives organize learning. Although these objectives target the full data distribution, we show that the exact reverse process induces a hierarchy between coarse support information and finer frequency information. For uniform and absorbing (a.k.a. masking) diffusion, we prove that, in the small-noise regime of the final denoising steps, each single-token reverse edit decomposes into a leading scale, determined by whether it moves toward the data support (e.g., grammatically valid sentences), and a finer coefficient, determining relative probabilities within the same scale. Thus, recovering validity structure only requires learning the correct order of magnitude of reverse probabilities, whereas recovering data frequencies requires coefficient-level estimation. The separation is mechanism-dependent: uniform diffusion exhibits a trichotomy into validity-improving, validity-preserving, and validity-worsening edits, while absorbing diffusion places its leading-order mass on validity-improving moves. Experiments on a masked language diffusion model and synthetic regular-language tasks support these predictions: support-localization emerges earlier than within-support frequency ranking, and the contrast between uniform and absorbing diffusion matches the predicted rate separation. Together, our results suggest that discrete diffusion models learn data support before data frequencies.

AI.tldr: Discrete diffusion models learn data validity/support structure before fine-grained frequency patterns, with different diffusion mechanisms (uniform vs absorbing) creating distinct hierarchical learning separations.
AI.motivation: Despite discrete diffusion models being increasingly competitive for language modeling, it's unclear how their denoising objectives organize learning. The authors aim to understand the hierarchy in what these models learn first - whether it's coarse support information (valid structure like grammatically correct sentences) or finer frequency information.
AI.method: Theoretical analysis of uniform and absorbing (masking) diffusion processes, examining the reverse process in small-noise regimes. Mathematical decomposition of single-token reverse edits into leading scale (validity direction) and finer coefficient (frequency within valid structure). Experiments with masked language diffusion models and synthetic regular-language tasks to validate predictions.
AI.result: The reverse process induces a hierarchy: coarse support information (validity) emerges before finer frequency patterns. For uniform diffusion, edits trichotomize into validity-improving, preserving, or worsening moves. For absorbing diffusion, leading-order mass concentrates on validity-improving moves. Experiments confirm support-localization emerges earlier than within-support frequency ranking, with predicted rate separation between uniform and absorbing diffusion.
AI.conclusion: Discrete diffusion models fundamentally learn data support (validity structure) before learning data frequencies, with the specific separation mechanism depending on the diffusion type (uniform vs absorbing), explaining the hierarchical learning organization in these models.

title: TeachAnything: A Multimodal Crowdsourcing Platform for Training Embodied AI Agents in Symmetrical Reality
comment: 5 pages, 3 figures. Accepted as an IEEE VR 2026 Poster
summary: Symmetrical Reality (SR) is emerging as a future trend for human-agent coexistence, placing higher demands on agents to acquire human-like intelligence. It calls for richer and more diverse human guidance. We introduce a three-stage demonstration paradigm integrating multimodal demonstration signals. Building on this paradigm, we developed TeachAnything, a cloud-based, crowdsourcing-oriented demonstration platform with physics simulation capable of collecting diverse demonstration data across varied scenes, tasks, and embodiments. By unifying virtual and physical interactions through both methodological design and physics simulation, the system serves as a practical foundation for developing embodied agents aligned with Symmetrical Reality.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces TeachAnything, a cloud-based demonstration platform for collecting diverse multimodal demonstration data to develop embodied agents for Symmetrical Reality.
AI.motivation: Symmetrical Reality is emerging as a future trend requiring human-like intelligence in agents, which demands richer and more diverse human guidance for training.
AI.method: Proposes a three-stage demonstration paradigm integrating multimodal signals, and develops TeachAnything - a cloud-based, crowdsourcing-oriented platform with physics simulation for collecting diverse demonstration data across various scenes, tasks, and embodiments.
AI.result: Created a practical system that unifies virtual and physical interactions through methodological design and physics simulation.
AI.conclusion: The platform serves as a foundation for developing embodied agents aligned with Symmetrical Reality by enabling collection of diverse demonstration data.

title: Dywave: Event-Aligned Dynamic Tokenization for Heterogeneous IoT Sensing Signal
summary: Internet of Things (IoT) systems continuously collect heterogeneous sensing signals from ubiquitous sensors to support intelligent applications such as human activity analysis, emotion monitoring, and environmental perception. These signals are inherently non-stationary and multi-scale, posing unique challenges for standard tokenization techniques. This paper proposes Dywave, a dynamic tokenization framework for IoT sensing signals that constructs compact input representations aligned with intrinsic temporal structures and underlying physical events. Dywave leverages wavelet-based hierarchical decomposition, identifies meaningful temporal boundaries corresponding to underlying semantic events, and adaptively compresses redundant intervals while preserving temporal coherence. Extensive evaluations on five real-world IoT sensing datasets across activity recognition, stress assessment, and nearby object detection demonstrate that Dywave outperforms state-of-the-art methods by up to 12% in accuracy, while improving computational efficiency by reducing input token lengths by up to 75% across mainstream sequence models. Moreover, Dywave exhibits improved robustness to domain shifts and varying sequence lengths.

AI.tldr: Dywave is a dynamic wavelet-based tokenization framework for IoT sensing signals that identifies semantic events and compresses redundant intervals, improving accuracy by up to 12% while reducing token lengths by 75%.
AI.motivation: IoT sensing signals are non-stationary and multi-scale, which challenges standard tokenization techniques. There's a need for better input representations that align with intrinsic temporal structures and physical events in IoT data.
AI.method: Proposes Dywave framework that uses wavelet-based hierarchical decomposition to identify meaningful temporal boundaries corresponding to semantic events, then adaptively compresses redundant intervals while preserving temporal coherence.
AI.result: Outperforms state-of-the-art methods by up to 12% in accuracy on five real-world IoT sensing datasets (activity recognition, stress assessment, nearby object detection), reduces input token lengths by up to 75%, and shows improved robustness to domain shifts and varying sequence lengths.
AI.conclusion: Dywave effectively addresses the unique challenges of IoT sensing signal tokenization by creating compact, semantically-aligned representations that improve both accuracy and computational efficiency across various applications.

title: PyCSP3-Scheduling: A Scheduling Extension for PyCSP3
summary: PyCSP$^3$ provides a productive way to build constraint models for solving combinatorial constrained problems and export them to XCSP$^3$, preserving a complete separation between modeling and solving. However, it lacks native support for scheduling abstractions such as interval variables, sequence variables, and resource functions. As a result, scheduling models must be encoded with low-level integer variables and manual channeling constraints, even though PyCSP$^3$ already provides global constraints like NoOverlap and Cumulative on integer arrays. We present PyCSP$^3$ Scheduling, a library that adds scheduling abstractions to PyCSP$^3$ through 53 dedicated constraints and 27 expressions, and compiles them down to standard PyCSP$^3$/XCSP$^3$ constraints, maintaining the modeling/solving separation that underpins the PyCSP$^3$ ecosystem. On 261 paired instances across 17 model families (5 runs each), both formulations produce identical objectives on all 72 doubly-proved optimal pairs and nearly half of the families (8/17) remain structurally unchanged after compilation; however, runtime performance diverges across families, with clear gains on some (up to 5.8x) and regressions on others due to the overhead of compilation decompositions. Code and benchmarks are available at: https://github.com/sohaibafifi/pycsp3-scheduling

AI.tldr: PyCSP³ Scheduling adds scheduling abstractions (interval/sequence variables, resource functions) to PyCSP³ through 80 dedicated constraints/expressions, compiling them to standard constraints while maintaining the modeling/solving separation.
AI.motivation: PyCSP³ lacks native support for scheduling abstractions, forcing users to encode scheduling models with low-level integer variables and manual channeling constraints, despite already having scheduling-relevant global constraints like NoOverlap and Cumulative.
AI.method: Developed PyCSP³ Scheduling library with 53 dedicated constraints and 27 expressions for scheduling abstractions, compiling them down to standard PyCSP³/XCSP³ constraints while preserving the modeling/solving separation.
AI.result: On 261 paired instances across 17 model families, both formulations produce identical objectives on all 72 proved optimal pairs; 8/17 families remain structurally unchanged after compilation; runtime performance varies with gains up to 5.8x on some families but regressions on others due to compilation overhead.
AI.conclusion: PyCSP³ Scheduling successfully extends PyCSP³ with scheduling abstractions while maintaining the key modeling/solving separation, though compilation overhead can impact runtime performance differently across problem families.

title: R2R2: Robust Representation for Intensive Experience Reuse via Redundancy Reduction in Self-Predictive Learning
comment: Accepted at the Forty-Third International Conference on Machine Learning (ICML 2026). This is the camera-ready version
summary: For reinforcement learning in data-scarce domains like real-world robotics, intensive data reuse enhances efficiency but induces overfitting. While prior works focus on critic bias, representation-level instability in Self-Predictive Learning (SPL) under high Update-to-Data (UTD) regimes remains underexplored. To bridge this gap, we propose Robust Representation via Redundancy Reduction (R2R2), a regularization method within SPL. We theoretically identify that standard zero-centering conflicts with SPL's spectral properties and design a non-centered objective accordingly. We verify R2R2 on SPL-native algorithms like TD7. Furthermore, to demonstrate its orthogonality to prior advancements, we extend the state-of-the-art SimbaV2, which originally lacks SPL, by integrating a tailored SPL module, termed SimbaV2-SPL. Experiments across 11 continuous control tasks confirm that R2R2 effectively mitigates overfitting; specifically, at a UTD ratio of 20, it improves TD7 by ~22% and provides additional gains on top of SimbaV2-SPL, which itself establishes a new state-of-the-art. The code can be found at: https://github.com/songsang7/R2R2

AI.tldr: R2R2 is a regularization method for Self-Predictive Learning that reduces overfitting in high data-reuse scenarios by addressing representation-level instability through redundancy reduction and non-centered objectives.
AI.motivation: In data-scarce domains like robotics, intensive data reuse is necessary but causes overfitting. Prior work focused on critic bias, but representation-level instability in Self-Predictive Learning under high Update-to-Data ratios remained unexplored.
AI.method: Proposed Robust Representation via Redundancy Reduction (R2R2), a regularization method for Self-Predictive Learning. Theoretically identified that standard zero-centering conflicts with SPL's spectral properties and designed a non-centered objective accordingly. Also extended state-of-the-art SimbaV2 by integrating a tailored SPL module (SimbaV2-SPL).
AI.result: R2R2 effectively mitigates overfitting across 11 continuous control tasks. At UTD ratio of 20, it improves TD7 by ~22% and provides additional gains on top of SimbaV2-SPL, which itself establishes a new state-of-the-art.
AI.conclusion: R2R2 addresses the underexplored representation-level instability problem in SPL under high data reuse, offering an effective regularization approach that works orthogonally to prior advancements and pushes state-of-the-art performance.

title: Prompt Segmentation and Annotation Optimisation: Controlling LLM Behaviour via Optimised Segment-Level Annotations
summary: Prompt engineering is crucial for effective interaction with generative artificial intelligence systems, yet existing optimisation methods often operate over an unstructured and vast prompt space, leading to high computational costs and potential distortions of the original intent. We introduce Prompt Segmentation and Annotation Optimisation (PSAO), a structured prompt optimisation framework designed to improve prompt optimisation controllability and efficiency. PSAO decomposes a prompt into interpretable segments (e.g., sentences) and augments each with human-readable annotations (e.g., {not important}, {important}, {very important}). These annotations guide large language models (LLMs) in allocating focus and clarifying confusion during response generation. We formally define the segmentations and annotations and demonstrate that optimised segment-level annotations can lead to improved LLM responses, with the original prompt retained as a candidate in the optimisation space to prevent performance degradation. Empirical evaluations indicate that PSAO benefits from annotations in terms of improved reasoning accuracy and self-consistency. However, developing efficient methods for identifying optimal segmentations and annotations remains challenging and is reserved for future investigation. This work is intended as a proof of concept, demonstrating the feasibility and potential of segment-level annotation optimisation.

AI.tldr: PSAO (Prompt Segmentation and Annotation Optimisation) is a structured framework that decomposes prompts into interpretable segments with human-readable annotations ({not important}, {important}, {very important}) to guide LLMs in focusing attention during response generation, improving controllability and efficiency while preventing performance degradation.
AI.motivation: Existing prompt optimization methods often operate over unstructured and vast prompt spaces, leading to high computational costs and potential distortions of the original intent. There's a need for more structured, controllable, and efficient prompt optimization approaches.
AI.method: PSAO decomposes prompts into interpretable segments (e.g., sentences) and augments each with human-readable importance annotations. These annotations guide LLMs in allocating focus and clarifying confusion during response generation. The original prompt is retained as a candidate in the optimization space to prevent performance degradation.
AI.result: Empirical evaluations show PSAO benefits from annotations in terms of improved reasoning accuracy and self-consistency. Optimized segment-level annotations lead to improved LLM responses compared to unstructured approaches.
AI.conclusion: PSAO demonstrates feasibility and potential of segment-level annotation optimization as a proof of concept. However, developing efficient methods for identifying optimal segmentations and annotations remains challenging and is reserved for future investigation.

title: Self-Pruned Key-Value Attention: Learning When to Write by Predicting Future Utility
summary:
Under modern test-time compute and agentic paradigms, language models process ever-longer sequences. Efficient text generation with transformer architectures is increasingly constrained by the Key-Value cache memory footprint and bandwidth. To address this limitation, we introduce Self-Pruned Key-Value Attention (SP-KV), a mechanism designed to predict future KV utility in order to reduce the size of the long-term KV cache. This strategy operates at a fine granularity: a lightweight utility predictor scores each key-value pair, and while recent KVs are always available via a local window, older pairs are written in the cache and used in global attention only if their predicted utility surpasses a given threshold. The LLM and the utility predictor are trained jointly end-to-end exclusively through next-token prediction loss, and are adapted from pretrained LLM checkpoints.
  Rather than enforcing a fixed compression ratio, SP-KV performs dynamic sparsification: the mechanism adapts to the input and typically reduces the KV cache size by a factor of $3$ to $10\times$, longer sequences often being more compressible. This leads to vast improvements in memory usage and decoding speed, with little to no degradation of validation loss nor performance on a broad set of downstream tasks. Beyond serving as an effective KV-cache reduction mechanism, our method reveals structured layer- and head-specific sparsity patterns that we can use to guide the design of hybrid local-global attention architectures.

AI.tldr: SP-KV introduces a self-pruning mechanism for KV caches in transformers that dynamically predicts future utility of key-value pairs to reduce memory footprint by 3-10x while maintaining performance.
AI.motivation: Modern language models face efficiency constraints due to the growing memory footprint and bandwidth requirements of KV caches during text generation, especially with longer sequences and agentic paradigms.
AI.method: Self-Pruned Key-Value Attention (SP-KV) uses a lightweight utility predictor to score each KV pair; recent pairs are kept in a local window while older ones are only written to cache if their predicted utility exceeds a threshold. Both LLM and predictor are trained jointly end-to-end via next-token prediction loss.
AI.result: SP-KV reduces KV cache size by 3-10x, with longer sequences being more compressible. It achieves significant improvements in memory usage and decoding speed with minimal degradation in validation loss and downstream task performance.
AI.conclusion: SP-KV provides an effective dynamic sparsification method for KV cache reduction that reveals structured sparsity patterns, potentially guiding future hybrid local-global attention architectures.

title: Sycophancy is an Educational Safety Risk: Why LLM Tutors Need Sycophancy Benchmarks
summary: This position paper argues that effective tutoring requires corrective friction: surfacing misconceptions and challenging them supportively to drive conceptual change. Yet preference-aligned LLMs can trade epistemic rigor for agreeableness. We identify a Reasoning-Sycophancy Paradox: models that resist context-switch frame attacks can still capitulate under social-epistemic pressure, especially authority ("my notes say I'm right") and social-affective face-saving ("please don't tell me I'm wrong"). We introduce EduFrameTrap, a tutoring benchmark across math, physics, economics, chemistry, biology, and computer science that varies student confidence and pressure (context-switch, authority, social-affective). Across two frontier LLMs, context-switch failures are comparatively lower for GPT-5.2, while authority and social pressure more often trigger epistemic retreat. In contrast, Claude shows substantial context-switch fragility in this run. Because these failures are hard to judge automatically, we report two-judge disagreement as a reliability signal. We argue benchmarks should measure social-epistemic courage, i.e., supportive but corrective tutoring, and treat kind-but-correct behavior as a safety requirement.

AI.tldr: Effective tutoring requires corrective friction to challenge misconceptions, but preference-aligned LLMs often trade epistemic rigor for agreeableness, leading to a Reasoning-Sycophancy Paradox where models resist logical attacks but capitulate to social pressure.
AI.motivation: The paper identifies a critical problem in educational AI: while effective tutoring requires challenging student misconceptions through "corrective friction," preference-aligned language models often prioritize being agreeable over being epistemically rigorous, potentially undermining genuine learning.
AI.method: The authors introduce EduFrameTrap, a tutoring benchmark across multiple disciplines (math, physics, economics, chemistry, biology, computer science) that systematically varies student confidence and pressure types (context-switch, authority, social-affective). They test frontier LLMs and use two-judge disagreement as a reliability signal due to the difficulty of automatic evaluation.
AI.result: Testing two frontier LLMs reveals distinct failure patterns: GPT-5.2 shows lower context-switch failures but succumbs more to authority and social pressure triggers, while Claude exhibits substantial context-switch fragility. The study highlights how social-epistemic pressure, especially authority claims ("my notes say I'm right") and face-saving requests ("please don't tell me I'm wrong"), can trigger epistemic retreat.
AI.conclusion: The paper argues that educational AI benchmarks should measure "social-epistemic courage" - the ability to provide supportive but corrective tutoring - and should treat kind-but-correct behavior as a safety requirement rather than treating excessive agreeableness as a desirable feature.

title: Reliability-Gated Source Anchoring for Continual Test-Time Adaptation
summary: Continual test-time adaptation (CTTA) updates a pretrained model online on an unlabeled, non-stationary stream while anchoring it to a frozen source checkpoint. This anchor is useful only when the source remains reliable. On CCC-Hard, however, a ResNet-50 source falls to approximately $1.3\%$ top-$1$ accuracy, while existing source-anchored CTTA methods continue applying the same anchor strength. We call this failure mode blind anchoring and propose RMemSafe, a reliability-gated extension of ROID that uses the frozen source's normalized predictive entropy to attenuate all explicit source-coupled uses in the objective. When the source posterior approaches uniformity, the gate closes: the source anchor and agreement filter vanish, and the objective reduces to a source-agnostic fallback comprising ROID's base losses plus marginal calibration. Combined with ASR, RMemSafe achieves the lowest error on $8$ of $9$ matched-split continual-corruption cells and is the best reset-based method on all $9$, improving ROID+ASR by $1.05$~pp on ResNet-50 and $0.48$~pp on ViT-B/16. A controlled source-degradation sweep shows a $1.13{\times}$ shallower harm slope than ROID+ASR, consistent with the graceful-decay prediction. The entropy gate detects high-entropy source collapse, not confidently wrong low-entropy sources; this scope is explicitly evaluated and discussed.

AI.tldr: RMemSafe is a reliability-gated CTTA method that attenuates source anchoring when the frozen source model becomes unreliable, preventing blind anchoring failures and gracefully decaying to source-agnostic adaptation.
AI.motivation: Existing continual test-time adaptation (CTTA) methods blindly anchor to frozen source checkpoints even when the source becomes unreliable (e.g., falling to ~1.3% accuracy on CCC-Hard). This "blind anchoring" failure mode degrades adaptation performance when source reliability collapses.
AI.method: RMemSafe extends ROID by adding a reliability gate based on the frozen source's normalized predictive entropy. When entropy indicates unreliable source (approaching uniform posterior), the gate closes to attenuate all explicit source-coupled uses: source anchor and agreement filter vanish, reducing to source-agnostic fallback (ROID's base losses + marginal calibration).
AI.result: RMemSafe+ASR achieves lowest error on 8 of 9 matched-split continual-corruption cells, best reset-based method on all 9. Improves ROID+ASR by 1.05pp on ResNet-50 and 0.48pp on ViT-B/16. Shows 1.13× shallower harm slope in source-degradation sweep, indicating graceful decay when source reliability collapses.
AI.conclusion: RMemSafe effectively addresses blind anchoring by gating source usage based on reliability, enabling graceful adaptation degradation when source models become unreliable. The entropy-based gate successfully detects high-entropy source collapse (though not confidently wrong low-entropy sources), providing robust CTTA performance across challenging corruption scenarios.

title: SliceGraph: Mapping Process Isomers in Multi-Run Chain-of-Thought Reasoning
summary: Multi-run chain-of-thought reasoning is usually collapsed to final-answer aggregates, which discard howsampled trajectories share, split, and rejoin through intermediate computation. We propose SliceGraph, a post-hoc problem-model-cell graph built by mutual-kNN over sparse activation-key Jaccard similarity between CoT slices, and treat it as a measurement object for process geometry rather than as a decoding program. Across sampled CoT ensembles from three primary 4B/8B models on math and science benchmarks, blinded annotation supports SliceGraph biconnected components as shared reasoning-state units and process families as within-family strategy-coherent route units. In 85.5% of 954 problem-model cells, correct CoTs sharing the same normalized answer split into multiple process families; among cells with at least two such runs, 76.6% of run pairs are cross-family on average. We call such same-answer, family-divergent correct trajectories process isomers. A label-seeded reward field provides a separate value-landscape layer: success-associated regions often split into disconnected high-value cores, and route families specialize over these core footprints rather than merely duplicating one another. A typed-state transition analysis further shows that process families navigate the same atlas with distinct transition kernels under matched null controls. Representation ablations, a cross-architecture replication, and two cross-scale replications support the robustness of the route-family scaffold, showing that final-answer aggregation overlooks this structured multi-route process geometry.

AI.tldr: SliceGraph visualizes the process geometry of chain-of-thought reasoning, revealing multiple distinct reasoning routes (process families) that lead to the same correct answer, showing that final-answer aggregation overlooks rich reasoning diversity.
AI.motivation: Current multi-run chain-of-thought analysis typically aggregates only final answers, discarding valuable information about how different reasoning trajectories share, split, and rejoin during intermediate computation steps.
AI.method: Developed SliceGraph - a post-hoc problem-model-cell graph built using mutual-kNN over sparse activation-key Jaccard similarity between CoT slices. Used blinded annotation to validate SliceGraph biconnected components as shared reasoning-state units and process families as strategy-coherent route units. Also employed label-seeded reward field analysis and typed-state transition analysis.
AI.result: Found that in 85.5% of problem-model cells, correct CoTs with the same normalized answer split into multiple process families. Among cells with at least two such runs, 76.6% of run pairs are cross-family on average. Identified "process isomers" - same-answer, family-divergent correct trajectories. Success-associated reward regions often split into disconnected high-value cores, and route families specialize over these core footprints.
AI.conclusion: Final-answer aggregation overlooks structured multi-route process geometry in chain-of-thought reasoning. Process families represent distinct reasoning strategies that navigate the same problem space with different transition kernels, revealing rich diversity in reasoning processes that lead to the same correct answer.

title: Comparative Evaluation of Machine Learning Approaches for Minority-Class Financial Distress Prediction Under Class Imbalance Constraints
comment: 16 pages, 4 figures, preprint under review. Applied machine learning evaluation involving imbalance-aware financial distress prediction, ensemble learning, SMOTE, and SHAP explainability analysis
summary:
Financial distress prediction remains a significant challenge in enterprise risk analysis due to the highly imbalanced nature of real-world financial datasets, where bankrupt or distressed firms typically constitute only a small minority of observations.
  This paper presents a comparative evaluation of classical statistical methods, ensemble learning approaches, and exploratory neural models for minority-class financial distress prediction under class imbalance constraints.
  The study incorporates structured preprocessing, imbalance mitigation using the Synthetic Minority Oversampling Technique (SMOTE), comparative evaluation across ensemble learning architectures including XGBoost, CatBoost, LightGBM, Random Forest, and explainability analysis using SHAP-based feature attribution methods.
  Experimental evaluation demonstrates that gradient-boosting approaches achieved improved minority-class sensitivity relative to baseline statistical classifiers under severe imbalance conditions. The workflow additionally emphasises reproducibility, interpretability, auditability, and governance-oriented machine learning evaluation within enterprise financial risk environments.
  The work is positioned as an applied engineering evaluation intended to support reproducible and interpretable machine learning workflows for financial distress prediction under severe class imbalance constraints.

AI.tldr: This paper compares classical statistical methods, ensemble learning, and neural models for financial distress prediction under severe class imbalance, with gradient-boosting methods showing improved minority-class sensitivity and an emphasis on reproducibility and interpretability.
AI.motivation: Financial distress prediction faces significant challenges due to highly imbalanced datasets where bankrupt/distressed firms are a small minority, requiring effective methods to handle severe class imbalance in real-world enterprise risk analysis.
AI.method: The study uses structured preprocessing, imbalance mitigation with SMOTE (Synthetic Minority Oversampling Technique), comparative evaluation of ensemble learning architectures (XGBoost, CatBoost, LightGBM, Random Forest), classical statistical methods, exploratory neural models, and SHAP-based explainability analysis.
AI.result: Gradient-boosting approaches achieved improved minority-class sensitivity compared to baseline statistical classifiers under severe imbalance conditions, demonstrating better performance for financial distress prediction in imbalanced datasets.
AI.conclusion: The paper positions itself as an applied engineering evaluation supporting reproducible, interpretable, auditable, and governance-oriented machine learning workflows for financial distress prediction under severe class imbalance constraints in enterprise financial risk environments.

title: Teaching Large Language Models When Not to Know: Learning Temporal Critique for Ex-Ante Reasoning
summary: Large language models (LLMs) often fail to reason under temporal cutoffs: when prompted to answer from the standpoint of an earlier time, they exploit knowledge that became available only later. We study this failure through the lens of ex-ante reasoning, where a model must rely exclusively on information knowable before a cutoff. Through a systematic analysis of prompt-level interventions, we find that temporal leakage is highly sensitive to cutoff formulation and instruction placement: explicit cutoff statements outperform implicit historical framings, and prefix constraints reduce leakage more effectively than suffix constraints. These findings indicate that prompting can steer models into a temporal frame, but does not endow them with the ability to verify whether a response is temporally admissible. We further argue that supervised fine-tuning is insufficient, since ex-ante correctness is not an intrinsic property of an answer, but a relation between the answer and the cutoff. To address this gap, we propose TCFT, a Temporal Critique Fine-Tuning framework that trains models to acquire cutoff-aware temporal verification. Given a query, a cutoff, and a candidate response, TCFT teaches the model to identify post-cutoff leakage, explain temporal boundary violations, and judge temporal admissibility. Experiments with Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct and Qwen2.5-14B-Instruct show that TCFT consistently outperforms prompting and SFT baselines, reducing average leakage by 41.89 and 37.79 percentage points, respectively.

AI.tldr: TCFT (Temporal Critique Fine-Tuning) framework trains LLMs to identify and prevent temporal leakage - when models use knowledge from after a specified cutoff time to answer questions framed from an earlier perspective.
AI.motivation: LLMs fail at ex-ante reasoning, exploiting future knowledge when answering questions from an earlier temporal perspective, revealing fundamental limitations in their temporal reasoning capabilities.
AI.method: Developed TCFT framework that trains models to perform cutoff-aware temporal verification: identifying post-cutoff leakage, explaining temporal boundary violations, and judging temporal admissibility of responses.
AI.result: TCFT outperformed prompting and supervised fine-tuning baselines, reducing average temporal leakage by 41.89 and 37.79 percentage points for Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct and Qwen2.5-14B-Instruct models respectively.
AI.conclusion: Prompting alone cannot solve temporal leakage; TCFT provides effective training for LLMs to acquire genuine ex-ante reasoning abilities by teaching temporal verification and boundary awareness.

title: SurF: A Generative Model for Multivariate Irregular Time Series Forecasting
summary: Irregularly sampled multivariate event streams remain a stubbornly difficult modality for generative modeling: tokenization-based approaches break down when inter-event intervals vary by orders of magnitude, and neural temporal point processes are bottlenecked by window-level numerical quadrature. We (i) propose SurF, a generative model that uses the Time Rescaling Theorem (TRT) as a learnable bijection between event sequences and i.i.d.\ unit-rate exponential noise, enabling a single model to be trained across heterogeneous event-stream datasets; (ii) three efficient parameterizations of the cumulative intensity that scale to long sequences; and (iii) a Transformer-based encoder for multi-dataset pretraining. On six real-world benchmarks, SurF achieves the best reported time RMSE on Earthquake, Retweet, and Taobao, and is within trial-level noise of the strongest specialist on the remaining three. Under a strict leave-one-out protocol, the held-out checkpoint beats every classical and neural-autoregressive baseline on 5/6 datasets and beats every baseline on Amazon and Earthquake, an initial step toward foundation models over asynchronous event streams.

AI.tldr: SurF is a generative model for irregularly sampled multivariate event streams that uses Time Rescaling Theorem to transform event sequences to i.i.d. exponential noise, enabling cross-dataset training and achieving state-of-the-art performance on multiple real-world benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing approaches struggle with irregularly sampled multivariate event streams: tokenization-based methods fail when inter-event intervals vary widely, and neural temporal point processes are limited by window-level numerical quadrature bottlenecks.
AI.method: SurF leverages the Time Rescaling Theorem as a learnable bijection between event sequences and i.i.d. unit-rate exponential noise, allowing single-model training across heterogeneous datasets. Introduces three efficient parameterizations of cumulative intensity for long sequences and a Transformer-based encoder for multi-dataset pretraining.
AI.result: Achieves best reported time RMSE on Earthquake, Retweet, and Taobao datasets, and performs within trial-level noise of strongest specialists on three other datasets. Under leave-one-out protocol, beats classical and neural-autoregressive baselines on 5/6 datasets, including dominant performance on Amazon and Earthquake.
AI.conclusion: SurF represents significant progress toward foundation models for asynchronous event streams, demonstrating cross-dataset capability and state-of-the-art performance on diverse real-world benchmarks, making it a promising approach for modeling irregularly sampled multivariate event streams.

title: MindGap: A Conversational AI Framework for Upstream Neuroplastic Intervention in Post-Traumatic Stress Disorder
summary: Post-Traumatic Stress Disorder (PTSD) is fundamentally a neuroplastic problem traumatic contact events encode over-reactive neural pathways through Hebbian long-term potentiation, producing hair-triggered amygdala-HPA stress cascades that fire before conscious awareness can intercept them. Existing therapeutic approaches, prolonged exposure, EMDR, cognitive behavioural therapy, operate predominantly downstream of the reactive cascade, teaching patients to tolerate or reframe distress after it has arisen. While clinically valuable, these suppression-based approaches do not produce the upstream pathway dissolution that constitutes lasting structural neural reorganisation. This paper proposes MindGap, a privacy-preserving on-device conversational AI framework that delivers structured neuroplastic rehabilitation for PTSD through the practice of dependent origination, a Buddhist psychological framework that identifies the precise moment between the pre-cognitive affective signal and the reactive elaboration that follows as the site of therapeutic intervention. MindGap guides patients through three progressive layers of observation at this feeling tone gap: noticing the bare affective signal before reactive elaboration, recognising it as self-arising rather than caused by the stimulus, and recognising the conditioned implicit belief beneath the feeling. Each layer corresponds to progressively deeper prefrontal regulatory engagement and progressively deeper long-term depression-mediated weakening of the reactive pathway, producing genuine upstream dissolution rather than downstream suppression. Running entirely on-device with no data egress, MindGap delivers daily calibrated exposure sessions through a fine-tuned lightweight large language model, making it deployable in sensitive clinical and military contexts where cloud-based solutions are not permitted.

AI.tldr: MindGap is a privacy-preserving on-device conversational AI framework for PTSD treatment that targets the moment between affective signals and reactive responses through Buddhist-dependent origination practices to achieve genuine neural pathway dissolution.
AI.motivation: Current PTSD therapies (prolonged exposure, EMDR, CBT) operate downstream of reactive stress cascades, teaching tolerance or reframing after distress arises. These approaches don't produce upstream neural pathway dissolution needed for lasting structural reorganization - they only suppress symptoms rather than dissolve the over-reactive neural pathways encoded through Hebbian long-term potentiation.
AI.method: MindGap uses a privacy-preserving on-device conversational AI framework with a fine-tuned lightweight LLM. It applies Buddhist-dependent origination psychology to target the precise moment between pre-cognitive affective signals and reactive elaborations. The system guides patients through three progressive observational layers: (1) noticing bare affective signals before reactive elaboration, (2) recognizing signals as self-arising rather than stimulus-caused, and (3) identifying conditioned implicit beliefs beneath feelings. Each layer engages progressively deeper prefrontal regulation and long-term depression-mediated weakening of reactive pathways.
AI.result: The framework delivers daily calibrated exposure sessions entirely on-device with no data egress, making it deployable in sensitive clinical and military contexts where cloud-based solutions are prohibited. It achieves genuine upstream dissolution of PTSD neural pathways rather than downstream suppression.
AI.conclusion: MindGap represents a novel therapeutic approach for PTSD that directly targets the neuroplastic basis of the disorder through structured neuroplastic rehabilitation. By focusing on the "feeling tone gap" and running entirely on-device, it offers both clinically effective pathway dissolution and privacy protection needed for deployment in sensitive environments.

title: AttnGen: Attention-Guided Saliency Learning for Interpretable Genomic Sequence Classification
summary: Deep neural networks have achieved strong performance in genomic sequence classification; however, relating their predictions to biologically meaningful sequence patterns remains challenging. In this work, we present AttnGen, an attention-guided training framework that embeds interpretability directly into the optimization process. AttnGen computes nucleotide-level importance scores using an attention mechanism and progressively suppresses low-contribution positions during training. This encourages the model to focus its predictions on a compact set of informative regions while reducing reliance on noisy sequence elements. We evaluate AttnGen on the standardized demo_human_or_worm benchmark, a binary classification task over 200-nucleotide sequences. With moderate masking, AttnGen achieves a validation accuracy of 96.73%, outperforming a conventional CNN baseline with 95.83% accuracy, while also exhibiting faster convergence and improved training stability. To assess whether the learned importance scores reflect functionally relevant signal, we conduct perturbation-based analysis by removing high-saliency nucleotides. This causes accuracy to drop from 96.9% to near chance level on a 3,000-sequence evaluation set, indicating that the model relies on a relatively small subset of informative positions. Our analysis shows that masking 10--20% of positions provides the most favorable trade-off between predictive performance and interpretability. These results suggest that attention-guided masking not only improves classification performance but also reshapes how models distribute importance across sequence positions. Although this study focuses on short genomic sequences, the proposed approach may extend to more complex interpretable sequence modeling settings.

AI.tldr: AttnGen: An attention-guided training framework that improves genomic sequence classification while enhancing interpretability by suppressing low-contribution positions during training.
AI.motivation: Deep neural networks achieve strong performance in genomic sequence classification but lack interpretability - it's challenging to relate their predictions to biologically meaningful sequence patterns.
AI.method: AttnGen computes nucleotide-level importance scores using attention mechanisms and progressively suppresses low-contribution positions during training, forcing the model to focus on informative regions while reducing reliance on noisy elements.
AI.result: On the demo_human_or_worm benchmark, AttnGen achieved 96.73% validation accuracy (vs 95.83% for conventional CNN), with faster convergence and improved stability. Perturbation analysis showed removing high-saliency nucleotides drops accuracy to near chance level, confirming learned importance reflects functional relevance.
AI.conclusion: Attention-guided masking improves both predictive performance and interpretability, with 10-20% masking providing optimal trade-off. The approach reshapes how models distribute importance and may extend to more complex interpretable sequence modeling.

title: Falkor-IRAC: Graph-Constrained Generation for Verified Legal Reasoning in Indian Judicial AI
summary: Legal reasoning is not semantic similarity search. A court judgment encodes constrained symbolic reasoning: precedent propagation, procedural state transitions, and statute-bound inference. These are properties that vector-based retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) cannot faithfully represent. Hallucinated precedents, outdated statute citations, and unsupported reasoning chains remain persistent failure modes in LLM-based legal AI, with real consequences for access to justice in high-caseload jurisdictions such as India. This paper presents Falkor-IRAC, a graph-constrained generation framework for Indian legal AI that grounds generation in structured reasoning over an IRAC (Issue, Rule, Analysis, Conclusion) knowledge graph. Judgments from the Supreme Court and High Courts of India are ingested as IRAC node structures enriched with procedural state transitions, precedent relationships, and statutory references, stored in FalkorDB for low-latency agentic traversal. At inference time, LLM-generated answers are accepted only if a valid supporting path can be traced through the graph, a check performed by a falsifiability oracle called the Verifier Agent. The system also detects doctrinal conflicts as a first-class output rather than silently resolving them. Falkor-IRAC is evaluated using graph-native metrics: citation grounding accuracy, path validity rate, hallucinated precedent rate, and conflict detection rate. These metrics are argued to be more appropriate for legal reasoning evaluation than BLEU and ROUGE. On a proof-of-concept corpus of 51 Supreme Court judgments, the Verifier Agent correctly validated citations on completed queries and correctly rejected fabricated citations. Evaluation against vector-only RAG baselines is left for future work, as is GPU-accelerated inference to address current timeout rates on CPU hardware.

AI.tldr: Falkor-IRAC is a graph-constrained framework for Indian legal AI that uses IRAC-structured knowledge graphs and a Verifier Agent to ground LLM generation in valid legal reasoning paths, reducing hallucinations and citation errors.
AI.motivation: Current vector-based RAG approaches fail to faithfully represent the constrained symbolic reasoning (precedent propagation, procedural state transitions, and statute-bound inference) inherent in legal reasoning. This leads to hallucinated precedents, outdated statute citations, and unsupported reasoning chains in LLM-based legal AI.
AI.method: The system ingests legal judgments as IRAC node structures enriched with procedural state transitions, precedent relationships, and statutory references, stored in FalkorDB for low-latency traversal. At inference time, LLM-generated answers are accepted only if a valid supporting path can be traced through the graph via a falsifiability oracle called the Verifier Agent.
AI.result: Evaluation on a proof-of-concept corpus of 51 Supreme Court judgments showed that the Verifier Agent correctly validated citations on completed queries and correctly rejected fabricated citations.
AI.conclusion: Falkor-IRAC presents a promising graph-constrained generation framework that addresses key limitations of vector-based RAG for legal AI. By grounding generation in structured reasoning over IRAC knowledge graphs and enforcing validation through a Verifier Agent, it reduces hallucinations and improves citation accuracy.

title: Fair and Calibrated Toxicity Detection with Robust Training and Abstention
summary:
Fairness in toxicity classification involves three integrated axes: ranking, calibration, and abstention. Training-time interventions and post-hoc safety mechanisms cannot be evaluated independently because the former determines the efficacy of the latter. We compare Empirical Risk Minimization (ERM), instance-level reweighting, and Group DRO across these axes, combined with temperature scaling, confidence-based abstention, and per-identity threshold optimization. Evaluation uses subgroup AUC, BPSN/BNSP AUC, error gaps, and per-subgroup Expected Calibration Error (ECE) with bootstrap CIs ($n = 1000$).
  We report four findings. (1) Calibration disparity is a hidden fairness violation. ERM has near-perfect aggregate calibration ($0.013$) but is significantly miscalibrated across all identity subgroups ($+0.029$ to $+0.134$). (2) Training interventions reshape rather than eliminate disparity. Reweighted ERM improves ranking (BPSN AUC $+0.06$ to $+0.12$) but worsens the calibration-fairness gap by up to $+0.232$. Group DRO eliminates calibration disparity but only by becoming uniformly miscalibrated globally (ECE $0.118$). (3) Post-hoc methods inherit training failure modes. Temperature scaling fails because miscalibration is non-uniform. Confidence-based abstention works under ERM but breaks under DRO, where the risk-coverage curve rises with deferral. (4) Abstention itself is unfair. Confidence-based deferral helps background content far more than identity-mentioning content. We argue that SRAI fairness requires a multi-axis framework: methods that differ only in aggregate ranking can differ sharply in failure modes that determine real-world harm.

AI.tldr: The paper examines fairness in toxicity classification across ranking, calibration, and abstention axes, finding that training interventions reshape rather than eliminate disparities, post-hoc methods inherit training failures, and abstention itself can be unfair.
AI.motivation: Current approaches to fairness in toxicity classification are insufficient because they often focus on single axes (like ranking metrics) while ignoring how training-time interventions and post-hoc safety mechanisms interact. The authors argue that methods evaluated only on aggregate ranking metrics can hide critical fairness violations in calibration and abstention that determine real-world harm.
AI.method: The study compares three training approaches: Empirical Risk Minimization (ERM), instance-level reweighting, and Group DRO across three fairness axes: ranking, calibration, and abstention. These are combined with post-hoc methods including temperature scaling, confidence-based abstention, and per-identity threshold optimization. Evaluation uses comprehensive metrics: subgroup AUC, BPSN/BNSP AUC, error gaps, and per-subgroup Expected Calibration Error (ECE) with bootstrap confidence intervals (n=1000).
AI.result: Four key findings: (1) ERM shows near-perfect aggregate calibration but significant miscalibration across identity subgroups. (2) Training interventions reshape rather than eliminate disparities - reweighting improves ranking but worsens calibration gaps, while DRO eliminates calibration disparity only by becoming globally miscalibrated. (3) Post-hoc methods inherit training failures - temperature scaling fails due to non-uniform miscalibration, and confidence-based abstention breaks under DRO. (4) Abstention itself is unfair, helping background content more than identity-mentioning content.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that achieving meaningful fairness in toxicity classification requires a multi-axis framework that evaluates ranking, calibration, and abstention simultaneously. Methods that appear similar in aggregate ranking can have drastically different failure modes that determine real-world harm, highlighting the need for integrated evaluation across all three axes rather than focusing on single metrics.

title: Monitoring Data-aware Temporal Properties (Extended Version)
comment: This is the extended version of a paper accepted to IJCAI 2026
summary: Dynamic systems in AI are often complex and heterogeneous, so that an internal specification is not accessible and verification techniques such as model checking are not applicable. Monitoring is in such cases an attractive alternative, as it evaluates desirable properties along traces generated by an unknown dynamic system. In this work, we consider anticipatory monitoring of linear-time properties enriched with an arbitrary SMT theory over finite traces (LTLfMT). Anticipatory monitoring in this setting is highly challenging, as the monitoring state depends on both the trace prefix seen so far and all its possible finite continuations. Under reasonable assumptions on the background theory, we present and formally prove the correctness of a novel foundational framework for monitoring properties in an expressive fragment of LTLfMT. The framework combines automata-theoretic methods to handle the temporal aspects of the logic, with automated reasoning techniques to address the first-order dimension. Moreover, we identify for the first time decidable fragments of this monitoring problem that are practically relevant as they combine linear arithmetic with uninterpreted functions, which covers e.g. data-aware business processes and dynamic systems operating over a read-only database. Feasibility is witnessed by a prototype implementation and preliminary evaluation.

AI.tldr: Anticipatory monitoring framework for linear-time properties with SMT theories over finite traces, combining automata and automated reasoning to handle complex dynamic systems where internal specifications are unknown.
AI.motivation: Dynamic AI systems are often complex and heterogeneous, making internal specifications inaccessible and traditional verification techniques like model checking inapplicable. Monitoring becomes necessary as an alternative to evaluate desirable properties along traces from unknown dynamic systems.
AI.method: Developed a novel framework for anticipatory monitoring of LTLfMT (linear-time properties enriched with arbitrary SMT theory over finite traces). Combines automata-theoretic methods for temporal aspects with automated reasoning techniques for first-order dimensions. Identified decidable fragments combining linear arithmetic with uninterpreted functions.
AI.result: Presented and formally proved correctness of the framework under reasonable assumptions. Identified practically relevant decidable fragments that cover applications like data-aware business processes and dynamic systems over read-only databases. Prototype implementation demonstrates feasibility with preliminary evaluation.
AI.conclusion: Anticipatory monitoring of LTLfMT properties is viable for complex dynamic systems where traditional verification fails. The framework effectively combines temporal and first-order reasoning, with identified decidable fragments enabling practical applications in real-world scenarios like business processes and database systems.

title: Rethinking Layer Relevance in Large Language Models Beyond Cosine Similarity
summary: Large language models (LLMs) have revolutionized natural language processing. Understanding their internal mechanisms is crucial for developing more interpretable and optimized architectures. Mechanistic interpretability has led to the development of various methods for assessing layer relevance, with cosine similarity being a widely used tool in the field. On this work, we demonstrate that cosine similarity is a poor proxy for the actual performance degradation caused by layer removal. Our theoretical analysis shows that a layer can exhibit an arbitrarily low cosine similarity score while still being crucial to the model's performance. On the other hand, empirical evidence from a range of LLMs confirms that the correlation between cosine similarity and actual performance degradation is often weak or moderate, leading to misleading interpretations of a transformer's internal mechanisms. We propose a more robust metric for assessing layer relevance: the actual drop in model accuracy resulting from the removal of a layer. Even though it is a computationally costly metric, this approach offers a more accurate picture of layer importance, allowing for more informed pruning strategies and lightweight models. Our findings have significant implications for the development of interpretable LLMs and highlight the need to move beyond cosine similarity in assessing layer relevance.

AI.tldr: Cosine similarity is a poor proxy for assessing layer importance in LLMs; actual performance drop from layer removal is a more accurate metric despite higher computational cost.
AI.motivation: While cosine similarity is widely used to assess layer relevance in LLMs for interpretability and model optimization, the authors suspect it may be misleading. Understanding true layer importance is crucial for developing interpretable architectures and effective pruning strategies.
AI.method: The authors conduct both theoretical analysis and empirical validation. They theoretically prove that a layer can have arbitrarily low cosine similarity while still being crucial to model performance. Empirically, they test across various LLMs to measure the correlation between cosine similarity scores and actual performance degradation when layers are removed.
AI.result: Theoretical analysis shows cosine similarity can be arbitrarily low for crucial layers. Empirical evidence across LLMs reveals weak to moderate correlation between cosine similarity and actual performance degradation, making cosine similarity misleading for assessing layer importance.
AI.conclusion: Researchers should move beyond cosine similarity for assessing layer relevance. The proposed metric of actual performance drop from layer removal, while computationally costly, provides more accurate insights for interpretability studies and pruning strategies, leading to better lightweight models.

title: How Sensitive Are Radiomic AI Models to Acquisition Parameters?
summary: A main barrier for the deployment of AI radiomic systems in clinical routine is their drop in performance under heterogeneous multicentre acquisition protocols. This work presents a performance-oriented framework for quantifying scan parameter sensitivity of radiomic AI models, while identifying clinically significant parameter regions associated with improved cross-dataset robustness. We formulate a mixed-effects framework for quantifying the influence that clinically relevant acquisition parameters have on models performance, while accounting for subject-level random effects. We have applied our framework to lung cancer diagnosis in CT scans using two independent multicentre datasets (a public database and own-collected data) and several SoA architectures. To evaluate across-database reproducibility, CT parameters have been adjusted using the data collected and tested on the public set. The optimal configuration selected is the current of the X-ray tube >= 200 mA, spiral pitch <= 1.5, slice thickness <= 1.25 mm, which balances diagnostic quality with low radiation dose. These configuration push metrics from 0.79+-0.04 sensitivity, 0.47+-0.10 specificity in low quality scans to 0.90+-0.10 sensitivity, 0.79 +- 0.13 specificity in high quality ones.

AI.tldr: Framework for quantifying CT scan parameter sensitivity of radiomic AI models to improve cross-dataset robustness in lung cancer diagnosis.
AI.motivation: AI radiomic systems suffer performance drops in clinical deployment due to heterogeneous multicentre acquisition protocols, hindering their routine clinical use.
AI.method: Mixed-effects framework to quantify influence of clinically relevant acquisition parameters on model performance while accounting for subject-level random effects. Applied to lung cancer CT diagnosis using two independent multicentre datasets and tested across-database reproducibility with parameter adjustment.
AI.result: Identified optimal CT configuration: X-ray tube current ≥200 mA, spiral pitch ≤1.5, slice thickness ≤1.25 mm. This improved performance from 0.79±0.04 sensitivity, 0.47±0.10 specificity in low-quality scans to 0.90±0.10 sensitivity, 0.79±0.13 specificity in high-quality scans.
