AI.tldr: This survey paper introduces the LIFE framework to analyze LLM-based multi-agent systems through four causally linked stages: capability foundation, agent integration, fault attribution, and autonomous self-improvement.
AI.motivation: LLM-based multi-agent systems face challenges with error propagation and lack structural self-improvement mechanisms. Existing research treats agent capabilities, collaboration, and evolution separately without examining their causal dependencies.
AI.method: The survey organizes research around the LIFE progression framework: 1) Lay capability foundation, 2) Integrate agents through collaboration, 3) Find faults through attribution, and 4) Evolve through autonomous self-improvement. It provides systematic taxonomies and formal characterizations of dependencies between stages.
AI.result: The survey synthesizes existing work, identifies challenges at stage boundaries, and proposes a cross-stage research agenda for closed-loop multi-agent systems capable of continuous failure diagnosis, structural reorganization, and agent refinement.
AI.conclusion: The LIFE framework bridges fragmented research threads and provides both a systematic reference and conceptual roadmap toward autonomous, self-improving multi-agent intelligence that extends current coordination frameworks toward more self-organizing collective intelligence.

authors: Francisco Nattero Santiago Mauricio Barron Bucolo
title: Active Learners as Efficient PRP Rerankers
summary: Pairwise Ranking Prompting (PRP) elicits pairwise preference judgments from an LLM, which are then aggregated into a ranking, usually via classical sorting algorithms. However, judgments are noisy, order-sensitive, and sometimes intransitive, so sorting assumptions do not match the setting. Because sorting aims to recover a full permutation, truncating it to meet a call budget does not produce a dependable top-K. We thus reframe PRP reranking as active learning from noisy pairwise comparisons and show that active rankers are drop-in replacements that improve NDCG@10 per call in the call-constrained regime. Our noise-robust framework also introduces a randomized-direction oracle that uses a single LLM call per pair. This approach converts systematic position bias into zero-mean noise, enabling unbiased aggregate ranking without the cost of bidirectional calls.

AI.tldr: Active rankers replace PRP's noisy pairwise judgments with active learning, improving top-K ranking quality under call constraints using a randomized-direction oracle for unbiased comparisons.
AI.motivation: Standard Pairwise Ranking Prompting (PRP) suffers from noisy, order-sensitive, and sometimes intransitive LLM judgments, and classical sorting algorithms don't handle this noise well. When constrained by limited LLM calls, truncating full permutations doesn't produce reliable top-K rankings.
AI.method: Reframe PRP reranking as active learning from noisy pairwise comparisons. Use active rankers as drop-in replacements for sorting algorithms. Introduce a randomized-direction oracle that makes a single LLM call per pair (instead of bidirectional calls) and converts systematic position bias into zero-mean noise.
AI.result: Active rankers improve NDCG@10 per call in the call-constrained regime. The randomized-direction oracle enables unbiased aggregate ranking without the cost of bidirectional calls.
AI.conclusion: A noise-robust active learning framework outperforms traditional PRP approaches by better handling noisy pairwise comparisons and call constraints, with a randomized-direction oracle providing an efficient, unbiased alternative to bidirectional calling.

title: COREKG: Coreset-Guided Personalized Summarization of Knowledge Graphs
summary:
Knowledge Graphs (KGs) are extensively used across different domains and in several applications. Often, these KGs are very large in size. Such KGs become unwieldy for tasks such as question answering and visualization. Summarization of KGs offers a viable alternative in such cases. Furthermore, personalized KG summarization is crucial in the current data-driven world as it captures the specific requirements of users based on their query patterns. Since it only maintains relevant information, the personalized summaries of KG are small, resulting in significantly smaller storage requirements and query runtime. In this work, we adapt the coreset theory to create personalized KG summaries. For a given dataset and a user-specific query workload, we present an approach that samples a relevant subset of triples using sensitivity-based importance sampling. We ensure that the subset approximates the characteristics of the full dataset with bounded approximation error. We define sensitivity scores that measure the importance of a triple with respect to a user's query workload, which are then used by our coreset construction algorithm. We explicitly focus on personalized knowledge graph summarization by constructing summaries independently for each user based on their query behaviour.
  Our evaluation on Freebase, WikiData, and DBpedia shows that COREKG delivers higher query-answering accuracy and structural coverage than the state-of-the-art methods, such as GLIMPSE, PPR, iSummary, PEGASUS and APEX$^2$ while requiring only a tiny fraction of the original graph.

AI.tldr: COREKG uses coreset theory to create personalized knowledge graph summaries by sampling relevant triples based on user query workloads, achieving better query accuracy with small summaries.
AI.motivation: Large knowledge graphs are unwieldy for tasks like question answering and visualization, and personalized summarization is needed to capture specific user requirements based on their query patterns.
AI.method: Adapt coreset theory with sensitivity-based importance sampling to select relevant triples, defining sensitivity scores that measure triple importance relative to user query workloads.
AI.result: COREKG outperforms state-of-the-art methods (GLIMPSE, PPR, iSummary, PEGASUS, APEX²) on Freebase, WikiData, and DBpedia with higher query-answering accuracy and structural coverage using only a tiny fraction of the original graph.
AI.conclusion: Personalized KG summarization using coreset theory effectively creates compact, user-specific summaries that maintain query accuracy while reducing storage and runtime requirements.

title: Paraphrasing Attack Resilience of Various AI-Generated Text Detection Methods
summary: The recent large-scale emergence of LLMs has left an open space for dealing with their consequences, such as plagiarism or the spread of false information on the Internet. Coupling this with the rise of AI detector bypassing tools, reliable machine-generated text detection is in increasingly high demand. We investigate the paraphrasing attack resilience of various machine-generated text detection methods, evaluating three approaches: fine-tuned RoBERTa, Binoculars, and text feature analysis, along with their ensembles using Random Forest classifiers. We discovered that Binoculars-inclusive ensembles yield the strongest results, but they also suffer the most significant losses during attacks. In this paper, we present the dichotomy of performance versus resilience in the world of AI text detection, which complicates the current perception of reliability among state-of-the-art techniques.

AI.tldr: Current AI text detection methods struggle with paraphrasing attacks - while ensemble methods with Binoculars perform best, they're also most vulnerable to attacks, revealing a performance vs. resilience tradeoff.
AI.motivation: The paper addresses the growing problem of detecting machine-generated text due to LLM proliferation and rising concerns about plagiarism/false information, compounded by the availability of AI detector bypassing tools. There's an urgent need for reliable machine-generated text detection that can withstand attacks.
AI.method: The researchers investigated paraphrasing attack resilience using three approaches: fine-tuned RoBERTa, Binoculars, and text feature analysis. They also evaluated ensemble methods combining these approaches using Random Forest classifiers. The study specifically examined how these methods perform when subjected to paraphrasing attacks.
AI.result: Binoculars-inclusive ensembles achieved the strongest detection results, but suffered the most significant performance degradation during paraphrasing attacks. This revealed a fundamental tradeoff: methods that perform best in normal conditions are most vulnerable to attacks, while more robust methods may have lower baseline performance.
AI.conclusion: Current state-of-the-art AI text detection methods face a performance-versus-resilience dichotomy that complicates their perceived reliability. The best-performing methods under normal conditions are paradoxically the most vulnerable to adversarial attacks, highlighting a critical challenge in developing truly robust detection systems.

title: KGPFN: Unlocking the Potential of Knowledge Graph Foundation Model via In-Context Learning
summary: Knowledge graph (KG) foundation models aim to generalize across graphs with unseen entities and relations by learning transferable relational structure. However, most existing methods primarily emphasize relation-level universality, while in-context learning, the other pillar of foundation models remains under-explored for KG reasoning. In KGs, context is inherently structured and heterogeneous: effective prediction requires conditioning on the local context around the query entities as well as the global context that summarizes how a relation behaves across many instances. We propose KGPFN, a KG foundation model using Prior-data Fitted Network that unifies transferable relational regularities with inference-time in-context learning from structured context. KGPFN first learns relation representations via message passing on relation graphs to capture cross-graph relational invariances. For query-specific reasoning, it encodes local neighborhoods using a multi-layer NBFNet as local context. To enable ICL at global scale, it constructs relation-specific global context by retrieving a large set of instances of the query relation together with their local neighborhoods, and aggregates them within a Prior-Data Fitted Network framework that combines feature-level and sample-level attention. Through multi-graph pretraining on diverse KGs, KGPFN learns when to instantiate reusable patterns and when to override them using contextual evidence. Experiments on 57 KG benchmarks demonstrate that KGPFN achieves strong adaptation to previously unseen graphs through in-context learning alone, consistently outperforming competitive fine-tuned KG foundation models. Our code is available at https://github.com/HKUST-KnowComp/KGPFN.

AI.tldr: KGPFN is a KG foundation model that combines transferable relational patterns with structured in-context learning, outperforming existing methods on 57 benchmarks through inference-time adaptation alone.
AI.motivation: Most existing KG foundation models focus on relation-level universality while neglecting in-context learning, which is crucial for effective KG reasoning as context in KGs is inherently structured and heterogeneous. Effective prediction requires conditioning on both local context around query entities and global context summarizing how relations behave across instances.
AI.method: KGPFN uses a Prior-data Fitted Network to unify transferable relational regularities with inference-time in-context learning. It learns relation representations via message passing on relation graphs, encodes local neighborhoods with multi-layer NBFNet, constructs relation-specific global context by retrieving large sets of relation instances, and aggregates them using feature-level and sample-level attention within the P-FN framework.
AI.result: Experiments on 57 KG benchmarks demonstrate that KGPFN achieves strong adaptation to previously unseen graphs through in-context learning alone, consistently outperforming competitive fine-tuned KG foundation models.
AI.conclusion: KGPFN demonstrates the effectiveness of combining transferable relational regularities with structured in-context learning for KG foundation models, showing strong adaptation to unseen graphs through in-context learning alone and outperforming fine-tuned competitors.

title: Latency-Quality Routing for Functionally Equivalent Tools in LLM Agents
summary: Tool-augmented LLM agents increasingly access the same tool type through multiple functionally equivalent providers, such as web-search APIs, retrievers, or LLM backends exposed behind a shared interface. This creates a provider-routing problem under runtime load: the router must choose among providers that differ in latency, reliability, and answer quality, often without gold labels at deployment time. We introduce LQM-ContextRoute, a contextual bandit router for same-function tool providers. Its key design is latency-quality matching: instead of letting low latency offset poor answers in an additive reward, the router ranks providers by expected answer quality per service cycle. It combines this capacity-aware score with query-specific quality estimation and LLM-as-judge feedback, allowing it to adapt online to both load changes and provider-quality differences. On the main web-search load benchmark, LQM-ContextRoute improves F1 by +2.18 pp over SW-UCB while staying on the latency-quality frontier. In a high-heterogeneity StrategyQA setting, LQM-ContextRoute avoids additive-reward collapse and improves accuracy by up to +18 pp over SW-UCB; on heterogeneous retriever pools, it improves NDCG by +2.91--+3.22 pp over SW-UCB. These results show that same-function tool routing benefits from treating latency as service capacity, especially when runtime pressure and provider-quality heterogeneity coexist.

AI.tldr: LQM-ContextRoute is a contextual bandit router for same-function tool providers that uses latency-quality matching to rank providers by expected answer quality per service cycle, outperforming SW-UCB on web-search load, StrategyQA, and heterogeneous retriever pools.
AI.motivation: Tool-augmented LLM agents increasingly access functionally equivalent tool providers (web-search APIs, retrievers, LLM backends) that differ in latency, reliability, and quality. This creates a provider-routing problem under runtime load where routers must choose among providers without gold labels at deployment time.
AI.method: LQM-ContextRoute uses latency-quality matching: instead of additive reward combining latency and quality, it ranks providers by expected answer quality per service cycle. It combines capacity-aware scoring with query-specific quality estimation and LLM-as-judge feedback to adapt online to load changes and provider-quality differences.
AI.result: On web-search load benchmark: +2.18 pp F1 improvement over SW-UCB while staying on latency-quality frontier. On StrategyQA with high heterogeneity: up to +18 pp accuracy improvement over SW-UCB. On heterogeneous retriever pools: +2.91-+3.22 pp NDCG improvement over SW-UCB.
AI.conclusion: Same-function tool routing benefits from treating latency as service capacity rather than letting low latency offset poor answers. This approach is especially effective when runtime pressure and provider-quality heterogeneity coexist.

title: From Sycophantic Consensus to Pluralistic Repair: Why AI Alignment Must Surface Disagreement
summary: Pluralistic alignment is typically operationalised as preference aggregation: producing responses that span (Overton), steer toward (Steerable), or proportionally represent (Distributional) diverse human values. We argue that aggregation alone is an incomplete primitive for deployed pluralistic alignment. Under genuine value pluralism, the failure mode of contemporary RLHF-trained assistants is not insufficient coverage but sycophantic consensus: a learned tendency to agree with, validate, and minimise friction with the immediate interlocutor. Because deployed AI systems now mediate consequential deliberation across health, civic life, labour, and governance, the collapse of disagreement at the interaction layer is not a narrow technical concern but a structural failure with distributive consequences. We reframe pluralistic alignment around three conversational mechanisms drawn from Grice's maxims: scoping (acknowledging the limits of one's perspective), signalling (surfacing value-conflict rather than smoothing it over), and repair (revising one's position on principled grounds, not on user pressure). We formalise a metric, the Pluralistic Repair Score (PRS), distinguishing principled revision from capitulation, and present a small-scale empirical illustration on two frontier RLHF-trained models (Claude Sonnet 4.5, N=198; GPT-4o, N=100) showing that, for both, agreement-following coexists with low repair-quality on contested-value prompts. PRS measures an interactional precondition for pluralism (visible disagreement; principled revision) rather than pluralism in full; we discuss the difference, take seriously the reflexive question of whose "principled" counts, and argue that pluralism is most decisively made or unmade at the deployment-governance layer: interfaces, preference-data pipelines, and audit infrastructure.

AI.tldr: The paper reframes pluralistic AI alignment from preference aggregation to conversational mechanisms that preserve visible disagreement and principled revision, identifying sycophantic consensus as the key failure mode in deployed RLHF-trained assistants.
AI.motivation: Current RLHF-trained AI assistants exhibit sycophantic consensus - they tend to agree with, validate, and minimize friction with users rather than preserving genuine value pluralism. This collapse of disagreement at the interaction layer has significant distributive consequences as AI systems mediate deliberation in health, civic life, labor, and governance.
AI.method: The paper proposes reframing pluralistic alignment around three conversational mechanisms from Grice's maxims: scoping (acknowledging perspective limits), signalling (surfacing value conflict), and repair (principled revision). It introduces the Pluralistic Repair Score (PRS) metric to distinguish principled revision from capitulation, and provides empirical evaluation on Claude Sonnet 4.5 (N=198) and GPT-4o (N=100).
AI.result: The empirical study shows that both frontier RLHF-trained models exhibit agreement-following behavior alongside low repair-quality on contested-value prompts, demonstrating that current systems fail to maintain principled disagreement.
AI.conclusion: Pluralistic alignment requires more than preference aggregation - it needs conversational mechanisms that preserve visible disagreement and principled revision. The PRS metric captures interactional preconditions for pluralism, but ultimately pluralism is determined at deployment-governance layers including interfaces, preference-data pipelines, and audit infrastructure.

title: Artificial Intelligence-Assistant Cardiotocography: Unified Model for Signal Reconstruction, Fetal Heart Rate Analysis, and Variability Assessment
summary: The monitoring of fetal heart rate (FHR) and the assessment of its variability are crucial for preventing fetal compromise and adverse outcomes. However, traditional methods encounter limitations arising from equipment performance, data transmission, and subjective assessments by doctors. We have developed a tailored AI-based FHrCTG model specifically for FHR monitoring, which effectively mitigates noise interference and precisely reconstructs signals. Our model was pre-trained on a massive dataset consisting of 558,412 unlabeled data points and further refined using 7,266 expert-reviewed entries. To validate FHR, we introduced the Intersection Overlapping Labels (IOL) approach, which transforms rate analysis into categorical judgments. Testing revealed that our model demonstrates high sensitivity and specificity in detecting critical FHR decelerations (89.13% and 87.78%, respectively) and accelerations (62.5% and 92.04%, respectively). Furthermore, based on Fischer's criteria for clinical application, our model achieved impressive AUC scores of 0.7214 and 0.9643 for verifying FHR periodicity and amplitude variation, respectively.

AI.tldr: An AI-based model for fetal heart rate monitoring that uses massive pre-training and expert refinement to accurately detect FHR patterns and variations, outperforming traditional methods.
AI.motivation: Traditional fetal heart rate (FHR) monitoring faces limitations due to equipment performance issues, data transmission problems, and subjective doctor assessments, which can compromise fetal safety and lead to adverse outcomes.
AI.method: Developed a tailored AI-based FHrCTG model with two-phase training: pre-training on 558,412 unlabeled data points followed by refinement using 7,266 expert-reviewed entries. Introduced the Intersection Overlapping Labels (IOL) approach to transform rate analysis into categorical judgments for FHR validation.
AI.result: The model achieved high sensitivity and specificity for detecting critical FHR decelerations (89.13%/87.78%) and accelerations (62.5%/92.04%). Based on Fischer's criteria, it achieved AUC scores of 0.7214 for verifying FHR periodicity and 0.9643 for amplitude variation.
AI.conclusion: The AI-based FHrCTG model effectively addresses limitations of traditional FHR monitoring by mitigating noise interference and precisely reconstructing signals, demonstrating strong clinical applicability for fetal health assessment.

title: GraphFlow: An Architecture for Formally Verifiable Visual Workflows Enabling Reliable Agentic AI Automation
summary:
GraphFlow is a visual workflow system designed to improve the reliability of agentic AI automation in multi-step, mission-critical processes. In these workflows, small errors compound rapidly: under an idealized model of independent steps, a ten-step process with 90% per-step reliability completes successfully only 35% of the time. Existing workflow platforms provide durable execution and observability but offer few semantic correctness guarantees, while agentic systems plan at inference time, making behavior sensitive to prompt variation and difficult to audit.
  GraphFlow is designed to address this gap by treating workflow diagrams as the executable specification, a single artifact defining data scope, execution semantics, and monitoring. At compile time, a restricted class of diagrams is specified to produce reusable automations whose contracts (preconditions, postconditions, and composition obligations) are intended to be proof-checked before admission to a shared library. At runtime, a durable engine records outcomes in an append-only event log and can enforce contracts at system boundaries, supporting replay, retries, and audit. Swimlanes make trust boundaries explicit, separating verified logic from external systems, human judgment, and AI decisions.
  A year-long pilot across three clinical sites executed 8,728 cohort-enrolled workflow runs with a 97.08% completion rate under an early prototype without the verified-core subsystem; observed failures were localized primarily to external integrations. The formal semantics and proof-checked admission model described here are specified and under active development. Evaluation of the verified core is reserved for future work.

AI.tldr: GraphFlow is a visual workflow system that improves reliability of agentic AI automation through compile-time verification and explicit trust boundaries, achieving 97% completion rate in clinical trials.
AI.motivation: Existing workflow platforms lack semantic correctness guarantees, and agentic systems are sensitive to prompt variation and difficult to audit, leading to compounding errors in multi-step critical processes where small errors rapidly reduce overall reliability.
AI.method: Treats workflow diagrams as executable specifications with compile-time verification of preconditions, postconditions, and composition obligations. Uses durable runtime engine with append-only event logs, explicit swimlanes for trust boundaries, and formal semantics for proof-checked admission to shared library.
AI.result: Year-long pilot across three clinical sites executed 8,728 workflow runs with 97.08% completion rate using early prototype. Formal semantics and proof-checked admission model specified and under development, with verified core evaluation reserved for future work.
AI.conclusion: GraphFlow addresses reliability gap in agentic AI workflows through verified specifications and explicit trust boundaries, showing promising results in clinical applications. Formal verification approach offers path to more reliable mission-critical automation systems.

title: Action-Conditioned Risk Gating for Safety-Critical Control under Partial Observability
summary: Many safety-critical control problems are modeled as risk-sensitive partially observable Markov decision processes, where the controller must make decisions from incomplete observations while balancing task performance against safety risk. Although belief-space planning provides a principled solution, maintaining and planning over beliefs can be computationally costly and sensitive to model specification in practical domains. We propose a lightweight risk-gated reinforcement learning approximation for risk-sensitive control under partial observability. The method constructs a compact finite-history proxy state and learns an action-conditioned predictor of near-term safety violation. This predicted candidate-action risk is used in two complementary ways: as a risk penalty during value learning, and as a decision-time gate that interpolates between optimistic and conservative ensemble value estimates. As a result, low-risk actions are evaluated closer to reward-seeking estimates, while high-risk actions are evaluated more conservatively. We evaluate the approach in two safety-critical partially observable domains: automated glucose regulation and safety-constrained navigation. Across adult and adolescent glucose-control cohorts, the method improves overall glycemic tradeoffs and substantially reduces runtime relative to a belief-space planning baseline. On Safety-Gym navigation benchmarks, it achieves a more favorable reward-cost balance than unconstrained RL and several standard safe-RL baselines. These results suggest that action-conditioned near-term risk can provide an effective local signal for approximate risk-sensitive POMDP control when full belief-space planning is impractical.

AI.tldr: A lightweight reinforcement learning method that uses action-conditioned near-term risk prediction for safety-critical control under partial observability, avoiding costly belief-space planning while maintaining risk sensitivity.
AI.motivation: Risk-sensitive partially observable Markov decision processes (POMDPs) are essential for safety-critical control, but belief-space planning is computationally expensive and sensitive to model specification, making it impractical for real-world applications. There's a need for a more lightweight approach that can handle partial observability while balancing task performance and safety risk.
AI.method: The method constructs a compact finite-history proxy state and learns an action-conditioned predictor of near-term safety violation. The predicted risk is used in two ways: 1) as a risk penalty during value learning, and 2) as a decision-time gate that interpolates between optimistic and conservative ensemble value estimates, allowing low-risk actions to be evaluated closer to reward-seeking estimates while high-risk actions are evaluated more conservatively.
AI.result: The approach was evaluated in two safety-critical partially observable domains: automated glucose regulation and safety-constrained navigation. For glucose control, it improved glycemic tradeoffs and substantially reduced runtime compared to belief-space planning baselines. For Safety-Gym navigation benchmarks, it achieved a better reward-cost balance than unconstrained RL and several standard safe-RL baselines.
AI.conclusion: Action-conditioned near-term risk prediction provides an effective local signal for approximate risk-sensitive POMDP control when full belief-space planning is impractical, offering a lightweight alternative that maintains safety while improving computational efficiency.

title: Explainable Detection of Depression Status Shifts from User Digital Traces
summary: Every day, users generate digital traces (e.g., social media posts, chats, and online interactions) that are inherently timestamped and may reflect aspects of their mental state. These traces can be organized into temporal trajectories that capture how a user's mental health signals evolve, including phases of improvement, deterioration, or stability. In this work, we propose an explainable framework for detecting and analyzing depression-related status shifts in user digital traces. The approach combines multiple BERT-based models to extract complementary signals across different dimensions (e.g., sentiment, emotion, and depression severity). Such signals are then aggregated over time to construct user-level trajectories that are analyzed to identify meaningful change points. To enhance interpretability, the framework integrates a large language model to generate concise and human-readable reports that describe the evolution of mental-health signals and highlight key transitions. We evaluate the framework on two social media datasets. Results show that the approach produces more coherent and informative summaries than direct LLM-based reporting, achieving higher coverage of user history, stronger temporal coherence, and improved sensitivity to change points. An ablation study confirms the contribution of each component, particularly temporal modeling and segmentation. Overall, the method provides an interpretable view of mental health signals over time, supporting research and decision making without aiming at clinical diagnosis.

AI.tldr: A framework combining BERT-based models and LLMs to detect and explain depression-related shifts in users' digital traces over time, creating interpretable mental health trajectories from social media data.
AI.motivation: Users generate timestamped digital traces (social media posts, chats, online interactions) that may reflect their mental state. These can be organized into temporal trajectories showing mental health evolution (improvement, deterioration, stability), but need explainable methods to detect and analyze depression-related status shifts.
AI.method: Combines multiple BERT-based models to extract complementary signals across dimensions (sentiment, emotion, depression severity). Aggregates signals over time to construct user-level trajectories and identify change points. Integrates an LLM to generate concise, human-readable reports describing mental health signal evolution and highlighting key transitions.
AI.result: Evaluation on two social media datasets shows the approach produces more coherent and informative summaries than direct LLM-based reporting, achieving higher coverage of user history, stronger temporal coherence, and improved sensitivity to change points. Ablation study confirms contributions of temporal modeling and segmentation components.
AI.conclusion: The method provides an interpretable view of mental health signals over time, supporting research and decision making without aiming at clinical diagnosis. The framework effectively combines BERT-based signal extraction with LLM-based explanation for analyzing depression-related shifts in digital traces.

title: EnergyLens: Predictive Energy-Aware Exploration for Multi-GPU LLM Inference Optimization
summary: We present EnergyLens, an end-to-end framework for energy-aware large language model (LLM) inference optimization. As LLMs scale, predicting and reducing their energy footprint has become critical for sustainability and datacenter operations, yet existing approaches either require production-level code and expensive profiling or fail to accurately capture multi-GPU energy behavior. As a result, practitioners lack tools for deciding which optimizations to prioritize and for selecting among existing deployment configurations when exhaustive profiling is impractical. EnergyLens addresses this gap with an intuitive einsum-based interface that captures LLM specifications including fusion, parallelism, and compute-communication overlap, combined with load-imbalance-aware MoE modeling and an empirically driven communication energy model for multi-GPU settings. We validate EnergyLens on Llama3 and Qwen3-MoE across tensor-parallel and expert-parallel configurations, achieving mean absolute percentage errors (MAPEs) between 9.25% and 13.19% for multi-GPU prefill and decode energy, and 12.97% across SM allocations for Megatron-style overlap. Our energy-driven exploration reveals up to 1.47x and 52.9x energy variation across configurations in prefill and decode efficiency and motivates distributed serving. We further show that compute-communication overlap is difficult to optimize with intuition alone, but EnergyLens correctly identifies Pareto-optimal overlap configurations.

AI.tldr: EnergyLens is an end-to-end framework for predicting and optimizing energy consumption in large language model inference across multi-GPU deployments, achieving under 13% error rates and revealing significant energy variations across configurations.
AI.motivation: As LLMs scale, predicting and reducing energy consumption has become critical for sustainability and datacenter operations. Current approaches either require expensive production-level profiling or fail to accurately capture multi-GPU energy behavior, leaving practitioners without practical tools for optimization prioritization and configuration selection.
AI.method: EnergyLens uses an intuitive einsum-based interface to capture LLM specifications including fusion, parallelism, and compute-communication overlap. It combines load-imbalance-aware MoE modeling with an empirically driven communication energy model for multi-GPU settings.
AI.result: The framework achieves mean absolute percentage errors between 9.25% and 13.19% for multi-GPU prefill and decode energy prediction, and 12.97% across SM allocations for Megatron-style overlap. Energy exploration reveals up to 1.47x and 52.9x energy variation across configurations in prefill and decode efficiency.
AI.conclusion: EnergyLens successfully addresses the gap in energy prediction tools for LLM inference, demonstrating that compute-communication overlap optimization requires systematic analysis rather than intuition, and correctly identifies Pareto-optimal configurations for energy-efficient distributed serving.

title: Learning Developmental Scaffoldings to Guide Self-Organisation
summary: From subcellular structures to entire organisms, many natural systems generate complex organisation through self-organisation: local interactions that collectively give rise to global structure without any blueprint of the outcome. Yet a significant portion of the information driving such processes is not produced by self-organisation itself, instead, it is often offloaded to initial conditions of the system. Biological development is a prime example, where maternal pre-patterns encode positional and symmetry-breaking information that scaffolds the self-organising process. From maternal morphogen gradients in early embryogenesis to tissue-level morphogenetic pre-patterns guiding organ formation, this transfer of information to initial conditions, analogous to a memory-compute trade-off in computational systems, is a fundamental part of developmental processes. In this work, we study this offloading phenomenon by introducing a model that jointly learns both the self-organisation rules and the pre-patterns, allowing their interplay to be varied and measured under controlled conditions: a Neural Cellular Automaton (NCA) paired with a learned coordinate-based pattern generator (SIREN), both trained simultaneously to generate a set of patterns. We provide information-theoretic analyses of how information is distributed between pre-patterns and the self-organising process, and show that jointly learning both components yields improvements in robustness, encoding capacity, and symmetry breaking over purely self-organising alternatives. Our analysis further suggests that effective pre-patterns do not simply approximate their targets; rather, they bias the developmental dynamics in ways that facilitate convergence, pointing to a non-trivial relationship between the structure of initial conditions and the dynamics of self-organisation.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a model combining neural cellular automata with learned pre-pattern generators to study how biological systems transfer information to initial conditions. This approach improves robustness and symmetry breaking while revealing that effective pre-patterns strategically bias developmental dynamics.
AI.motivation: To understand how biological systems transfer information to initial conditions (pre-patterns) that scaffold self-organizing processes, analogous to memory-compute trade-offs in computational systems. This offloading phenomenon is fundamental to developmental processes but not well understood mechanistically.
AI.method: Developed a model combining a Neural Cellular Automaton (NCA) for self-organization with a learned coordinate-based pattern generator (SIREN) for pre-patterns. Both components were trained simultaneously to generate target patterns, with information-theoretic analysis of information distribution between pre-patterns and self-organization.
AI.result: Joint learning of pre-patterns and self-organization rules yields improvements in robustness, encoding capacity, and symmetry breaking over purely self-organizing alternatives. Effective pre-patterns facilitate convergence by biasing developmental dynamics rather than approximating targets.
AI.conclusion: The study reveals that combining learned pre-patterns with self-organizing processes leads to more effective pattern generation than pure self-organization. Effective pre-patterns act as developmental scaffolds that facilitate convergence through strategic bias rather than simply approximating target patterns.

title: Not All Timesteps Matter Equally: Selective Alignment Knowledge Distillation for Spiking Neural Networks
summary: Spiking neural networks (SNNs), which are brain-inspired and spike-driven, achieve high energy efficiency. However, a performance gap between SNNs and artificial neural networks (ANNs) still remains. Knowledge distillation (KD) is commonly adopted to improve SNN performance, but existing methods typically enforce uniform alignment across all timesteps, either from a teacher network or through inter-temporal self-distillation, implicitly assuming that per-timestep predictions should be treated equally. In practice, SNN predictions vary and evolve over time, and intermediate timesteps need not all be individually correct even when the final aggregated output is correct. Under such conditions, effective distillation should not force every timestep toward the same supervision target, but instead provide corrective guidance to erroneous timesteps while preserving useful temporal dynamics. To address this issue, we propose Selective Alignment Knowledge Distillation (SeAl-KD), which selectively aligns class-level and temporal knowledge by equalizing competing logits at erroneous timesteps and reweighting temporal alignment based on confidence and inter-timestep similarity. Extensive experiments on static image and neuromorphic event-based datasets demonstrate consistent improvements over existing distillation methods. The code is available at https://github.com/KaiSUN1/SeAl

AI.tldr: SeAl-KD improves SNN performance via selective knowledge distillation that targets erroneous timesteps instead of uniform temporal alignment, leveraging confidence and similarity-based weighting.
AI.motivation: Current KD methods for SNNs enforce uniform alignment across all timesteps, ignoring that SNN predictions naturally evolve over time and intermediate timesteps don't need to be individually correct for final accuracy. Forcing equal treatment of all timesteps is suboptimal.
AI.method: Proposes Selective Alignment Knowledge Distillation (SeAl-KD) that selectively aligns class-level and temporal knowledge by: 1) equalizing competing logits at erroneous timesteps, and 2) reweighting temporal alignment based on confidence and inter-timestep similarity metrics.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on static image and neuromorphic event-based datasets show consistent improvements over existing distillation methods, demonstrating the effectiveness of the selective alignment approach.
AI.conclusion: Selective alignment that targets erroneous timesteps while preserving useful temporal dynamics is more effective than uniform temporal alignment for SNN knowledge distillation, bridging the performance gap between SNNs and ANNs.

title: Small, Private Language Models as Teammates for Educational Assessment Design
summary: Generative AI increasingly supports educational design tasks, e.g., through Large Language Models (LLMs), demonstrating the capability to design assessment questions that are aligned with pedagogical frameworks (e.g., Bloom's taxonomy). However, they often rely on subjective or limited evaluation methods; focus primarily on proprietary models; or rarely systematically examine generation, evaluation, or deployment constraints in real educational settings. Meanwhile, Small Language Models (SLMs) have emerged as local alternatives that better address privacy and resource limitations; yet their effectiveness for assessment tasks remains underexplored. To address this gap, we systematically compare LLMs and SLMs for assessment question design; evaluate generation quality across Bloom's taxonomy levels using reproducible, pedagogically grounded metrics; and further assess model-based judging against expert-informed evaluation by analyzing reliability and agreement patterns. Results show that SLMs achieve competitive performance across key pedagogically motivated quality dimensions while enabling local, privacy-sensitive deployment. However, model-based evaluations also exhibit systematic inconsistencies and bias relative to expert ratings. These findings provide evidence to posit language models as bounded assistants in assessment workflows; underscore the necessity of Human-in-the-Loop; and advance the automated educational question generation field by examining quality, reliability, and deployment-aware trade-offs.

AI.tldr: Generative AI (especially SLMs) can design educationally valid assessment questions but requires human oversight due to evaluation biases and quality trade-offs.
AI.motivation: Current generative AI for educational design relies on proprietary LLMs with subjective evaluations, while emerging SLMs offer privacy/resource benefits but their effectiveness for assessment tasks is underexplored. There's a need for systematic comparison of LLMs vs. SLMs using pedagogically grounded metrics in real educational contexts.
AI.method: Systematic comparison of LLMs and SLMs for assessment question design; evaluation of generation quality across Bloom's taxonomy levels using reproducible, pedagogically grounded metrics; assessment of model-based judging versus expert-informed evaluation with analysis of reliability and agreement patterns.
AI.result: SLMs achieve competitive performance across key pedagogically motivated quality dimensions while enabling local, privacy-sensitive deployment. However, model-based evaluations exhibit systematic inconsistencies and bias relative to expert ratings.
AI.conclusion: Language models should be positioned as bounded assistants in assessment workflows requiring Human-in-the-Loop oversight. The research advances automated educational question generation by examining quality, reliability, and deployment-aware trade-offs.

title: Architecture-Aware Explanation Auditing for Industrial Visual Inspection
summary: Industrial visual inspection systems increasingly rely on deep classifiers whose heatmap explanations may appear visually plausible while failing to identify the image regions that actually drive model decisions. This paper operationalizes an architecture-aware explanation audit protocol grounded in the native-readout hypothesis: the perturbation-based faithfulness of an explanation method is bounded by its structural distance from the model's native decision mechanism. On WM-811K wafer maps (9 classes, 172k images) under a three-seed zero-fill perturbation protocol, ViT-Tiny + Attention Rollout attains Deletion AUC 0.211 against 0.432-0.525 for Swin-Tiny / ResNet18+CBAM / DenseNet121 + Grad-CAM (abs(Cohen's d) > 1.1), despite lower classification accuracy. Swin-Tiny disentangles architecture family from readout structure: despite being a Transformer, its spatial feature-map hierarchy makes it Grad-CAM compatible, showing that the operative factor is readout structure rather than architecture family. A model-agnostic control (RISE) compresses all families to Deletion AUC about 0.1, indicating the gap arises from the explainer pathway; notably, RISE outperforms all native methods, so native readout is a compatibility principle rather than an optimality guarantee. A blur-fill sensitivity analysis shows that the family ordering reverses under a different perturbation baseline, reinforcing that faithfulness rankings are joint properties of (model, explainer, perturbation operator) triples. An exploratory boundary-condition study on MVTec AD (pretrained models) indicates that audit results are dataset/task dependent and identifies conditions requiring qualification. The protocol yields actionable guidance: explanation pathways should be co-designed with model architectures based on readout structure, and deployed heatmaps should be accompanied by quantitative faithfulness metrics.

AI.tldr: Explanation faithfulness depends on the compatibility between model readout structure and explanation method, with native readout serving as a practical design principle rather than optimality guarantee - heatmaps should always be accompanied by quantitative faithfulness metrics.
AI.motivation: Industrial visual inspection systems increasingly rely on deep classifiers whose heatmap explanations may appear visually plausible while failing to identify the image regions that actually drive model decisions. The paper aims to address this critical gap by understanding what makes explanation methods faithful to model decisions, particularly examining the native-readout hypothesis as a guiding principle for explainability in practical applications.
AI.method: The paper operationalizes an architecture-aware explanation audit protocol grounded in the native-readout hypothesis. It employs a three-seed zero-fill perturbation protocol across multiple datasets (WM-811K wafer maps with 9 classes and 172k images, MVTec AD for boundary conditions). The protocol evaluates various model-architecture combinations (ViT-Tiny, Swin-Tiny, ResNet18+CBAM, DenseNet121) with different explanation methods (Attention Rollout, Grad-CAM, RISE) using quantitative metrics like Deletion AUC and Cohen's d effect sizes.
AI.result: The audit reveals that ViT-Tiny + Attention Rollout attains significantly lower Deletion AUC (0.211) than other combinations (0.432-0.525) despite lower classification accuracy. Swin-Tiny demonstrates that readout structure is more important than architecture family for explanation compatibility. RISE compresses all families to similar Deletion AUC (~0.1) and outperforms all native methods, showing that native readout is a compatibility principle rather than optimality guarantee. A blur-fill sensitivity analysis reveals that faithfulness rankings reverse under different perturbation baselines.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that explanation faithfulness is a joint property of model, explainer, and perturbation operator triples rather than being determined by architecture family alone. Native readout compatibility serves as a practical design principle for more faithful explanations, but doesn't guarantee optimality. The key recommendation is that explanation pathways should be co-designed with model architectures based on readout structure, and deployed heatmaps should be accompanied by quantitative faithfulness metrics.

title: Orchard: An Open-Source Agentic Modeling Framework
summary: Agentic modeling aims to transform LLMs into autonomous agents capable of solving complex tasks through planning, reasoning, tool use, and multi-turn interaction with environments. Despite major investment, open research remains constrained by infrastructure and training gaps. Many high-performing systems rely on proprietary codebases, models, or services, while most open-source frameworks focus on orchestration and evaluation rather than scalable agent training. We present Orchard, an open-source framework for scalable agentic modeling. At its core is Orchard Env, a lightweight environment service providing reusable primitives for sandbox lifecycle management across task domains, agent harnesses, and pipeline stages. On top of Orchard Env, we build three agentic modeling recipes. Orchard-SWE targets coding agents. We distill 107K trajectories from MiniMax-M2.5 and Qwen3.5-397B, introduce credit-assignment SFT to learn from productive segments of unresolved trajectories, and apply Balanced Adaptive Rollout for RL. Starting from Qwen3-30B-A3B-Thinking, Orchard-SWE achieves 64.3% on SWE-bench Verified after SFT and 67.5% after SFT+RL, setting a new state of the art among open-source models of comparable size. Orchard-GUI trains a 4B vision-language computer-use agent using only 0.4K distilled trajectories and 2.2K open-ended tasks. It achieves 74.1%, 67.0%, and 64.0% success rates on WebVoyager, Online-Mind2Web, and DeepShop, respectively, making it the strongest open-source model while remaining competitive with proprietary systems. Orchard-Claw targets personal assistant agents. Trained with only 0.2K synthetic tasks, it achieves 59.6% pass@3 on Claw-Eval and 73.9% when paired with a stronger ZeroClaw harness. Collectively, these results show that a lightweight, open, harness-agnostic environment layer enables reusable agentic data, training recipes, and evaluations across domains.

AI.tldr: Orchard is an open-source framework for scalable agentic modeling with three specialized agent recipes achieving state-of-the-art results in coding, GUI interaction, and personal assistance domains.
AI.motivation: Current open research in agentic modeling is limited by infrastructure gaps and reliance on proprietary systems, while most open-source frameworks focus only on orchestration and evaluation rather than scalable training.
AI.method: Orchard framework includes: 1) Orchard Env - lightweight environment service for sandbox lifecycle management; 2) Three agentic modeling recipes: Orchard-SWE (coding agents with credit-assignment SFT and Balanced Adaptive Rollout RL), Orchard-GUI (vision-language computer-use agents), and Orchard-Claw (personal assistant agents); 3) Specialized training approaches including trajectory distillation, credit-assignment SFT, and RL techniques.
AI.result: Orchard-SWE achieves 64.3% (SFT) and 67.5% (SFT+RL) on SWE-bench Verified, new SOTA for open-source models. Orchard-GUI achieves 74.1%, 67.0%, and 64.0% on three benchmarks, strongest open-source model. Orchard-Claw achieves 59.6% pass@3 on Claw-Eval, 73.9% with stronger harness.
AI.conclusion: A lightweight, open, harness-agnostic environment layer enables reusable agentic data, training recipes, and evaluations across domains, demonstrating that effective agentic modeling can be achieved with open-source approaches.

title: Dynamics of the Transformer Residual Stream: Coupling Spectral Geometry to Network Topology
summary: Large language models are remarkably capable, yet how computation propagates through their layers remains poorly understood. A growing line of work treats depth as discrete time and the residual stream as a dynamical system, where each layer's nonlinear update has a local linear description. However, previous analyses have relied on scalar summaries or approximate linearizations, leaving the full spectral geometry of trained LLMs unknown. We perform full Jacobian eigendecomposition across three production--scale LLMs and show that training installs a monotonic spectral gradient through depth -- from non-normal, rotation-dominated early layers to near--symmetric late layers -- together with a cumulative low-rank bottleneck that funnels perturbations into a small fraction of the residual stream's effective dimensions. Our experiments reveal that this gradient and the dimensional collapse are learned rather than architectural, and is largely dissolved when structured non-normality is removed. We further show that the topological positioning of graph communities predicts whether the Jacobian amplifies or suppresses them, with the sign of the coupling determined by the local operator type, a relationship absent at initialization. These results map a learned spectral geometry in LLMs that links perturbation propagation and compression to the network's functional topology.

AI.tldr: The paper reveals that large language models develop a learned spectral geometry where early layers are rotation-dominated (non-normal) and later layers become near-symmetric, creating a gradient that funnels information through a low-rank bottleneck.
AI.motivation: While LLMs are highly capable, there's poor understanding of how computation propagates through layers. Existing analyses use scalar summaries or approximate linearizations, leaving the full spectral geometry of trained LLMs unknown. The authors want to understand the actual propagation dynamics across layers.
AI.method: Performed full Jacobian eigendecomposition across three production-scale LLMs, analyzing how training affects spectral properties. Examined spectral gradients through depth, dimensional collapse, and perturbation propagation. Also studied how structured non-normality removal affects these patterns.
AI.result: Found that training installs a monotonic spectral gradient: early layers are non-normal and rotation-dominated, while late layers become near-symmetric. Also discovered cumulative low-rank bottleneck that funnels perturbations into small fraction of residual stream dimensions. These patterns are learned (not architectural) and topological positioning of graph communities predicts whether Jacobian amplifies or suppresses them.
AI.conclusion: The paper maps a learned spectral geometry in LLMs that links perturbation propagation and compression to the network's functional topology. Training induces systematic changes in spectral properties that shape information flow, creating distinct computational regimes across layers.

title: Case-Based Calibration of Adaptive Reasoning and Execution for LLM Tool Use
summary: Tool use extends large language models beyond parametric knowledge, but reliable execution requires balancing appropriate reasoning depth with strict structural validity. We approach this problem from a case-based perspective to present CAST, a case-driven framework that treats historical execution trajectories as structured cases. Instead of reusing raw exemplar outputs, CAST extracts case-derived signals to identify complexity profiles for estimating optimal reasoning strategies, alongside failure profiles to map likely structural breakdowns. The framework translates this knowledge into a fine-grained reward design and adaptive reasoning, enabling the model to autonomously internalize case-based strategies during reinforcement learning. Experiments on BFCLv2 and ToolBench demonstrate that CAST improves both schema-faithful execution and task-level tool-use success while reducing unnecessary deliberation. The approach achieves up to 5.85 percentage points gain in overall execution accuracy and reduces average reasoning length by 26%, significantly mitigating high-impact structural errors. Ultimately, this demonstrates how historical execution cases can provide reusable adaptation knowledge for calibrated tool use.

AI.tldr: CAST is a case-driven framework that uses historical execution trajectories to create adaptive reasoning strategies and reward signals, improving tool-use reliability while reducing unnecessary deliberation.
AI.motivation: Tool use in large language models requires balancing appropriate reasoning depth with strict structural validity, but current approaches struggle with finding the right reasoning strategies and avoiding structural breakdowns in tool execution.
AI.method: CAST treats historical execution trajectories as structured cases, extracting complexity profiles to estimate optimal reasoning strategies and failure profiles to map likely structural breakdowns. It translates this knowledge into fine-grained reward design and adaptive reasoning, enabling models to internalize case-based strategies during reinforcement learning.
AI.result: Experiments on BFCLv2 and ToolBench show CAST improves both schema-faithful execution and task-level tool-use success while reducing unnecessary deliberation. It achieves up to 5.85 percentage points gain in overall execution accuracy and reduces average reasoning length by 26%, significantly mitigating high-impact structural errors.
AI.conclusion: Historical execution cases can provide reusable adaptation knowledge for calibrated tool use, demonstrating that structured case-based learning enables more reliable and efficient tool execution in large language models.

title: TILT: Target-induced loss tilting under covariate shift
summary: We introduce and analyze Target-Induced Loss Tilting (TILT) for unsupervised domain adaptation under covariate shift. It is based on a novel objective function that decomposes the source predictor as $f+b$, fits $f+b$ on labeled source data while simultaneously penalizing the auxiliary component $b$ on unlabeled target inputs. The resulting fit $f$ is deployed as the final target predictor. At the population level, we show that this target-side penalty implicitly induces relative importance weighting at the population level, but in terms of an estimand $b^*_f$ that is self-localized to the current error, and remains uniformly bounded for any source-target pair (even those with disjoint supports). We prove a general finite-sample oracle inequality on the excess risk, and use it to give an end-to-end guarantee for training with sparse ReLU networks. Experiments on controlled regression problems and shifted CIFAR-100 distillation show that TILT improves target-domain performance over source-only training, exact importance weighting, and relative density-ratio baselines, with a stable dependence on the regularization parameter.

AI.tldr: TILT is a new unsupervised domain adaptation method that decomposes source predictor into main and auxiliary components, fitting on source data while penalizing the auxiliary component on target data to achieve better target performance.
AI.motivation: Addressing covariate shift in unsupervised domain adaptation where source and target distributions differ, improving upon existing methods like importance weighting.
AI.method: Decompose source predictor as f+b, fit f+b on labeled source data while penalizing auxiliary component b on unlabeled target inputs, then deploy f as final target predictor. Uses sparse ReLU networks for training.
AI.result: Theoretical: Shows TILT induces relative importance weighting with self-localized estimand, proves finite-sample oracle inequality and end-to-end guarantees. Empirical: Improves target performance over baselines on regression problems and shifted CIFAR-100, with stable regularization dependence.
AI.conclusion: TILT provides an effective unsupervised domain adaptation approach with theoretical guarantees and empirical improvements over existing methods for covariate shift problems.

title: Dual-Dimensional Consistency: Balancing Budget and Quality in Adaptive Inference-Time Scaling
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) have demonstrated remarkable abilities in reasoning. However, maximizing their potential through inference-time scaling faces challenges in trade-off between sampling budget and reasoning quality. Current strategies remain inefficient as they typically treat sampling width and depth as orthogonal objectives, where width consensus methods risk reinforcing hallucinations, while depth pruning mechanisms prematurely truncate complex yet valid reasoning chains. Therefore, we propose Dual-Dimensional Consistency (DDC), a unified framework that bridges path quality with adaptive termination. By coupling Confidence-Weighted Bayesian protocol with a Trend-Aware Stratified Pruning, our method ensures that computational resources are concentrated on high quality reasoning paths, filtering hallucinations while accelerating consensus. Evaluations across five benchmarks demonstrate that this approach reduces token consumption by over 10 times while maintaining or exceeding the accuracy of strong baselines across various LLMs.

AI.tldr: DDC is a unified framework that optimizes LLM reasoning by jointly optimizing sampling width and depth, reducing token usage by 10x while maintaining or improving accuracy.
AI.motivation: Current LLM reasoning strategies inefficiently treat sampling width and depth as separate objectives - width methods risk reinforcing hallucinations while depth methods prematurely truncate valid reasoning chains, creating trade-offs between sampling budget and reasoning quality.
AI.method: Dual-Dimensional Consistency (DDC) framework that bridges path quality with adaptive termination through Confidence-Weighted Bayesian protocol and Trend-Aware Stratified Pruning, concentrating computational resources on high-quality reasoning paths.
AI.result: The approach reduces token consumption by over 10 times while maintaining or exceeding accuracy of strong baselines across five benchmarks with various LLMs.
AI.conclusion: DDC provides an effective solution for efficient LLM reasoning by adaptively optimizing both width and depth dimensions, filtering hallucinations while accelerating consensus formation.

title: Smooth Multi-Policy Causal Effect Estimation in Longitudinal Settings
summary: Comparative evaluation of multiple dynamic treatment policies is essential for healthcare and policy decisions, yet conventional longitudinal causal inference methods estimate each in isolation, preventing information sharing across counterfactuals. We demonstrate that this separate estimation paradigm induces a structurally uncontrolled second-order bias, inflating finite-sample variance even after standard debiasing with longitudinal targeted maximum likelihood estimation(LTMLE). To address this, we propose a policy-aware reparameterization of Iterative Conditional Expectation (ICE) Q-functions that enables joint estimation through shared representations. We implement this approach in the Policy-Encoded Q Network (PEQ-Net), an architecture centered on a shared policy encoder. The encoder is trained using kernel mean embeddings, ensuring that the learned representation space reflects population-level policy dissimilarities. After applying an LTMLE correction step, we prove this design imposes a structural constraint on the second-order remainder, thereby stabilizing finite-sample variance. Experiments on semi-synthetic datasets demonstrate that PEQ-Net consistently outperforms existing ICE-based methods, achieving substantial reductions in root-mean-square error, particularly when evaluating closely related policies.

AI.tldr: PEQ-Net: A novel architecture that enables joint estimation of multiple dynamic treatment policies using shared representations, reducing second-order bias and improving variance stabilization compared to existing methods.
AI.motivation: Current longitudinal causal inference methods estimate each dynamic treatment policy in isolation, preventing information sharing across counterfactuals. This separate estimation induces second-order bias that inflates finite-sample variance, even after debiasing with longitudinal targeted maximum likelihood estimation (LTMLE).
AI.method: Proposes Policy-Encoded Q Network (PEQ-Net) with policy-aware reparameterization of Iterative Conditional Expectation (ICE) Q-functions. Uses shared policy encoder trained with kernel mean embeddings to ensure representation space reflects population-level policy dissimilarities. Applies LTMLE correction step.
AI.result: PEQ-Net consistently outperforms existing ICE-based methods on semi-synthetic datasets, achieving substantial reductions in root-mean-square error, particularly when evaluating closely related policies.
AI.conclusion: Joint estimation through shared representations with PEQ-Net imposes structural constraints on second-order remainder, stabilizing finite-sample variance and enabling more efficient comparative evaluation of multiple dynamic treatment policies.

title: Why Neighborhoods Matter: Traversal Context and Provenance in Agentic GraphRAG
comment: 7 pages, 2 figures, Submitted at IJCAI-ECAI 2026 Joint Workshop on GENAIK and NORA
summary: Retrieval-Augmented Generation can improve factuality by grounding answers in external evidence, but Agentic GraphRAG complicates what it means for citations to be faithful. In these systems, an agent explores a knowledge graph before producing an answer and a small set of citations. We frame citation faithfulness as a trajectory-level problem: final citations should not only support the answer, but also account for the graph traversal, structure, and visited-but-uncited entities that may influence it. Through controlled ablation experiments, we compare the effects of isolating, removing, and masking cited and uncited graph entities. Our results show that cited evidence is often necessary, as removing it substantially changes answers and reduces accuracy. However, citations are not sufficient, because accurate answers can also depend on uncited traversal context and surrounding graph structure. These findings suggest that citation evaluation in Agentic GraphRAG should move beyond source support toward provenance over the broader retrieval trajectory.

AI.tldr: Agentic GraphRAG systems complicate citation faithfulness because agents traverse knowledge graphs before generating answers with limited citations, requiring evaluation to consider not just cited sources but the entire retrieval trajectory and visited-but-uncited entities.
AI.motivation: Traditional citation faithfulness in RAG focuses on whether cited sources support answers, but Agentic GraphRAG introduces complexity because agents explore knowledge graphs before producing answers with a small set of citations. The motivation is to understand how graph traversal, structure, and visited-but-uncited entities influence answers and what this means for citation faithfulness.
AI.method: The researchers frame citation faithfulness as a trajectory-level problem and conduct controlled ablation experiments comparing the effects of isolating, removing, and masking both cited and uncited graph entities to understand their influence on answer accuracy.
AI.result: Cited evidence is often necessary (removing it substantially changes answers and reduces accuracy), but citations are not sufficient - accurate answers can also depend on uncited traversal context and surrounding graph structure that influence the reasoning process.
AI.conclusion: Citation evaluation in Agentic GraphRAG should move beyond simple source support toward provenance over the broader retrieval trajectory, considering the entire graph exploration process rather than just the final cited sources.

title: MetaMoE: Diversity-Aware Proxy Selection for Privacy-Preserving Mixture-of-Experts Unification
summary: Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) models scale capacity by combining specialized experts, but most existing approaches assume centralized access to training data. In practice, data are distributed across clients and cannot be shared due to privacy constraints, making unified MoE training challenging. We propose MetaMoE, a privacy-preserving framework that unifies independently trained, domain-specialized experts into a single MoE using public proxy data as surrogates for inaccessible private data. Central to MetaMoE is diversity-aware proxy selection, which selects client-domain-relevant and diverse samples from public data to effectively approximate private data distributions and supervise router learning. These proxies are further used to align expert training, improving expert coordination at unification time, while a context-aware router enhances expert selection across heterogeneous inputs. Experiments on computer vision and natural language processing benchmarks demonstrate that MetaMoE consistently outperforms recent privacy-preserving MoE unification methods. Code is available at https://github.com/ws-jiang/MetaMoE.

AI.tldr: MetaMoE is a privacy-preserving framework that unifies independently trained, domain-specialized experts from distributed clients using public proxy data, with diversity-aware proxy selection and context-aware routing.
AI.motivation: MoE models typically assume centralized training data access, but real-world data is distributed across clients and cannot be shared due to privacy constraints, making unified MoE training challenging in federated settings.
AI.method: Proposes MetaMoE with: 1) diversity-aware proxy selection from public data to approximate private distributions and supervise router learning; 2) using proxies to align expert training for better coordination; 3) context-aware router for expert selection across heterogeneous inputs.
AI.result: Experiments on computer vision and NLP benchmarks show MetaMoE consistently outperforms recent privacy-preserving MoE unification methods.
AI.conclusion: MetaMoE effectively addresses privacy-preserving MoE unification by leveraging public proxy data with diversity-aware selection and expert alignment, enabling better performance than existing methods while maintaining privacy constraints.

title: Minimal-Intervention KV Retention: A Design-Space Study and a Diversity-Penalty Survivor
comment: 12 pages, 2 figures, 3 tables. Code and data: https://github.com/libophd/minimal-kv-retention
summary: KV-cache compression at small budgets is a crowded design space spanning cache representation, head-wise routing, compression cadence, decoding behavior, and within-budget scoring. We study seven mechanisms across these five families under matched mean cache on long-form mathematical reasoning (MATH-500~\cite{hendrycks2021math}) with two distilled-reasoning models (Qwen-7B and Llama-8B variants of DeepSeek-R1-Distill~\cite{deepseek2025r1}) at budgets $b \in \{64, 128\}$. All seven were rejected. We then propose $α$, a one-function modification to the TriAttention~\cite{mao2026triattention} retention scorer that replaces argmax-top-$k$ with greedy facility-location-inspired selection under a V-space redundancy penalty controlled by a single weight $λ$. A pre-registered protocol tunes $λ$ on a frozen development split and confirms on a disjoint held-out split; with $λ= 0.5$, $α$ clears Bonferroni on two of the four (model, budget) cells (Qwen $b{=}128$ and Llama $b{=}64$), no cell is significantly negative, and the pre-registered Branch~A triggers. The finding is asymmetric: a minimal scoring modification beat heavier structural redesigns in this regime, and the combined matched-memory, sympy-graded, held-out confirmation protocol is the evidence standard that made the asymmetry visible.

AI.tldr: A minimal scoring modification (α) to TriAttention's retention scorer outperforms heavier structural KV-cache compression designs at small budgets (64,128 tokens), beating them on held-out mathematical reasoning benchmarks.
AI.motivation: KV-cache compression at small budgets is a crowded design space with many mechanisms, but existing approaches were all rejected in testing. The authors aimed to find a simple yet effective modification that could outperform complex structural redesigns.
AI.method: Studied 7 mechanisms across 5 families of KV-cache compression, then proposed α - a one-function modification to TriAttention's retention scorer that replaces argmax-top-k with greedy facility-location-inspired selection under a V-space redundancy penalty controlled by λ. Used pre-registered protocol tuning λ on development split and confirming on held-out split.
AI.result: With λ=0.5, α cleared Bonferroni correction on 2 of 4 (model, budget) cells (Qwen b=128 and Llama b=64), no cell was significantly negative, and pre-registered Branch A triggered. Asymmetric finding: minimal scoring modification beat heavier structural redesigns.
AI.conclusion: A minimal scoring modification can outperform complex structural KV-cache compression designs at small budgets, and the combined matched-memory, sympy-graded, held-out confirmation protocol is the evidence standard that reveals such asymmetries.

title: OpenDeepThink: Parallel Reasoning via Bradley--Terry Aggregation
summary: Test-time compute scaling is a primary axis for improving LLM reasoning. Existing methods primarily scale depth by extending a single reasoning trace. Scaling breadth by sampling multiple candidates in parallel is straightforward, but introduces a selection bottleneck: choosing the best candidate without a ground-truth verifier, since pointwise LLM judging is noisy and biased. To address this, we introduce OpenDeepThink, a population-based test-time compute framework that selects via pairwise Bradley-Terry comparison. Each generation, the LLM judges random pairs of candidates and aggregates votes via Bradley-Terry into a global ranking; top-ranked candidates are preserved and the top three quarters are mutated using the natural-language critiques produced during comparison; the bottom quarter is discarded. OpenDeepThink raises Gemini 3.1 Pro's effective Codeforces Elo by +405 points in eight sequential LLM-call rounds (~27 minutes wall-clock). The pipeline transfers across weaker and stronger models without retuning, and on the multi-domain HLE benchmark, gains appear concentrated in objectively verifiable domains and reverse in subjective ones. We release CF-73, a curated set of 73 expert-rated Codeforces problems with International Grandmaster annotation and 99% local-evaluation agreement against the official verdict.

AI.tldr: OpenDeepThink is a population-based test-time compute framework that scales LLM reasoning breadth by sampling multiple candidates and selecting the best via pairwise Bradley-Terry comparison and mutation, achieving significant performance gains on code generation tasks.
AI.motivation: Current test-time compute scaling methods primarily focus on extending depth through single reasoning traces. While scaling breadth by sampling multiple candidates in parallel is straightforward, it introduces a selection bottleneck: choosing the best candidate without ground-truth verification, since pointwise LLM judging is noisy and biased.
AI.method: The framework introduces OpenDeepThink, which uses pairwise Bradley-Terry comparison for selection. Each generation, the LLM judges random pairs of candidates and aggregates votes via Bradley-Terry into a global ranking. Top-ranked candidates are preserved, the top three quarters are mutated using natural-language critiques from comparisons, and the bottom quarter is discarded. This creates an evolutionary population-based approach to reasoning.
AI.result: OpenDeepThink raises Gemini 3.1 Pro's effective Codeforces Elo by +405 points in eight sequential LLM-call rounds (~27 minutes wall-clock). The pipeline transfers across weaker and stronger models without retuning. On the multi-domain HLE benchmark, gains appear concentrated in objectively verifiable domains and reverse in subjective ones. The authors also release CF-73, a curated set of 73 expert-rated Codeforces problems with International Grandmaster annotation.
AI.conclusion: OpenDeepThink demonstrates that population-based approaches with pairwise comparison selection can effectively scale test-time compute breadth, providing significant performance improvements on code generation tasks while being transferable across models without retuning.

title: Policy Optimization in Hybrid Discrete-Continuous Action Spaces via Mixed Gradients
summary: We study reinforcement learning in hybrid discrete-continuous action spaces, such as settings where the discrete component selects a regime (or index) and the continuous component optimizes within it -- a structure common in robotics, control, and operations problems. Standard model-free policy gradient methods rely on score-function (SF) estimators and suffer from severe credit-assignment issues in high-dimensional settings, leading to poor gradient quality. On the other hand, differentiable simulation largely sidesteps these issues by backpropagating through a simulator, but the presence of discrete actions or non-smooth dynamics yields biased or uninformative gradients. To address this, we propose Hybrid Policy Optimization (HPO), which backpropagates through the simulator wherever smoothness permits, using a mixed gradient estimator that combines pathwise and SF gradients while maintaining unbiasedness. We also show how problems with action discontinuities can be reformulated in hybrid form, further broadening its applicability. Empirically, HPO substantially outperforms PPO on inventory control and switched linear-quadratic regulator problems, with performance gaps increasing as the continuous action dimension grows. Finally, we characterize the structure of the mixed gradient, showing that its cross term -- which captures how continuous actions influence future discrete decisions -- becomes negligible near a discrete best response, thereby enabling approximate decentralized updates of the continuous and discrete components and reducing variance near optimality. All resources are available at github.com/MatiasAlvo/hybrid-rl.

AI.tldr: HPO (Hybrid Policy Optimization) combines differentiable simulation and policy gradients for reinforcement learning in hybrid discrete-continuous action spaces, outperforming PPO by maintaining unbiased gradients while enabling backpropagation through smooth dynamics.
AI.motivation: Standard policy gradient methods (using score-function estimators) suffer from severe credit-assignment issues in high-dimensional hybrid action spaces, while differentiable simulation methods yield biased gradients when faced with discrete actions or non-smooth dynamics. There's a need for a method that can handle both continuous and discrete components effectively in hybrid action spaces common in robotics, control, and operations.
AI.method: Proposes Hybrid Policy Optimization (HPO) which uses a mixed gradient estimator combining pathwise (differentiable simulation) and score-function gradients. The method backpropagates through the simulator where smoothness permits, maintains gradient unbiasedness, and can reformulate problems with action discontinuities into hybrid form. The approach enables approximate decentralized updates by exploiting that the cross-term in the mixed gradient (capturing continuous actions' influence on future discrete decisions) becomes negligible near optimal discrete responses.
