Evaluation of inherently interpretable models often targets a specific aspect of interpretability relevant to the model, but remains underdeveloped in terms of generating insight across a suite of measures. We introduce AIM, a comprehensive framework that addresses these limitations by measuring Accuracy, Instance-level explanations, and Model-level explanations. AIM is formulated with minimal constraints to enhance flexibility and facilitate broad applicability. Here, we use AIM in a pipeline, extracting explanations from inherently interpretable GNNs such as graph kernel networks (GKNs) and prototype networks (PNs), evaluating these explanations with AIM, identifying their limitations and obtaining insights to their characteristics. Taking GKNs as a case study, we show how the insights obtained from AIM can be used to develop an updated model, xGKN, that maintains high accuracy while demonstrating improved explainability. Our approach aims to advance the field of Explainable AI (XAI) for GNNs, providing more robust and practical solutions for understanding and improving complex models.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces AIM, a comprehensive framework for evaluating explainability in Graph Neural Networks that measures Accuracy, Instance-level explanations, and Model-level explanations to enable better comparison and insights across models.
AI.motivation: There is a lack of comprehensive evaluation frameworks for explainability in GNNs. Existing frameworks focus on post-hoc explanations for single models, making cross-model comparisons difficult, and evaluation of inherently interpretable models is underdeveloped in providing comprehensive insights.
AI.method: The authors introduce the AIM framework with minimal constraints for flexibility. They apply AIM in a pipeline to extract explanations from inherently interpretable GNNs (graph kernel networks and prototype networks), evaluate these explanations using AIM, identify limitations, and gain insights into their characteristics.
AI.result: Using graph kernel networks as a case study, the insights from AIM were used to develop an updated model called xGKN that maintains high accuracy while demonstrating improved explainability.
AI.conclusion: The AIM framework advances Explainable AI for GNNs by providing more robust and practical solutions for understanding and improving complex models through comprehensive evaluation of explainability across accuracy, instance-level, and model-level aspects.

title: TILBench: A Systematic Benchmark for Tabular Imbalanced Learning Across Data Regimes
summary: Imbalanced learning remains a fundamental challenge in tabular data applications. Despite decades of research and numerous proposed algorithms, a systematic empirical understanding of how different imbalanced learning methods behave across diverse data characteristics is still lacking. In particular, it remains unclear how different method families compare in predictive performance, robustness under varying data characteristics, and computational scalability. In this work, we present Tabular Imbalanced Learning Benchmark (TILBench), a large-scale empirical benchmark for tabular imbalanced learning. TILBench evaluates more than 40 representative algorithms across 57 diverse tabular datasets, resulting in over 200000 controlled experiments across a wide range of data characteristics. Our findings show that no single method consistently dominates across all settings; instead, the effectiveness of imbalanced learning methods depends strongly on dataset characteristics and computational constraints. Based on these findings, we provide practical recommendations for selecting appropriate methods in real-world applications.

AI.tldr: TILBench is a large-scale benchmark evaluating 40+ imbalanced learning algorithms on 57 tabular datasets, revealing no single dominant method and providing practical selection guidelines.
AI.motivation: Despite decades of research on imbalanced learning for tabular data, there's no systematic understanding of how different methods perform across diverse data characteristics, making it unclear which methods work best under what conditions.
AI.method: Created TILBench, a large-scale empirical benchmark that evaluates 40+ representative imbalanced learning algorithms across 57 diverse tabular datasets, conducting over 200,000 controlled experiments covering various data characteristics.
AI.result: No single method consistently dominates across all settings; method effectiveness strongly depends on dataset characteristics and computational constraints, showing significant variability in predictive performance, robustness, and scalability.
AI.conclusion: Imbalanced learning method selection should be data-dependent, with practical recommendations provided for choosing appropriate methods in real-world applications based on specific dataset characteristics and computational requirements.

title: A Mutual Information Lower Bound for Multimodal Regression Active Learning
summary: Active learning for continuous regression has lacked an acquisition function that targets epistemic uncertainty when the predictive distribution is multimodal: variance misses modal disagreement, and information-theoretic targets like BALD are designed for discrete outputs. We introduce a Two-Index framework that makes this separation explicit: one stochastic index selects among competing model hypotheses (epistemic source), while a second governs within-hypothesis randomness (aleatoric source). An entropy decomposition within the framework identifies the mutual information between the output and the epistemic index as a principled acquisition objective, and we prove this quantity vanishes as the model is trained on growing datasets, confirming that it captures exactly the uncertainty data can resolve. Because this mutual information is intractable for continuous outputs, we derive the Mutual Information Lower Bound (MI-LB) acquisition function, a closed-form approximation for Mixture Density Network ensembles. On benchmarks featuring multimodal systems, MI-LB matches or beats every baseline evaluated and is the only method to do so consistently -- geometric and Fisher-based baselines compete only when the input space already encodes the multimodality, and collapse otherwise.

AI.tldr: MI-LB acquisition function enables effective active learning for continuous regression with multimodal predictive distributions by targeting epistemic uncertainty through a novel Two-Index framework and mutual information approximation.
AI.motivation: Existing active learning methods for continuous regression lack acquisition functions that properly target epistemic uncertainty when predictive distributions are multimodal. Variance measures miss modal disagreement, and information-theoretic targets like BALD are designed for discrete outputs.
AI.method: Proposes a Two-Index framework separating epistemic (between competing model hypotheses) and aleatoric (within-hypothesis) uncertainty sources. Derives Mutual Information Lower Bound (MI-LB) acquisition function, a closed-form approximation for Mixture Density Network ensembles that targets the mutual information between outputs and epistemic index.
AI.result: MI-LB matches or beats all baselines on multimodal system benchmarks and is the only consistently effective method. Geometric and Fisher-based baselines only compete when input space encodes multimodality and collapse otherwise. Theoretical proof shows the targeted mutual information vanishes as models train on growing datasets.
AI.conclusion: The Two-Index framework provides principled separation of uncertainty sources for continuous regression, and MI-LB acquisition function effectively targets epistemic uncertainty in multimodal settings where existing methods fail, establishing a foundation for active learning in complex regression problems.

title: Learning with Shallow Neural Networks on Cluster-Structured Features
summary: The success of deep learning in high-dimensional settings is often attributed to the presence of low-dimensional structure in real-world data. While standard theoretical models typically assume that this structure lies in the target function, projecting unstructured inputs onto a low-dimensional subspace, data such as images, text or genomic sequences exhibit strong spatial correlations within the input space itself. In this paper, we propose a tractable model to study how these correlations affect the sample complexity of learning with gradient descent on shallow neural networks. Specifically, we consider targets that depend on a small number of latent Boolean variables, and input features grouped into clusters and correlated with the latent variables. Under an identifiability assumption, we show that for a layerwise gradient-descent variant, the sample complexity scales with the number of hidden variables and, when the signal-to-noise ratio is sufficiently high, is independent of the input dimension, up to logarithmic terms. We empirically test our theoretical findings on both synthetic and real data.

AI.tldr: Sample complexity of learning with gradient descent on shallow neural networks can be independent of input dimension when data has structured correlations with latent Boolean variables.
AI.motivation: Standard theoretical models assume low-dimensional structure lies in target functions, but real-world data (images, text, genomic sequences) exhibits strong spatial correlations within the input space itself. Need to understand how these correlations affect sample complexity.
AI.method: Propose tractable model with targets depending on small number of latent Boolean variables, input features grouped into clusters and correlated with latent variables. Use layerwise gradient-descent variant under identifiability assumption.
AI.result: Sample complexity scales with number of hidden variables and, when signal-to-noise ratio is sufficiently high, is independent of input dimension up to logarithmic terms. Empirical validation on synthetic and real data.
AI.conclusion: Input-space correlations can dramatically reduce sample complexity, making learning feasible in high dimensions when data exhibits structured relationships with latent variables.

title: A Hardware-Aware, Per-Layer Methodology for Post-Training Quantization of Large Language Models
summary: Scaled Outer Product (SOP) is a post-training quantization methodology for large language model weights, designed to deliver near-lossless fidelity at 4.5--6 bits per weight on hardware with per-layer LUT decode. The methodology combines per-layer search of fixed and dynamic codebook pairs selected by a per-block selection bit, signed per-block scales, activation-weighted cosine selection, and multiple-choice knapsack promotion of sensitive layers with outlier and sparse-residual correction. Fixed codebooks include NF4, BOF4, Split87, and SH4; per-layer optimized codebooks (DD4) are hosted in LUT SRAM. A new hardware-efficient LUT output format (HIF) is proposed to improve performance, energy, and cost. Across six open model families, the recommended FP6 operating point (E2M3sUE4M4, 6.5 bpw) achieves lower weight reconstruction error than the conventional per-layer-POT FP8 baseline (E4M3, 8.0 bpw) at 1.5 bpw lower storage cost, demonstrating that block-scaled small atoms with carefully chosen scale precision can replace conventionally-deployed FP8. Full evaluation across the 4.5--6 bpw range, including layer promotion and sparse residual correction, is reported in a companion paper.

AI.tldr: SOP is a post-training quantization method that achieves near-lossless 4.5-6 bit quantization for LLMs using per-layer codebook search, block scaling, and specialized hardware formats.
AI.motivation: To reduce LLM weight storage costs while maintaining high fidelity, specifically targeting better performance than conventional FP8 quantization at lower bit rates (4.5-6 bits vs 8 bits).
AI.method: Combines per-layer search of fixed/dynamic codebook pairs with per-block selection bits, signed per-block scales, activation-weighted cosine selection, multiple-choice knapsack promotion for sensitive layers, and outlier/sparse-residual correction. Uses specialized fixed codebooks (NF4, BOF4, Split87, SH4) and per-layer optimized DD4 codebooks stored in LUT SRAM.
AI.result: FP6 operating point (6.5 bpw) achieves lower weight reconstruction error than conventional per-layer FP8 baseline (8.0 bpw) at 1.5 bpw lower storage cost, demonstrating block-scaled small atoms can replace FP8.
AI.conclusion: SOP successfully delivers near-lossless quantization at 4.5-6 bits, outperforming FP8 at lower storage costs, with proposed hardware-efficient HIF format improving performance, energy, and cost efficiency.

title: Octopus: History-Free Gradient Orthogonalization for Continual Learning in Multimodal Large Language Models
summary: Continual learning in multimodal large language models (MLLMs) aims to sequentially acquire knowledge while mitigating catastrophic forgetting, yet existing methods face inherent limitations: architecture-based approaches incur additional computational overhead and often generalize poorly to new tasks, rehearsal-based methods rely on storing historical data, raising privacy and storage concerns, and conventional regularization-based strategies alone are insufficient to fully prevent parameter interference. We propose Octopus, a two-stage continual learning framework based on History-Free Gradient Orthogonalization (HiFGO), which enforces gradient-level orthogonality without historical task data. Our proposed two-stage finetuning strategy decouples task adaptation from regularization, achieving a principled balance between plasticity and stability. Experiments on UCIT show that Octopus establishes state-of-the-art performance, surpassing prior SOTA by 2.14% and 6.82% in terms of Avg and Last.

AI.tldr: Octopus is a two-stage continual learning framework for multimodal large language models that uses History-Free Gradient Orthogonalization to prevent catastrophic forgetting without storing historical data, achieving state-of-the-art performance on UCIT benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing continual learning methods for MLLMs have significant limitations: architecture-based approaches add computational overhead and generalize poorly, rehearsal-based methods require storing historical data (privacy/storage concerns), and regularization strategies alone cannot fully prevent parameter interference during sequential knowledge acquisition.
AI.method: The proposed Octopus framework uses a two-stage finetuning strategy with History-Free Gradient Orthogonalization (HiFGO). This enforces gradient-level orthogonality to prevent interference between tasks without needing historical task data. The two-stage approach decouples task adaptation from regularization to balance plasticity (learning new tasks) and stability (retaining old knowledge).
AI.result: Experiments on UCIT benchmark show Octopus establishes state-of-the-art performance, surpassing previous state-of-the-art methods by 2.14% in Avg score and 6.82% in Last score.
AI.conclusion: Octopus provides an effective continual learning solution for MLLMs that addresses key limitations of existing approaches by achieving gradient-level orthogonality without historical data storage, offering a principled balance between plasticity and stability that results in superior performance on benchmark tasks.

title: Efficient Online Conformal Selection with Limited Feedback
summary: We address the problem of conformal selection, where an agent must select a minimal subset of options to ensure that at least one ``success'' is identified with a pre-specified target probability $φ$. While traditional online conformal prediction focuses on maintaining validity for the observed sequence, minimizing the resource cost (efficiency) of such selections, especially under limited feedback, remains a significant challenge. In this work, we consider settings with the most limited ``bandit'' feedback, and demonstrate that the simple Adaptive Conformal Inference (ACI) update rule, when applied to the appropriate control parameter or dual variable, is both adversarially valid, ensuring the success target is met on average for any input sequence (and hence under distribution shifts), and stochastically efficient, achieving sublinear efficiency regret for $i.i.d.$ inputs against an appropriate stochastic benchmark. We show such guarantees under canonical models capturing bandit and semi-bandit feedback to the agent via a unifying algorithmic technique, and analytic framework involving Lyapunov functions. Our approach handles more complex settings than prior work, while requiring significantly less feedback, and our results provide a new theoretical bridge between efficient online learning with limited feedback and distribution-free uncertainty quantification.

AI.tldr: Adaptive Conformal Inference with bandit feedback achieves both adversarial validity (meeting success targets under distribution shifts) and stochastic efficiency (sublinear regret against stochastic benchmarks).
AI.motivation: Traditional online conformal prediction maintains validity but struggles with efficiency under limited feedback. There's a need to minimize resource cost while ensuring success probability targets are met, especially in bandit/semi-bandit feedback settings where information is scarce.
AI.method: Apply Adaptive Conformal Inference (ACI) update rule to appropriate control parameters or dual variables. Use Lyapunov functions and unifying algorithmic techniques to handle bandit and semi-bandit feedback models while maintaining validity guarantees.
AI.result: The approach ensures adversarial validity (success target met on average for any sequence, including distribution shifts) and achieves stochastic efficiency with sublinear efficiency regret for i.i.d. inputs against appropriate stochastic benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: This work establishes a theoretical bridge between efficient online learning with limited feedback and distribution-free uncertainty quantification, handling more complex settings than prior work with significantly less feedback.

title: InfoSFT: Learn More and Forget Less with Information-Aware Token Weighting
summary:
Supervised fine-tuning (SFT) provides the standard approach for teaching LLMs new behaviors from offline expert demonstrations. However, standard SFT uniformly fits all samples -- including those with low likelihood under the base model -- which can disproportionately drive training updates toward overfitting specific samples rather than learning the target behavior. Moreover, adapting to these unlikely samples induces substantial policy shifts that degrade prior capabilities. Existing methods mitigate this by filtering, regenerating, or down-weighting low-likelihood data. In doing so, they often suppress precisely the novel behaviors the base model has yet to learn.
  We propose InfoSFT, a principled weighting scheme for the SFT objective that concentrates learning signals on maximally informative, medium-confidence tokens -- those neither overly familiar to the base model nor too unlikely to cause instability. Requiring only a one-line modification to the standard token-wise loss, InfoSFT demonstrably improves generalization over vanilla SFT and likelihood-weighted baselines across math, code, and chain-of-thought tasks with diverse model families, while better preserving pre-existing capabilities.

AI.tldr: InfoSFT is a new weighting scheme for supervised fine-tuning that focuses learning on medium-confidence tokens to improve generalization while preserving existing capabilities, outperforming standard SFT and likelihood-based methods.
AI.motivation: Standard SFT uniformly fits all samples, including those with low likelihood under the base model, which can cause overfitting to specific samples and degrade prior capabilities by inducing substantial policy shifts. Existing methods that filter or down-weight low-likelihood data suppress the novel behaviors that the base model needs to learn.
AI.method: InfoSFT proposes a principled weighting scheme for the SFT objective that concentrates learning signals on maximally informative, medium-confidence tokens - those that are neither too familiar to the base model nor too unlikely to cause instability. This requires only a one-line modification to the standard token-wise loss function.
AI.result: InfoSFT demonstrably improves generalization over vanilla SFT and likelihood-weighted baselines across math, code, and chain-of-thought tasks with diverse model families, while better preserving pre-existing capabilities.
AI.conclusion: InfoSFT provides an effective solution to the fundamental trade-off in supervised fine-tuning - learning novel behaviors without destabilizing the base model - by focusing on optimally informative tokens, offering a simple yet powerful modification to standard SFT training.

title: Distance-Matrix Wasserstein Statistics for Scalable Gromov--Wasserstein Learning
summary: Gromov--Wasserstein (GW) distances compare graphs, shapes, and point clouds through internal distances, without requiring a common coordinate system. This invariance is powerful, but discrete GW is a nonconvex quadratic optimal transport problem and is difficult to estimate at scale. We propose \emph{Distance-Matrix Wasserstein} (DMW), a hierarchy of Wasserstein statistics comparing laws of random finite distance matrices. Rather than optimizing a global point-level alignment, DMW samples $n$ points from each space, records their pairwise distances, and transports the resulting matrix laws. We prove that DMW is a relaxation and lower bound of GW, and establish a reverse approximation inequality: the GW--DMW gap is controlled by the Wasserstein error of approximating each original measure with $n$ samples. Hence population DMW converges to GW as sampled subspaces become dense. We further give finite-sample bounds, including intrinsic-dimensional rates that depend on the data manifold rather than the ambient matrix dimension $\binom n2$. For scalable computation, we introduce sliced and multi-scale DMW; for $p=1$, the sliced multi-scale dissimilarity yields positive-definite exponential kernels. Experiments on synthetic metric spaces, scalability benchmarks, graph classification, and two-sample testing validate the theory and demonstrate an interpretable GW-style proxy for structural comparison.

AI.tldr: DMW is a scalable, sample-based approximation of Gromov-Wasserstein distances that compares random distance matrices instead of optimizing point-level alignments, with provable convergence guarantees and practical computational methods.
AI.motivation: Gromov-Wasserstein distances are powerful for comparing graphs and shapes without requiring common coordinates, but they're computationally expensive, nonconvex quadratic optimal transport problems that scale poorly with data size.
AI.method: Proposes Distance-Matrix Wasserstein (DMW): samples n points from each space, records pairwise distances as matrices, and transports the laws of these random distance matrices using Wasserstein statistics instead of optimizing point-to-point alignments.
AI.result: DMW is a provable relaxation and lower bound of GW with convergence guarantees; introduces scalable sliced and multi-scale variants; provides finite-sample bounds with intrinsic-dimensional rates; and validates through experiments on synthetic data, graph classification, and two-sample testing.
AI.conclusion: DMW offers a scalable, theoretically-grounded approximation to GW distances that maintains interpretable structural comparison capabilities while being computationally feasible for large-scale problems.

title: DeepTokenEEG Enhancing Mild Cognitive Impairment and Alzheimers Classification via Tokenized EEG Features
summary: The detection of Alzheimers disease (AD) is considered crucial, as timely intervention can improve patient outcomes. Electroencephalogram (EEG)-based diagnosis has been recognized as a non-invasive, accessible, and cost-effective approach for AD detection; however, it faces challenges related to data availability, accuracy of modern deep learning methods, and the time-consuming nature of expert-based interpretation. In this study, a novel lightweight and high-performance model, DeepTokenEEG, was designed for the diagnosis of AD and the classification of EEG signals from AD patients, individuals with other neurological conditions, and healthy subjects. Unlike traditional heavy-weight models, DeepTokenEEG ultilizes spatial and temporal tokenizer that effectively captures AD-related biomarkers in both temporal and frequency domain with only 0.29 million paramaters. Trained in a combined dataset of 274 subjects, including 180 AD cases, and 94 healthy controls, the proposed method achieves a maximum recorded accuracy of 100% on specific frequency bands, representing an improvement of 1.41-15.35% over state-of-the-art methods on the same dataset. These results indicate the potential of DeepTokenEEG for early detection and screening of AD, with promising applicability for deployment due to its compact size.

AI.tldr: DeepTokenEEG is a lightweight model that achieves 100% accuracy for Alzheimer's detection using EEG signals with only 0.29M parameters, outperforming existing methods.
AI.motivation: Alzheimer's disease detection is crucial for timely intervention. EEG-based diagnosis offers non-invasive, accessible, and cost-effective screening but faces challenges with data availability, accuracy of deep learning methods, and time-consuming expert interpretation.
AI.method: DeepTokenEEG uses a novel spatial and temporal tokenizer to capture AD-related biomarkers in both temporal and frequency domains. It's a lightweight model with only 0.29 million parameters, trained on a dataset of 274 subjects (180 AD cases and 94 healthy controls).
AI.result: Achieves maximum recorded accuracy of 100% on specific frequency bands, representing 1.41-15.35% improvement over state-of-the-art methods on the same dataset.
AI.conclusion: DeepTokenEEG shows strong potential for early detection and screening of Alzheimer's disease, with promising deployment applicability due to its compact size and high performance.

title: TopoPrimer: The Missing Topological Context in Forecasting Models
summary:
We introduce TopoPrimer, a framework that makes the global topological structure of the series population an explicit input
  to any forecasting model. TopoPrimer improves accuracy across diverse domains, stabilizes forecasts under seasonal demand
  spikes, and closes the cold-start gap. Precomputed once per domain via persistent homology and spectral sheaf coordinates,
  TopoPrimer deploys per token for fully-trained models and as a lightweight adapter for pre-trained backbones. Of these two
  components, sheaf coordinates are the primary accuracy driver. Across four public benchmarks on Chronos and TimesFM,
  TopoPrimer consistently improves forecasting accuracy, with gains of up to 7.3% MSE on ECL. The topology advantage persists
  with near-identical magnitude across zero-shot and fine-tuned backbones, suggesting topology and per-series training
  capture complementary signals. The gains are most pronounced in difficult regimes. Under peak seasonal demand, classical
  and zero-shot models degrade by up to 50%, while TopoPrimer stays within 10%. At cold start with no item history,
  TopoPrimer reduces MAE by 27% over a topology-free baseline.

AI.tldr: TopoPrimer is a framework that incorporates global topological structure of time series populations as an explicit input to forecasting models, improving accuracy, stabilizing predictions during seasonal spikes, and addressing cold-start problems.
AI.motivation: Traditional forecasting models don't explicitly incorporate the global topological structure of time series populations, which limits their accuracy, especially in challenging scenarios like seasonal demand spikes and cold-start situations with no historical data.
AI.method: TopoPrimer precomputes global topological features using persistent homology and spectral sheaf coordinates per domain. These features are then deployed as per-token inputs for fully-trained models or as lightweight adapters for pre-trained backbones.
AI.result: TopoPrimer improves forecasting accuracy by up to 7.3% MSE on ECL benchmark, maintains performance during seasonal peaks (within 10% degradation vs 50% for classical models), reduces cold-start MAE by 27%, and shows complementary benefits with per-series training.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating global topological structure as an explicit input to forecasting models significantly improves performance across diverse domains, especially in challenging regimes, with spectral sheaf coordinates being the primary accuracy driver. The topological advantage works well with both zero-shot and fine-tuned models.

title: Separating Intrinsic Ambiguity from Estimation Uncertainty in Deep Generative Models for Linear Inverse Problems
summary: Recently, deep generative models have been used for posterior inference in inverse problems, including high-stakes applications in medical imaging and scientific discovery, where the uncertainty of a prediction can matter as much as the prediction itself. However, posterior uncertainty is difficult to interpret because it can mix ambiguity inherent to the forward operator with uncertainty propagated through inference. We introduce a structural decomposition of posterior uncertainty that isolates intrinsic ambiguity. A cascade formulation makes this ambiguity accessible for calibration analysis, enabling qualitative diagnostics and simulation-based calibration tests that reveal failure modes that remain hidden when models are selected by reconstruction quality alone. We first validate the approach on a Gaussian example with analytical posterior structure, then illustrate the decomposition on accelerated magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), and finally apply the calibration diagnostics to electroencephalography (EEG) source imaging.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a method to decompose posterior uncertainty in inverse problems, separating intrinsic ambiguity from inference uncertainty, with applications to medical imaging diagnostics.
AI.motivation: Posterior uncertainty in inverse problems (like medical imaging) is difficult to interpret because it mixes inherent forward operator ambiguity with uncertainty from the inference process. Current approaches focus on reconstruction quality but hide important failure modes in uncertainty quantification.
AI.method: Introduces a structural decomposition of posterior uncertainty that isolates intrinsic ambiguity. Uses a cascade formulation to make this ambiguity accessible for calibration analysis, enabling qualitative diagnostics and simulation-based calibration tests.
AI.result: Validated on Gaussian example with analytical posterior structure, demonstrated on accelerated MRI, and applied to EEG source imaging. The calibration diagnostics reveal failure modes that remain hidden when models are selected by reconstruction quality alone.
AI.conclusion: The proposed structural decomposition provides interpretable uncertainty quantification for high-stakes applications, enabling better diagnostics and calibration analysis that goes beyond simple reconstruction quality metrics.

title: An Interpretable Latency Model for Speculative Decoding in LLM Serving
summary: Speculative decoding (SD) accelerates large language model (LLM) inference by using a smaller draft model to propose multiple tokens that are verified by a larger target model in parallel. While prior work demonstrates substantial speedups in isolated or fixed-batch settings, the behavior of SD in production serving systems remains poorly understood: request load varies over time, and effective batch size emerges from the serving system rather than being directly controlled or observed. In this work, we develop a simple and interpretable latency model for SD in LLM serving. We infer effective batch size from request rate using Little's Law and decompose per-request demand into load-independent and load-dependent components for prefill, drafting, and verification. We validate our model using extensive measurements from vLLM across verifier and drafter model sizes, prefill and decode lengths, request rates, draft lengths, and acceptance probabilities. The model accurately describes observed latency, explains why speedups often diminish as server load increases, and characterizes how draft length, acceptance rate, and verifier-drafter size shape latency across serving conditions, with implications for configuring SD in deployed systems. We further show how the framework extends to mixture of experts models, where sparse expert activation changes the effective service costs across load regimes. Together, our results provide a structured framework for understanding SD in real LLM serving systems.

AI.tldr: A latency model for speculative decoding in LLM serving systems that explains why speedups diminish under high load and provides insights for configuring SD in production deployments.
AI.motivation: While speculative decoding shows promising speedups in isolated settings, its behavior in production serving systems with varying request loads and emergent batch sizes is poorly understood, creating a need for better understanding of SD's performance in real-world deployments.
AI.method: Developed an interpretable latency model that infers effective batch size from request rate using Little's Law, decomposing per-request demand into load-independent and load-dependent components for prefill, drafting, and verification phases.
AI.result: The model accurately describes observed latency from vLLM experiments, explains why speedups diminish under high server load, and characterizes how draft length, acceptance rate, and model sizes shape latency across different serving conditions.
AI.conclusion: Provides a structured framework for understanding speculative decoding in real LLM serving systems, with implications for SD configuration in deployed systems, and shows extensibility to mixture of experts models.

title: DiffusionOPD: A Unified Perspective of On-Policy Distillation in Diffusion Models
summary: Reinforcement learning has emerged as a powerful tool for improving diffusion-based text-to-image models, but existing methods are largely limited to single-task optimization. Extending RL to multiple tasks is challenging: joint optimization suffers from cross-task interference and imbalance, while cascade RL is cumbersome and prone to catastrophic forgetting. We propose DiffusionOPD, a new multi-task training paradigm for diffusion models based on Online Policy Distillation (OPD). DiffusionOPD first trains task-specific teachers independently, then distills their capabilities into a unified student along the student own rollout trajectories. This decouples single-task exploration from multi-task integration and avoids the optimization burden of solving all tasks jointly from scratch. Theoretically, we lift the OPD framework from discrete tokens to continuous-state Markov processes, deriving a closed-form per-step KL objective that unifies both stochastic SDE and deterministic ODE refinement via mean-matching. We formally and empirically demonstrate that this analytic gradient provides lower variance and better generality compared to conventional PPO-style policy gradients. Extensive experiments show that DiffusionOPD consistently surpasses both multi-reward RL and cascade RL baselines in training efficiency and final performance, while achieving state-of-the-art results on all evaluated benchmarks.

AI.tldr: DiffusionOPD is a multi-task training paradigm for diffusion models that uses online policy distillation to avoid cross-task interference, achieving state-of-the-art results across benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing reinforcement learning methods for diffusion models are limited to single-task optimization, while multi-task approaches suffer from cross-task interference, imbalance issues, or cumbersome cascade RL prone to catastrophic forgetting.
AI.method: DiffusionOPD trains task-specific teachers independently, then distills their capabilities into a unified student along the student's own rollout trajectories, decoupling single-task exploration from multi-task integration. It lifts OPD framework to continuous-state Markov processes with a closed-form per-step KL objective.
AI.result: DiffusionOPD consistently surpasses both multi-reward RL and cascade RL baselines in training efficiency and final performance, achieving state-of-the-art results on all evaluated benchmarks with lower variance and better generality than PPO-style gradients.
AI.conclusion: DiffusionOPD provides an effective multi-task training paradigm that overcomes cross-task interference issues, offering superior performance and efficiency for improving diffusion-based text-to-image models across multiple tasks.

title: Croissant Baker: Metadata Generation for Discoverable, Governable, and Reusable ML Datasets
comment: 23 pages, 5 figures, 11 tables. Project: https://lcp.mit.edu/croissant-baker/ Code: https://github.com/MIT-LCP/croissant-baker
summary: Croissant has emerged as the metadata standard for machine learning datasets, providing a structured, JSON-LD-based format that makes dataset discovery, automated ingestion, and reproducible analysis machine-checkable across ML platforms. Adoption has accelerated, and NeurIPS now requires Croissant metadata in every submission to its dataset tracks. Yet in practice Croissant generation usually starts with uploading data to a public platform, a path infeasible for governed and large local repositories that hold much of the high-value data ML increasingly relies on. We release Croissant Baker, a local-first, open-source command-line tool that generates validated Croissant metadata directly from a dataset directory through a modular handler registry. We evaluate Croissant Baker on over 140 datasets, scaling to MIMIC-IV at 886 million rows and 374 Parquet files. On held-out comparisons against producer-authored or standards-derived ground truth, Croissant Baker reaches 97-100% agreement across multiple domains.

AI.tldr: Croissant Baker is a local-first CLI tool that generates validated Croissant metadata directly from dataset directories, enabling ML datasets from governed/local repositories to be machine-readable without needing to upload to public platforms first.
AI.motivation: While Croissant has become the standard metadata format for ML datasets (required by NeurIPS), current generation workflows require uploading data to public platforms first, which is infeasible for governed/large local repositories that contain high-value data used by ML systems.
AI.method: Developed Croissant Baker - an open-source command-line tool with modular handler registry that generates validated Croissant metadata directly from dataset directories. The tool uses a local-first approach and was evaluated on over 140 datasets including large-scale datasets like MIMIC-IV (886M rows, 374 Parquet files).
AI.result: Achieved 97-100% agreement with producer-authored or standards-derived ground truth across multiple domains. Successfully scaled to handle extremely large datasets like MIMIC-IV. The tool enables generation of validated Croissant metadata without requiring data upload to public platforms.
AI.conclusion: Croissant Baker addresses the practical limitation of existing Croissant generation workflows by enabling local-first metadata creation, making it feasible for governed and large local repositories to adopt the Croissant standard, thereby expanding machine-readable dataset accessibility across the ML ecosystem.

title: Proposal and study of statistical features for string similarity computation and classification
summary: Adaptations of features commonly applied in the field of visual computing, co-occurrence matrix (COM) and run-length matrix (RLM), are proposed for the similarity computation of strings in general (words, phrases, codes and texts). The proposed features are not sensitive to language related information. These are purely statistical and can be used in any context with any language or grammatical structure. Other statistical measures that are commonly employed in the field such as longest common subsequence, maximal consecutive longest common subsequence, mutual information and edit distances are evaluated and compared. In the first synthetic set of experiments, the COM and RLM features outperform the remaining state-of-the-art statistical features. In 3 out of 4 cases, the RLM and COM features were statistically more significant than the second best group based on distances (P-value < 0.001). When it comes to a real text plagiarism dataset, the RLM features obtained the best results.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes adaptations of visual computing features (co-occurrence matrix and run-length matrix) for string similarity computation, showing they outperform existing statistical measures in synthetic experiments and real text plagiarism detection.
AI.motivation: To develop language-agnostic statistical features for string similarity computation that can work across any language or grammatical structure, addressing limitations of language-dependent approaches.
AI.method: Adapt visual computing features (co-occurrence matrix and run-length matrix) for string similarity, and compare them against existing statistical measures like longest common subsequence, maximal consecutive longest common subsequence, mutual information, and edit distances.
AI.result: In synthetic experiments, COM and RLM features outperformed other statistical features. In 3 out of 4 cases, they were statistically more significant than the second-best group (P-value < 0.001). In real text plagiarism detection, RLM features achieved the best results.
AI.conclusion: Adapted COM and RLM features from visual computing provide effective, language-agnostic statistical measures for string similarity computation that outperform existing statistical approaches, with RLM features particularly effective for text plagiarism detection.

title: Learning from Language Feedback via Variational Policy Distillation
summary: Reinforcement learning from verifiable rewards (RLVR) suffers from sparse outcome signals, creating severe exploration bottlenecks on complex reasoning tasks. Recent on-policy self-distillation methods attempt to address this by utilizing language feedback to generate dense, token-level supervision. However, these approaches rely on a fixed, passive teacher to interpret the feedback. As the student policy improves, the teacher's zero-shot assessment capabilities plateau, ultimately halting further learning. To overcome this, we propose Variational Policy Distillation (VPD), a framework that formalizes learning from language feedback as a Variational Expectation-Maximization (EM) problem. VPD co-evolves both policies: in the E-step, the teacher is actively refined on trajectory outcomes via an adaptive trust-region update, translating textual feedback into a dynamically improved target token distribution. In the M-step, the student internalizes this dense distributional guidance on its own on-policy rollouts. By continuously improving the teacher's ability to extract actionable signals from textual critique, VPD overcomes the limitations of passive distillation. Evaluated across diverse sources of diagnostic feedback on scientific reasoning and code generation tasks, VPD consistently outperforms both standard RLVR and existing self-distillation baselines. Finally, by stress-testing our framework on rigid mathematical reasoning and cold-start regimes, we illuminate the fundamental bounds of feedback-driven self-distillation compared to pure environment-driven RL.

AI.tldr: VPD (Variational Policy Distillation) is a new framework that actively co-evolves both teacher and student policies through Variational EM to overcome limitations of passive distillation in reinforcement learning from verifiable rewards.
AI.motivation: Current RLVR methods suffer from sparse reward signals causing exploration bottlenecks. Existing self-distillation methods use fixed passive teachers whose zero-shot assessment capabilities plateau as the student improves, limiting further learning.
AI.method: VPD formalizes learning from language feedback as Variational Expectation-Maximization problem. E-step: teacher actively refined on trajectory outcomes via adaptive trust-region updates to create improved target token distributions. M-step: student internalizes this dense distributional guidance on on-policy rollouts.
AI.result: VPD consistently outperforms both standard RLVR and existing self-distillation baselines across diverse diagnostic feedback on scientific reasoning and code generation tasks. Stress-testing on rigid mathematical reasoning and cold-start regimes reveals fundamental bounds of feedback-driven distillation vs pure environment-driven RL.
AI.conclusion: By continuously improving the teacher's ability to extract actionable signals from textual critique, VPD overcomes limitations of passive distillation, enabling more effective learning from language feedback in complex reasoning tasks.

title: Natural Synthesis: Outperforming Reactive Synthesis Tools with Large Reasoning Models
summary: Reactive synthesis, the problem of automatically constructing a hardware circuit from a logical specification, is a long-standing challenge in formal verification. It is elusive for two reasons: It is algorithmically hard, and writing formal specifications by hand is notoriously difficult. In this paper, we tackle both sides of the problem. For the algorithmic side, we present a neuro-symbolic approach to reactive synthesis that couples large reasoning models with model checkers to iteratively repair a synthesized Verilog implementation via sound symbolic feedback. Our approach solves more benchmarks than the best dedicated tools in the annual synthesis competition and extends to constructing parameterized systems, a problem known to be undecidable. On the specification side, we introduce an autoformalization step that shifts the specification task from temporal logic to natural language by introducing a hand-authored dataset of natural-language specifications for evaluation. We demonstrate performance comparable to that of starting from formal specifications, establishing natural synthesis as a viable end-to-end workflow.

AI.tldr: Neuro-symbolic approach to reactive synthesis combines reasoning models with model checkers to iteratively repair synthesized hardware circuits, plus natural language autoformalization to simplify specification writing.
AI.motivation: Reactive synthesis is challenging due to algorithmic hardness and difficulty of writing formal specifications by hand. The paper addresses both problems: algorithmic complexity and specification burden.
AI.method: 1) Neuro-symbolic approach coupling large reasoning models with model checkers to iteratively repair synthesized Verilog implementations via symbolic feedback. 2) Autoformalization step shifting specification from temporal logic to natural language, using hand-authored natural-language specification dataset.
AI.result: The approach solves more benchmarks than best dedicated tools in annual synthesis competition and extends to parameterized systems (undecidable problem). Natural language specifications achieve comparable performance to formal specifications, establishing natural synthesis as viable end-to-end workflow.
AI.conclusion: The neuro-symbolic approach effectively tackles reactive synthesis challenges by combining AI reasoning with formal methods, while autoformalization makes specification more accessible, enabling practical end-to-end natural synthesis workflows.

title: Causal Foundation Models with Continuous Treatments
summary: Causal inference, estimating causal effects from observational data, is a fundamental tool in many disciplines. Of particular importance across a variety of domains is the continuous treatment setting, where the variable of intervention has a continuous range. This setting is far less explored and represents a substantial shift from the binary treatment setting, with models needing to represent effects across a continuum of treatment values. In this paper, we present the first causal foundation model for the continuous treatment setting. Our model meta-learns the ability to predict causal effects across a wide variety of unseen tasks without additional training or fine-tuning. First, we design a novel prior over data-generating processes with continuous treatment variables in order to generate a rich causal training corpus. We then train a transformer to reconstruct individual treatment-response curves given only observational data, leveraging in-context learning to amortize expensive Bayesian posterior inference. Our model achieves state-of-the-art performance on individual treatment-response curve reconstruction tasks compared to causal models which are trained specifically for those tasks.

AI.tldr: First causal foundation model for continuous treatment settings that meta-learns to predict causal effects across unseen tasks without additional training, outperforming task-specific models.
AI.motivation: Causal inference with continuous treatments is fundamental but underexplored compared to binary treatments, requiring models that can represent effects across treatment continua. Existing approaches lack generalizable foundation models for this setting.
AI.method: 1) Designs novel prior over data-generating processes with continuous treatments to generate rich causal training corpus; 2) Trains transformer to reconstruct individual treatment-response curves using only observational data; 3) Leverages in-context learning to amortize Bayesian posterior inference.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance on individual treatment-response curve reconstruction tasks compared to causal models specifically trained for those tasks.
AI.conclusion: Successfully develops the first causal foundation model for continuous treatment settings that can meta-learn to predict causal effects across diverse unseen tasks without task-specific training or fine-tuning.

title: Training ML Models with Predictable Failures
summary: Estimating how often an ML model will fail at deployment scale is central to pre-deployment safety assessment, but a feasible evaluation set is rarely large enough to observe the failures that matter. Jones et al. (2025) address this by extrapolating from the largest k failure scores in an evaluation set to predict deployment-scale failure rates. We give a finite-k decomposition of this estimator's forecast error and show that it has a built-in bias toward over-prediction in the typical case, which is the safety-favorable direction. This bias is offset when the evaluation set misses a rare high-failure mode that the deployment set contains, leaving the forecast to under-predict at deployment scale. We propose a fine-tuning objective, the forecastability loss, that addresses this failure mode. In two proof-of-concept experiments, a language-model password game and an RL gridworld, fine-tuning substantially reduces held-out forecast error while preserving primary-task capability and achieving safety similar to that of supervised baselines.

AI.tldr: A method to estimate ML model failure rates at deployment scale by extrapolating from evaluation set failure scores, with a proposed fine-tuning objective to address rare failure modes.
AI.motivation: Estimating ML model failure rates at deployment scale is crucial for safety assessment, but evaluation sets are often too small to observe rare but important failures, necessitating extrapolation methods.
AI.method: Extrapolate from largest k failure scores in evaluation set to predict deployment-scale failure rates, analyze forecast error decomposition, and propose a "forecastability loss" fine-tuning objective to address rare high-failure modes.
AI.result: The estimator has built-in bias toward over-prediction (safety-favorable) but can under-predict when evaluation set misses rare high-failure modes; fine-tuning with forecastability loss reduces held-out forecast error while preserving primary-task capability and safety.
AI.conclusion: The forecastability loss fine-tuning approach effectively addresses deployment-scale failure rate estimation challenges by reducing forecast errors while maintaining model performance and safety, demonstrated in two proof-of-concept experiments.

title: Forgetting That Sticks: Quantization-Permanent Unlearning via Circuit Attribution
summary: Standard unlearning evaluations measure behavioral suppression in full precision, immediately after training, despite every deployed language model being quantized first. Recent work has shown that 4-bit post-training quantization can reverse machine unlearning; we show this is not a tuning artefact but a systematic dual failure: gradient-based methods that achieve meaningful forgetting lose it under compression, while methods that survive quantization barely change the model. Both failures trace to the same root cause: across all baselines, per-parameter updates lie 47-828x below the NF4 quantization bin width; updates diffused across billions of parameters cannot clear quantization bin boundaries, a consequence we formalize as a sparsity-permanence tradeoff. We present MANSU (Mechanistic-Aligned Null-Space Unlearning), which resolves both modes by combining causal circuit attribution to isolate the minimal forget-set subgraph, circuit-restricted null-space projection with a diagonal-Fisher retain bound, and a per-parameter magnitude floor guaranteeing quantization survival by construction. We additionally introduce Circuit Attribution Divergence (CAD), a mechanistic verification metric distinguishing structural erasure from behavioral suppression, a distinction existing metrics cannot make. Across multiple model families and hazard benchmarks, MANSU is the first method to jointly satisfy all four properties with margin on each (meaningful forgetting, retain preservation, non-positive PTQ gap, and structural erasure), while gradient-based baselines recover up to +0.05 accuracy under compression.

AI.tldr: A new unlearning method called MANSU maintains effectiveness after model quantization by combining causal circuit attribution with null-space projection and magnitude floors, addressing the dual failure where gradient-based methods lose forgetting under compression while other methods barely change the model.
AI.motivation: Current unlearning evaluations fail to account for model quantization in deployment. Recent work shows 4-bit post-training quantization can reverse machine unlearning, but the authors argue this isn't just a tuning artifact but a systematic dual failure: methods that achieve meaningful forgetting lose it under compression, while methods that survive quantization barely change the model.
AI.method: MANSU combines three key components: 1) causal circuit attribution to isolate the minimal forget-set subgraph, 2) circuit-restricted null-space projection with diagonal-Fisher retain bound, and 3) a per-parameter magnitude floor guaranteeing quantization survival by construction. The method also introduces Circuit Attribution Divergence (CAD) as a mechanistic verification metric.
AI.result: Across multiple model families and hazard benchmarks, MANSU is the first method to jointly satisfy all four properties: meaningful forgetting, retain preservation, non-positive PTQ gap, and structural erasure. Gradient-based baselines recover up to +0.05 accuracy under compression, showing their forgetting is reversed.
AI.conclusion: The paper identifies a fundamental sparsity-permanence tradeoff in unlearning and presents MANSU as a solution that achieves both meaningful forgetting and quantization survival by design, providing both behavioral and structural guarantees that existing methods cannot achieve simultaneously.

title: When Are Two Networks the Same? Tensor Similarity for Mechanistic Interpretability
summary: Mechanistic interpretability aims to break models into meaningful parts; verifying that two such parts implement the same computation is a prerequisite. Existing similarity measures evaluate either empirical behaviour, leaving them blind to out-of-distribution mechanisms, or basis-dependent parameters, meaning they disregard weight-space symmetries. To address these issues for the class of tensor-based models, we introduce a weight-based metric, tensor similarity, that is invariant to such symmetries. This metric captures global functional equivalence and accounts for cross-layer mechanisms using an efficient recursive algorithm. Empirically, tensor similarity tracks functional training dynamics, such as grokking and backdoor insertion, with higher fidelity than existing metrics. This reduces measuring similarity and verifying faithfulness into a solved algebraic problem rather than one of empirical approximation.

AI.tldr: Introduces "tensor similarity" - a weight-based metric for comparing neural network components that is invariant to weight-space symmetries and handles out-of-distribution mechanisms better than existing methods.
AI.motivation: Existing similarity measures for mechanistic interpretability have two main limitations: (1) empirical behavior metrics are blind to out-of-distribution mechanisms, and (2) basis-dependent parameter metrics disregard weight-space symmetries. There's a need for a better way to verify when two model parts implement the same computation.
AI.method: Proposes "tensor similarity" - a weight-based metric specifically designed for tensor-based models that is invariant to weight-space symmetries. Uses an efficient recursive algorithm that accounts for cross-layer mechanisms and captures global functional equivalence.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation shows tensor similarity tracks functional training dynamics (like grokking and backdoor insertion) with higher fidelity than existing metrics. The approach reduces similarity measurement and faithfulness verification from an empirical approximation problem to a solved algebraic problem.
AI.conclusion: Tensor similarity provides a principled, symmetry-invariant way to compare neural network components that works better than existing methods, making similarity verification a formal algebraic problem rather than an empirical approximation challenge.

title: Ready from Day 1: Population-Aware Coordination for Large-Scale Constrained Multi-Agent Systems
summary: In large-scale multi-agent systems with shared resource constraints, an upstream planner must iteratively evaluate candidate resource plans -- assessing feasibility, aggregate response, and marginal cost -- before committing to one. Lagrangian relaxation separates local decisions through a broadcast cost signal, but the planner still needs the cost-to-utilization response map to explore plan space, and this map depends on population composition that changes across planning cycles. We propose \emph{population-aware coordination interfaces}: learned primal and dual maps, conditioned on compact population summaries, that the planner queries inside its iterative loop. The primal map predicts aggregate utilization under a proposed cost trajectory; the dual map predicts the cost trajectory for a target plan. By encoding response-relevant population structure, these maps remain reliable across evolving populations without per-cycle retraining, and support coordination of large populations from compact subsamples. We additionally cast Sim2Real transfer as a backtestable procedure, enabling evaluation before deployment. In a supply-chain capacity-control case study, population-aware interfaces reduce forecast error by 16--19\% and capacity violations by 20--51\% relative to population-unaware baselines under composition shift; 20K-agent cohorts support accurate coordination of 500K-agent populations; and simulator-trained primal maps achieve 11.1\% MAPE on real observations versus 13--24\% for baselines.

AI.tldr: Learned population-aware coordination interfaces enable large-scale multi-agent resource planning by predicting agent responses conditioned on population composition, maintaining reliability across changing populations without retraining.
AI.motivation: In large multi-agent systems with shared resources, planners need to evaluate resource plans but face challenges with changing population compositions across planning cycles, requiring costly repeated computations of agent response maps.
AI.method: Propose population-aware coordination interfaces: learned primal and dual maps conditioned on compact population summaries. Primal map predicts aggregate utilization given cost trajectories; dual map predicts cost trajectories for target plans. These encode response-relevant population structure for reliability across evolving populations.
AI.result: In supply-chain capacity-control case study: reduced forecast error by 16-19%, capacity violations by 20-51% vs population-unaware baselines; 20K-agent cohorts accurately coordinate 500K-agent populations; simulator-trained primal maps achieved 11.1% MAPE on real observations vs 13-24% for baselines.
AI.conclusion: Population-aware coordination interfaces provide effective, transferable solutions for large-scale multi-agent resource planning under changing population compositions, enabling efficient coordination without per-cycle retraining and supporting Sim2Real transfer.
